content: The Dahshur pyramids are a living proof of the Egyptians first attempts to build smooth-sided pyramids before they could build the Great Pyramid of Giza. During your visit to Dahshur you will get to see the Bent Pyramid and the Red Pyramid, both constructed during the reign of Sneferu (2613-2589 BC).The Bent Pyramid was an unsuccessful first attempt at a smooth-sided pyramid. The engineering apparently failed and this is why the pyramid is "bent" and changes angles about halfway up the sides. History says that Sneferu was not pleased with this pyramid so he built another called the Red Pyramid. This was the first true smooth-sided pyramid in the world. You must see it! The Red Pyramid at Dahshur is only for you, no crowds, no sellers. Climbing up and into the Red Pyramid is free of both charge and harass. I had it completely to myself for about two hours. (April 2014). Uphill the pyramid is safe and not physically demanding.  At the top you will find a “guard” who escorts you if you decide to go inside (for free). He was pleasantly sleeping when I got there. Tight corridors lead down into the bowels of the pyramid. It is approx. 65-70m to the bottom. I would strongly recommend you to go down thought it can get hot and stuffy. The way up may be a bit hard for your legs but I’m sure that everyone can do it.During my entire time at Dahshur I didn’t see any other tourists around. It is PEACEFUL, enjoyable and free of charge. Bring some snacks and water, as there is nothing for sale there. You will pay a small fee to enter the whole site.IMPORTANT: If you don’t have a car you may want to hire a taxi for the day (Giza-Saqqara-Dahshur). But be careful, some taxi drivers would ruin your day if you let them push you. My driver Hosni was extremely annoying, complaining all day long about his economic situation and taking me to places is which he gets a commission. He got really mad at me for refusing the LE800 tour in Giza(I walked on my own). He also complained to me because I didn’t buy anything at the Papyrus shop (I had already bought my original papyrus at the very well-known Said’s place in Islamic Cairo for half of the price). First he treated me as a criminal for asking him to take a picture of me at the bend pyramid… but then he said “Ok my dear brother how much you pay me for this, it is hot outside the car!”. I heard similar stories from other visitors; some drivers may take you to the places they want to, hiding from your some of the very important attractions. Be careful when you get yours, my hotel recommended Hosni!

I really was impressed with this place but as part of a tour we couldn't stay long I suggest that you arrange the tour so you have time to walk around properly. Again another market close by as soon as you land they come over. Walk down steps to site not sure if any wheelchair access.

I have to admit that I was somewhat underwhelmed when I first arrived at the pyramids.  I guess I was surprised at just how close the the urban sprawl they actually are.  They are still an amazing site to see and you couldn’t visit Egypt and not visit the great pyramids at Giza.  As a professional photographer, I was there to capture images of the pyramids and it is best to make two visits, one in the morning and one in the late afternoon to get the best images.  Most of the day will be extremely crowded.  There are others pyramids further out of town that are far less crowded and just as spectacular.
Although not the largest tomb in the VOK- it's still interesting and absolutely worth a visit.  To walk down and see the tomb of King Tut in person will make the hairs stand up on your arms.
Temple is amazing , you have to take a boat to reach the place ,, very nice scenery lovely people ,, enjoy

There are so many items to display, the museum has them piled and stacked in certain areas of the museum. This is the perfect end to a guided trip to Egypt. We learn so much along the way and then get to see artifacts from every place we visited while in the museum.
Wonderful temple in a fabulous setting.  One of the unmissable sights of Egypt.

I really enjoyed this place, and would definitelly recommend this site. Howeverm I wouldn't spend more than 1 hour there. The building is amazing, and the sight of the Nile opposite to it is very beautiful. Some nicely preserved inscriptions with original colors as well.

I didn’t know what to expect when we went to Karnak as part of a tourist tour fra Marsa Alam to Luxor. I was very amazed at the complex that Karnak turned out to be. The huge pillars, obelisks and sfinks were much more and more numerous than I had thought. Also they are well preserved and the carvings and the colours are still bright (some places) and sharp.Take your time to walk around and be amazed!

We came here with a guide.After over an hour of him telling us the history of artifacts a few of us took off on our own.There is so much in here too see I don't know if I would recommend a guide.I highly recommend the gold room (it is moving to the new museum not sure when since it was on fire while were there). You weren't allowed to take pictures in this area.I do recommend paying extra to go in the mummy room. These mommy's were so well preserved its hard to believe. If I remember correctly it was about 50 Egyptian lbs.

The columns were my favorite part of the temple. A beautiful, must see site. I recommend you go here with a certified guide to get the full understanding and best experience. I also recommend you visit during the cooler months in Egypt (October-April)

Hapshetsut's mortuary temple is unlike any other in the Nile Valley at Luxor. It's line of columns was to inspire the greeks many centuries later. It's set right into the rock cliff with the inner temple rooms carved out of the rock on the outer edge of the Valley of the Kings. The imposing cliffs soar right up against the rear of the temple and have in the past fallen and ruined the adjacent temple of her husband.You can get a trolley car to take you to the base of the temple but its nicer to walk the km or so and really get a sense of the size and grandeur of the temple as you approach.As a "queen" king she ruled with her husband/brother and then in her own right as regent to her nephew, then as co-ruler with him for a further 14 years. She conducted a large trade mission south and this is recorded on the walls of her temple, as is her "divine" birth which she used to justify her title as pharoah.One of the shrine areas at the very rear of the temple has only just recently opened so you can now see areas never before shown in public, although only through the doorway/gate. Hopefully you might be able to get one of the guards to take a photo for you (for a small fee!)
The more pyramids we saw, the more we became mesmerised by their beauty and grandeur.  We are still amazed how they were built and there are still no answers.
Visited here in October 2014. it was very hot so wear a hat, take water and wear comfortable shoes as there is a bit of walking involved. The temple is amazing and really impressive. Breathtaking.

If you want a magical experience, stay at the Pyramids Overlook Inn - the closest you will get to the sphinx and the view from the roof terrace is breathtaking from sunrise to sunset - see the Sound & Light show in comfort!
Apart from all the Ancient Egyptian collection of antiques, a must-see for King Tut's collection and his special Jewelry room.
All my life I have heard about THE Sphinx, but to stand and look down this avenue is truly impressive.

The statue of Ramses II is located in the museum of Memphis, it is striking for its gigantic dimensions and for its excellent state of conservation, the statue is displayed in a horizontal position, "lying down", it is possible to admire it and walk around it in all directions, and also see it from above.

I thought the best of Abu Simbel is the size of the statue but I was wrong. The ancient interior carvings are extremely beautiful, clear & magnificent! The long journey definitely worth it. Maybe staying a night at Abu Simbel would allow to witness the night light show.

The Karnak Temple at one time was connected to the Luxor Temple through the Shinx Avenue. The avenue at this temple is with statues of a Ram on both sides. It has 134 column with the symbol of papyrus flower. It was constructed by Seti I and completed by Rameses II. There is a lake at the far end. A sound and light show is held here in the evening. I have not seen it but it was highly recommended by the guide.

When reach the tomb you will realize why its discovery was great incident in 1922. The paintings on the wall is so artistic and you will not think it has been done 1300 BC.The drama and the stories are fascinating when you have the right guide. In short this is cradle of the world history.Every minute is enjoyable, best visit in the winter

What an incredible place.My recommendation would be to get there early before it gets too busy and too hot! We used a guide and a driver to show us around the Pyramids and Sphinx, telling us all about the history of who built them, how and why. Plenty of amazing photo spots around the site of you have a guide and driver too. We used Deluxe Tours Egypt and couldn’t recommend them highly enough.
Huge statues. It makes you wonder how the rest of the temple looked. Too bad it all disapered. Looks great

We visited the Museum as part of an all inclusive tour of Cairo.  This was something that I have always wanted to see and it was quite something to see that early life had many of the luxuries we have today.  The Tutankhamen display was truly something - buy the postcards or a book as the glass cabinets don't always allow one to take a clear photo as reflections from the skylights show in the glass.  Not sure why they don't have non-reflective glass but still quite something to see.  We were fortunate to have a knowledgable "Egyptologist" with us who gave us a very informative and well informed visit.There were parts that I would have liked to have visited but due to being part of a group it was not possible.  Another tick on the bucket list.
very very very good and should be visited and u can have the best feeling i have felt when making a diving and see the beautiful fish near from u.

This confirmed everything I have begun to understand about the roots of islamic art and architecture. As you admire this pre-Islamic Christian place of worship note how closely the art and architecture resembles many modern mosques all over the globe. Especially those to be found in Southern Spain and North Africa.

Superb Amazing and wonderful for sight seeing, very huge sized pyramids total in all are 9 in nos. Such an iconic place to visit who love archaeological and history. Recommend the guests to visit this place early morning as its to hot and humid climate in afternoon time. Although i did not get chance to visit inside as i had been during RAMADAN season. You can be able to walk till the entrance and climbing is NOT PERMITTED. well there may be some guides available or local people they may charge you exponentially high.Please take plenty of water and also cover your head from getting sun stroke. For travelling entire u may either take permission for your own car or else u can take horses, camels or carts for extra money.
We took a VIP boat trip out for snorkelling and it was by far the best day of our holiday. The snorkelling was amazing!
This was my favorite snorkeling place It is a stunning marine park with excellent hard/soft corals i love it
This is as nice as any of the temples of Karnak (just not as sprawling), but without the crowds. I believe my friend and I were the only people in this temple. Make it part of a West Bank day.
Massive. Love hearing how this was discovered and engineering solution to move it, learning about cartouches that identify Ramses II.

Bucket list ticked ... A couple of tips people selling ... say straight up No thanks and keep walking ... Do the small pyramid entry better value and same thing as big but shorter walk ... We used a tour guide from Egypt tailor made ... Awesome guide fully experienced and driver is always close by ... Saved immense amounts of time ... Happy to answer any questions or give tips

A must!There has been a lot written about this well preserved ruins and the technological effort of the disassembling and the reassembling   that I won‘t write about that.I my opinion in order to fully admire this place you need to beat the crowds. Most people visit Abu Simbel in an organized tour from Assuan or a cruise. So when you arrive, everybody arrives because they all leave Assuan at the same time. So it will be very crowded and noisy.My advice is to overnight in Abu Simbel and visit in the very early morning.
Everything is great, we liked everything, keep it up guys, we'll be back soon, the breakfast is amazing,)) spacious rooms

all that I can say is he looks real good for his age. The walls in the tomb are so well preserved. They try and keep you moving so they can get every one through, but if you smile nice to the guards it helps to linger a little longer

These burial grounds are much more extensive underground than one would imagine. Go down right to the bottom of the stairs and you can wander around for quite a while. However, note that there are no remains still in the graves, just open spaces where the bodies/ashes used to be.

This was our first stop as part of a day trip from Cairo to Alexandria.  Along with Pompei's Pillar, it was also our favorite.  We loved the spiral staircase entrance to the tomb and how people would station themselves at the openings so as to not let the body hit the sides of the walls.  Because the water levels had changed, the bottom of the catacombs are flooded which meant a very interesting trip on shaky boards resting just above the waterline.  We felt like we were part of a Tomb Raiders movie!

As an Egyptian,the pyramids are our source of proud,you will never see something like that again,I'm lucky to see them daily,tip for the tourists,try to visit in a group with Egyptian guide to avoid surrounding annoying merchants.little bit exhausty but I promise you will never forget

My friend and I picked up from Aqua Blu Hotel greeted by the drivers in an air condition mini bus..we arrived at park 20 minutes later..Muhammed was our English speaking guide and he was lovely. Showed us around the park and explained each exhibit which related to Egypts most finest monuments ..such as the pyramids,mosques,sphinx,government building,palaces etc all replicated in a small scale..Muhammed got us posing for photographs (see pics )..No Restaurant open..no camel rides or boating which was advertised in the price..We did purchase Pepsi or sprite for 2 euros each.Our guide Mohammed made the tour enjoyable and stayed with us all the time..the park tour lasted an hour .

I guess it depends on what floats your boat, but for me this was one of the best excursions I have ever done. My husband and I are fairly active, but after a 2.5 hr drive at silly o'clock I was feeling a bit daunted at the thought of how much activity was still to come, however, from the minute we started the climb time flew.  Our guide was excellent and humourous, I would have said you need to be reasonably fit to do this trip but so many very young and quite elderly managed it to the top, I think I have that very wrong.  The only advice I will offer is that we did the climb in December, and by the time we got to the top it really was quite cold,(not really sure how this compares with other times of the year), but if you find you have not got the amount of clothing quite right, you can hire blankets from the last stop point near the top of the mountain, this only costs 20 egyptian pounds (about £2), when you reach the top you will be hanging around for about an hour to watch the sunrise, so the blanket for us was a lifesaver.  Everything about this trip was amazing, if you get the chance do not miss it

Obviously we all expect the outside to be magnificent but like me you may not have expected the inside to be so impressive too. There are some fabulous paintings there but no photography allowed. Our guide sold a DVD for £10 with photos of all the places where we could no longer take photos.
Great place to dive, wonderful reefs and drift dives.Tremendous places and sites, highly recommended

We flew from Aswan to Abu Simbel . A wake up call at 4.30am and disembark our boat cruise at 5am was worth it. Abu Simbel has 2 gorgeous  temples that were built by Ramesses II to honor himself and a smaller temple to honor his queen.  They were carved out of the cliff face.  When the Aswan high dam was built it was recognized that these temples would be submerged.  An international effort was started that cut up the temples and reassembled them 650 ft higher and 200 ft back from where they had been. The carvings inside are still  quite intake and just fantastic. You should not miss this if you are in this part of the world. We had 2hrs at the temple before we caught our return flight to Aswan.  Even from the plane we got a short glimpse of the temples. At the same time the view of the fine sand of Sahara desert and the clear blue water of the  Nile flowing was breath taking. Memories of a lifetime.
I visited it twice once in 2004 and another in 2008It is amazing place where you can see the old Egypt history and a lot of statues are there
You can visit castle comfortable bu caring to kids .Castle is telling Turkish and Egypt history and  army of ancient times with mediterrenian view...

The way from Aswan to the temples of Abu Simbel is long. It is only about 50 kilometers from the Sudanese border but in Ancient Egypt, Abu Simbel was in Nubia.The famous pharaoh Ramesses II built two temples here. The first one, the largest one, was dedicated to the gods Re, Ptah Amun and himself in deified form and the smaller temple was dedicated to the goddess Hathor and "given" to his favorite wife, Nefertari.When you arrive in Abu Simbel, you go through a visitor's center, where you can watch a movie about UNESCO's moving of the temples in the 1960'es and read various info about the temples and the moving. It is also here you buy your tickets.If you watch the movie, you will know that the temple, as you see it today, is built into a fake mountain, the real mountain now submerged by the Lake Nasser.You also enter from the back. In ancient times, people were supposed to come sailing and become awestruck upon seeing the enormous seated statues of Ramesses flanking the entrance to the temple.But chances are that you will still become awestruck when you walk around the mountain and stand face to face with Ramesses. The colossi outside are about 20 meters high and between the legs stands queens and royal children. When you enter through the doorway you come into a hall with pillars shaped like Osiris. In this hall you can see depictions of Ramesses' famous battle against the hittitties in the Syrian town of Kadesh. Further into the temple you reach the shrine with the four seated statues of Ptah, Amun, Re-Horakhte and Ramesses. Around the shrine you find several smaller chambers with marvellous reliefs.Well outside you can visit the smaller temple further to your right. The entrance is flanked by six standing statues: four is of Ramesses and two are showing Nefertiti. Inside the temple there are wonderful reliefs with the gods and goddesses of Egypt, the king and the queen.You can go to Abu Simbel either by bus (circa 3 hours) or by plane, which of course is much faster. But generally you are not allowed as much time at the site when you travel by plane as you do when travelling by bus. At the moment you must pre-book your trip to Abu Simbel with a certified agent, preferably in Aswan or with your agency at home. You cannot drive your own car or go with a taxi to Abu Simbel. You will travel in a convoy with other groups.

Before visiting the Giza Pyramids, I had a mental image of pyramids in the middle of the desert - I was surprised to find out that the city of Cairo butts right up to the pyramids complex! However, when I arrived at Saqqara, I felt like all my expectations for what the Giza pyramids would look like were fulfilled by the Step Pyramid, Red Pyramid, and Bent Pyramids of Saqqara & Dahshur. They are set apart from the city and surrounded by desert. I loved the landscape and felt like I was transported back in time as we walked around, marveling at the pyramids with very few or no other tourists nearby. Cool experience!

The day proved to be a very long day but fantastic seeing the more popular sites in Luxor in one day.The site visit was planned well by our tour guide to miss the main coach tour timings giving us a very quiet time to really take in this amazing temple for a Pharaoh who knew what she wanted and got it despite the taboo's of being a woman!

WHEN I first came here I lost my heart to this temple dedicated to the great pharoah, Ramasses III.That is a dramatic statement to make but, even though this was my first visit to Egypt, I had researched Karnak and Luxor, the Pyramids, Sphinx and Egyptian Museum and so knew a good bit about them even though I had never seen them.But I knew nothing about the Temple of Medinet Habu, hadn’t researched it or seen pictures of it and so was absolutely captivated when I saw this wonderful site for the first time.Not quite as grand as Karnak, it still stopped me in my tracks when I got out of the coach at the entrance where an EGP80 (about UK£3.50) entrance ticket let you inside.The shock factor of seeing that huge gate in the shape of a Syrian fort for the first time will live with me forever.It is decorated with battle scenes showing Ramasses’ wars in Syria and it is quite simply breathtaking from the giant figures to the wealth of hieroglyphics.Here was greatness commemorating greatness and, walking closer, the hairs on the back of my neck were standing up as I was swallowed by the great gate.There is no let up inside where the sheer visual impact of the inner courtyard is like a blow to the senses.On the left is a row of heavily carved columns in front of a wall full of painted scenes and on the right is a row of large figures and deeply carved square columns while the quality of the figures and hieroglyphics either side of the next entranceway is superb.You should also look up and enjoy a riot of colour on paintings underneath ceiling stonework which has protected it from the twin ravages of weather and time.One of the carved scenes showing Ramasses on a wild ox hunt must have been done by a master because it is brilliant.Moving through into a second courtyard with more wonderful carvings and paintings, you go on until you emerge into an area which has a network of rooms and broken column bases including royal throne plinths and even a toilet hole.The wonders just kept coming from baboon and vulture figures to numerous figures of the gods, many still in full colour, and we were even lucky enough to see mud bricks being made in the traditional way by restoration workmen.A disabled person in a wheelchair could negotiate many parts of this site because ramps link different sections, but there are many uneven stone surfaces and it would be a bumpy ride although well worth the effort.It was all absolutely fascinating and I just couldn’t get enough of it. Tearing myself away to leave was one of the hardest things I did in my entire holiday because I just didn’t want to go.Fortunately I have numerous pictures and videos to remind myself of this wonderful place which covers 64,000 sq mtrs, one of the largest memorial temples in Egypt.It will always have a place in my memories and my heart and I cannot recommend the site too highly. It is that much over-used expression – a “must see” place.Don’t miss it under any circumstances and, if necessary, dedicate an entire morning to see it. We did and loved every minute of it. A truly stunning experience.

i couldn't find proper word to describe the beautiful scene and history related to it as well.it's the best temple compared to the rest although its not that huge.i enjoyed also that we reach it by boat
Originally a long line of sphinxes linked Karnak and Luxor temples. Today only a short section remains, however the authorities plan to reopen the entire route. Still spectacular even now.
we dont dive or snorkell but enjoyed the lazy day on the boat was a bit pricey 65 english pounds each food was good did not see much on the glass bottom part of boat
Brilliant. One of the premier dive sites of the World. Don't miss this one. Species of fauna and coral galore.

The content of the Antiquities museum is extensive. There is a lot on display. Having a guide to provide context to the artifacts is beneficial. You can go through relatively quickly or really take it all in with more time. It all depends if you seeing the museum as one of several attractions or a specific point of interest.
We were fortunate to have an excellent Egyptologist as our guide and gave our group so much insight to the history and Muslim practices.

It's not my first time and it's not my last one, it's taking a piece of my hart, just relax and enjoy the life, perfect spot for snorkeling and diving, you must camp here for many days or for ever if u can.

It is worth coming to Cairo just to come here.The number and quality of the exhibits is amazing. The Tutankhamun exhibits alone are simply awesome. If you are very short on time it is possible to head straight for them (upstairs on the right hand side of the museum, the mask is at the back), but you would be missing out.The museum was much quieter than we expected. There were some very large tour groups but if you were flexible in the order you moved around the museum these were easy to avoid.We were staying near the museum so just got a taxi there. It was easy to buy tickets on arrival. Please note you need to buy an extra EGP50 ticket for each camera you wish to use inside the museum.It is also easy to hire an English speaking guide outside the museum. We chose not to as we wanted to wander at our own pace.

Very historical and beautiful place with two mosques you can enter and also a military museum. There is also a restaurant that sells delicious grilled meats. You will also find stunning views of the whole of Cairo,  there are many stands selling cold drinks and gifts. Some amazing Islamic architecture, can be very busy at times.

One of my favorites in Egypt! The history, the romance, the beautiful island, the boat ride, the sunset, the magnificent temple and the love story behind it, the Nile river views from the island...everything perfect! Thank you to UNESCO for preserving this. This temple used to be located in the island of Philae but had to be relocate to save it from frequent floods and also due to the construction of the Aswan Dam.

Words aren’t enough to describe the feeling of touching those ancient structures. Feeling so close to that time. Beautiful from panoramic view.Still you can touch them, i am sure in future you have to watch from far away. If you travel Egypt and not going pyramid it’s like going nandos and not eating chicken !

The Temple of Horus at Edfu (about 70 miles south of Luxor) is a little over 2000 years old with the oldest parts built by Ptolemy III, and so is from a completely different era to the temples at Luxor. It Is a huge, well preserved structure with most ceilings still intact, consequently it makes quite an impact.An enormous Pylon, with its capital intact, displaying gigantic carvings of the hawk-god Horus is the impressive introduction to the temple.Beyond is a gigantic courtyard with 18 columns and further along are more halls with columns and ceilings still showing vivid colours, a torch would be helpful in some parts of this area.At the far end of the temple is a sarcophagus decorated with a model of a traditional funeral barque, a most impressive sight.On one of the outer walls on the left hand side of the temple can be seen a memorial to British soldiers killed in service in this region, although incongruous it is still quite moving.The complex is about a 20 minute walk from the river, but no tourist should attempt to walk. Edfu town was the least appealing place we visited in Egypt, dusty, dirty with dilapidated buildings and many locals just sat by the side of the road. Stray dogs scavenging for food, people crammed in the backs of ancient vans or trucks making their way about town, contrasted with some individuals in new prestige cars.Transport is generally by a caleche, a small carriage probably pulled by an emaciated, badly treated horse, which is not a pleasant experience either.Of course entering and leaving the temple site you will have to pass the avenue of vendors, who are even more aggressive than those in Luxor.Overall a wonderful temple, well worth a visit, but be prepared.

Of course an amazing collection of artifacts from the ancient Egypt, need at least three to four hours just for a first visit. King Tut's tomb is definitively fabulous.But really the authorities in Egypt should maintain this museum it looks so miserable, no maintenance the content is fabulous but the set up is miserable and do not do any good for the artifacts

If you visit only one temple on the leg between Aswan and Luxor, this should be it because it is largely intact and therefore gives a very good idea of the structure, layout and design of ancient Egyptian temples. We were there in the early afternoon and so avoided the crowds.
it is better to go early morning before many tourist come there. then every one can have awesome experience same like us. grate place and many wonders like tunnels and other constructions.

The Temple dedicated to Ramses III is an unmissable attraction, as its construction is well preserved, as are the paintings and reliefs that, for the most part, depict combat scenes. Wear comfortable clothes and shoes to make the most of the trip.

What an awesome experience to tour the Pyramids of Giza!!! To be able to go inside the oldest of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World is a mind blowing experience. I keep asking myself - Who Does This and when the answer is Me, Me Me - I’m so humbled by these fantastic opportunities. It was just amazing to see and feel the Pyramid.I paid the extra fee to go inside and had planned to climb to the upper room but once inside, I experienced a hot flash and had to forego climbing up into the rafters. But just going inside made me happy because, once again, not many get that experience.You have opportunities to ride camels, get photos with camels and so much more while visiting the Pyramids.I also got to go to the evening Sight and Sound show at the Pyramids.There is a lot of history on these grounds and it really make you think about the Bible and it’s teachings.It was a wonderful experience and I highly recommend visiting the Pyramids.

Given the fly in-fly out tour we were on, this was just enough to whet our appetite for more - more than the tour guide could squeeze in between planes while still letting us get to tour the temples. Now we have to go back and see the National Geographic issues that covered the detail so well. Also, this was the hottest day in our tour - over 40'C so be sure to take water with you everywhere.
Yes, spectacular wall and roof painting/carvings but many other tombs are almost as good and the 50% extra charge to visit this single tomb cannot really be justified.

Definitely worth visiting the pyramids of Giza. It will cost you 160 Egyptian pounds to get in and from there you can wonder around. People will constantly try to get you to ride their camels and you shouldn’t pay more than 50 Egyptian pounds each for this. You will have to pay them if they take your photo on the camel also.
I've watched the sun rise! Rode a camel half way up and walk the rest of the way! Awesome!!! Make sure you bargain with the Bedouins when you get half way!

This temple is as good as it gets, just spectacular! It is really 9 temples on the same 10 -20 acre site, One temple has an amazing 134 huge column 15m high. It take 10 people holding out reached hands to go around the base of one column! The animal sculptures that are plentiful on this site are just amazing as they form a long guard to the entry. WARNING do not be tricked by the guy wanted to swap 50 Euro coins for a 50 euro note. The Egyptian pound looks like a 1 euro coin and there will be a number of those in the mix.

We visited King Tut's tomb today. Very small but really beautiful paintings and a lovely gold wooden coffin in a stone sarcophagus. To see his mummy was moving. It will be a thing for the kids to tell their friends back home, given his fame. We had the place to ourselves. The  paintings have stuck in my mind more than the other tombs we saw in Valley of the Kings.

A guide is a must to show you the most seen exhibits like the tut museum side of thingsMummy room is soooo amazingNot all exhibits are clearly markedAmazing placeNeed time to study it...... read on the exhibits so that you can enjoy more when you actually see it.

I REALLY enjoyed this place. From its classic Edwardian Building. To the vast number of displays at the museum. The only problem is that there is so much stuff to see from the bodies of dead Pharaohs to a unbelievable amount of real gold that is just everywhere. If you have the time do go and be prepared to have a great time.

unfortunately i ve saw music fountain show in dubai so this is smaller and no so exciting as dubai, is nice but compare to the other one small,i would like if they have egyptian music and not international

Whatever else you see during your visit to luxor you must not miss going here. This is by far one of my favourite places to take my tourists to. It is a wonderful place, very serene. The mortuary temple of Ramses III. Some timeless carvings. It really is awesome.

A fantastic experience! Ras Mo is so gorgeous both above and below sea level. With many beaches, mangroves, hidden lake, earthquake crack and wonderful rock formations, it looks almost extraterrestrial.Go for diving or snorkelling and it's an underwater paradise! Marsa Breika is one of my favourite, with the beautiful Bedawi Bedouin Eco Camp right on the beach :) A must visit in South Sinai!

This place is jam-packed with thousands of pieces any one of which would anchor a museum somewhere else. Room after room of crowded displays of antiquities. You pay extra to see the mummies but it is well worth it. There is something odd about paying money to see the desiccated remains of people who ruled the world (or at least their corner of it) thousands of years ago. Our four year old had been looking forward to seeing the mummies but was surprised to learn that they didn't look like the wrapped up creatures in Scooby Doo movies. He was initially scared and uncomfortable but managed to make his way through the room without too much trauma! The items from King Tut's tomb are incredible and shine as brightly today as they did when he was alive. I recommend going with a guide who can help make sure you see the highlights and explain their significance.

From all the temples I visited in Egypt, this one, was specially amazing. It is so majestic and spectacular; there are not other words to describe it. We stayed there for three hours, admiring and observing every part of it, trying to understand the magnificent work that lays there.

I was just blown away by this place. The idea that this was done 4000 years ago and that the color and the carvings are still intact is amazing. Our guide Rania Raouf of Aladdin Tours, wanted for us to visit the site in the morning to avoid the crowds. She was absolutely right. By the time we were walking out, all the tour buses were lining up to get into the temple. If you can I also would recommend you getting a guide and doing a private tour. Makes it easier to maneuver around, ask questions and get more information.
This visit was wonderful but actually to much to absorb in a few hours. You could easily spend a day there.

Our first trip from the boat to Karnak temple was mind blowing, It did not let me down in any way from what I was expecting instead it far exceeded anything I could have imagined.  Great not to be missed......

Very good temple that can be seen easily from the Nile, dedicated to two gods and each has their own equal side. fairy intact and we visited late in the day when the sun was going down, this made the place look spectacular with warm tones of the stone. The crocodile mummy museum is a couple of minutes away and worth a visit.

We ended up adding this to our stay in Aswan last minuet and could not have been happier with our choice. We took a boat cruise from our hotel to the Nubian village. Upon arrival we visited the local craftsman who were selling merchandise and more then willing to explain there craft! We got many beautiful handmade scarfs. We then proceeded to a local Nubian home. We were welcomed in with open arms where we were explained the local culture. We were able to walk freely around the house and see multiple crocodiles which are a local tradition to keep in the homes! We thought this was going to be super touristy but we're pleasantly surprised by the local flavor this tour brought.

Glad we travelled by plane instead of the coach. However the add on cost for us was £203 rather than £106 (each). Having said that though the temples were magnificent so well worth the money. I will say to anyone go to Egypt now, due to the downturn in tourism you will have the sights almost to yourself.An absolute must see.

The museum has the best collection of Egyptian artifacts in the world. What it lacks in fancy display it makes up for in the quality of its artifacts.The extra mummy room exhibit is awesome and worth the extra cost.They are building an ever bigger Egyptian museum in Giza which should open in 2020. They are slowly being over pieces from this museum to the new one, so go soon before too much is gone, but the new one isn’t open yet.
What makes this museum excellent is for sure the Tut exhibition.  Honestly, every thing else is kind of shabby looking but you go for the Tut exhibition.

If you have the chance to visit Egypt, you should definitely go and visit this complex - it is an amazing proof of the ancient civilization & modern technology for preserving it.It is quite a long drive (we came by bus from Aswan) but it is worth it.

The mosque soars to the heavens, and is definitely worth the visit.  Amazing history.  It also boasts wonderful views of the city.  We had to fend off one of the "picture for a fee" folks, and and aggressive cabbie on the way out.
This is a definite must see! So much knowledge found in one building! The mummy room is also a definite must see!
snorkeling here was fab, excellent guides, food, boat nothing bad to say.It is like a totally different world, the instructors give you all the help and confidence needed to really enjoy yourself.

I visited in March 2012 and I highly recommend going to Dahshur to see the Red, Bent, and Black pyramids. The Red pyramid is the one that you can go inside, and your ticket to visit the Red and Bent pyramids includes the price to go inside the Red pyramid.The guard at the top will expect some baksheesh, so give him no more than 2 - 3 pounds, after you come out! You can give him nothing since he really doesn't do anything for you except to say hello. If you take a pic with him, then tip him.You can see the Black pyramid from the Bent pyramid, but you can't get any closer unless you want to walk over. You cannot go inside the Bent pyramid, but you should walk around to the back of the Bent pyramid to see the Queen's pyramid.You should allot approximately 2 hours for these pyramids, this time includes going inside the Red pyramid. Photos are allowed inside the Red pyramid.Would I visit again? With a group, yes, but not solo again. It is worth the once in a lifetime visit.

Built by Hatshepsut - the only woman pharaoh to rule Egypt (1473-1458 BCE) – the Hatshepsut temple is actually an ancient funerary shrine in the upper part of Egypt. Though the earlier mortuary Temple of Mentuhotep II located close by is considered to be an inspiration for this temple, the Hatshepsut Temple has been layered as per the classical Theban form & displays many dissimilarities with its original model. But the most interesting aspect of this temple is its alignment with the winter solstice sunrise & clever use of a light box to direct sunlight in a pre-determined path to illuminate certain statues as per their importance & hierarchy! Simply magical!!
this large temple in the city of Luxor is worth seeing, don't miss it.You will spend a couple of hours here and can then go to the valley of kings which is near by.
Very much worth a visit if you like your history. Standing  structures and statues with loads of engraving. .. excellent visit but hot in afternoon 38° no pools to cool off so make sure you cover up.

The trip was comfortable since it is a long car trip, 4 hours round trip and 4 hours back. It's all morning, but it's worth it. Our guide Mohammed was very attentive to all the needs we could have. Our group was varied and of different nationalities. He was very courteous and we felt safe with him. We would have liked a little more explanation about the temple but no guide enters them. The explanations are outside the temple and then free time.Both going there and coming back there was no problem and the temperature for this time was perfect!Thank you Mohammed. Thank u Mohammed!

The only thing worth seeing in Egypt! Entrance to the area 200 EGP, entrance to the pyramid 400 EGP more. We travelled Egypt throughout and the pyramids was the only nice visit. Here is a serious tip to travel Egypt: Arrive on a morning via plane (trust me, you do not want to even see the trains or buses), take an Uber to the pyramids (also trust me, you do not want to use a regular taxi), see the pyramids (takes c. 3h), beware of con artists everywhere, trust nobody, even the authorities, take an Uber back to the airport and fly away. Note that there are corrupted authorities also at the airport.

I love this place!I have been lucky enough to have been to this museum 4 times on different trips to Cairo and without doubt this is one of the most amazing museums in the world!There is just so much to see - it's a shame to run past some spectacular artefacts, time just gets away from you here....The death mask from Tutankhamen's tomb is obviously the must see piece here, but do not miss the incredible smaller artefacts . Take time to view as much as possible!This is spectacular... A visit to Egypt without heading to this stunning place  would be a travesty!

Loved every minute visiting the temple. It's amazing. Be prepared to pay an Egyptian pound to use the loo!  It's busy but you can see everything. Worth getting a guided tour as they explain everything you need to know to appreciate what you're seeing. Beware of the men that hang around. They start taking you around and talking about the Temple then expect money when you didn't ask for their advice in the first place. Only downside but apart from that it's brilliant.

One must spend time here! You need to walk amongst these ruins to appreciate the expertise and craftmanship of days gone by! Nothing the current generations walking the earth can build will ever surpass this temple!

Loved the being able to get up close and personal wioth so many exhibits. And an added bonus, they have just now lifted the ban on taking photo's inside, provided that you dont use your flash...we were so lucky the ban was lifted just three days prior to our attending. There are over 250 exhibits to view...those that are not in storage. Certainly a must for any traveller to Cairo.

Have been enthralled by this museum since first visiting in the mid-1990s.  Came back in 2002 and again in 2022.  It's old, dusty and has no a/c but the collection is phenomenal.  Some of the good stuff is moving to the Grand Egyptian Museum (date TBD), but there will still be enough here to keep anyone interested in ancient Egypt immersed for months.

If Luxor were like an American shopping mall, then the Karnak Temple would be like the Macy's because it's the anchor that brings in business.  The trouble with Karnak is, it's very touristy.  It gets all the traffic, and the shops and vendors that come with it.  It's absolutely astounding, no question.  But it takes some effort to filter out the distractions and just focus on the majesty of the place.  It's a very large complex, too.  So either bring a stroller or be prepared to listen to your kids whining (if you bring kids).  We brought kids to Luxor, and their favorite part of the whole trip wasn't Karnak, wasn't Valley of the Kings, wasn't Hatshepsut's Deir el-Bahri - the little ones liked the hotel swimming pool and ice cream the most.  Just a warning to anyone contemplating bringing small ones to Luxor.  Next trip, I'm taking the kids to Sharm El Sheikh where they can swim their hearts out and devour gelato.

The Hanging Church is a unique church that was built literally floating on water, too bad it the water was removed lately to prevent the church from any threats. The Church has some good stuff to buy. It is decorated in the Islamic tradition, I prefer Coptic tradition, but it is good to see how a church with some Islamic touches would look like. People there are friendly. I am looking forward to attend a liturgy or a mass there! Too bad photographing is not allowed.

Finally I made it to the pyramids and it was worth it. We have all seen pyramids on thousands of pictures but nothing can replace the visit. To be there and enjoy the whole area buy walking around makes your knowledge and impressions totally different from the perspective you had. Pyramids are big and beautiful, impressive and this World Heritage should be taken care off better so it is preserved for the future generations.

Overall a lovely day out with lunch included. It costs extra to hire snorkelling equipment so take your own if you have it, the only negative we have is that whilst snorkelling we noticed the toilet had been emptied and we were snorkelling close by to toilet paper smeared with faecal excrement ... in an area of conservation this was pretty appalling and also dangerous to our health, surely there must be a safer, more environmentally friendly way of disposing of toilet waste?
Another incredible sight that Egypt is known for. The size of this complex is immense. Well worth the drive down from Aswan.

You just have to go here.  It is such an eye-opener to firstly drive through the 'garbage city' to get to the churches.  A very humbling experience in itself.Then to get to the incredible cave church of St Simon but then to find 2 other amazing cave churches there, also with stunning sculptures carved into the cave walls.  There was something truly special about this place.  There is NO entrance fee but a donation for the upkeep of the "church "is accepted, and to be honest it was absolutely worth a donation.You just have to see this!

One can reach Dendera (near Qena) by cruise from Luxor, by renting a motorboat (expensive)or by car/minibus trip through local tour-operator, which usually also includes a visit to Abydos. Cruise departures very early in the morning and during our the first two hours were freezingly cold. Despite this, the calm and beautiful nature views compensate this inconvinience. There is a lot to see in Dendera complex - I would recommed not to miss the Sacred Lake, the Temple of the birth of Isis, a walk around the temple walls of Hathor Temple and to climb the stairway to the roof of that to see the famous Zodiak depiction and kiosk.

This was the second time of visiting this site and it was just as breath-taking.  We were lucky in that the 2 of us had a private vehicle with drivers and guide from Aswan.  It was a very early start (03:30) but well worth it as you travel across the desert in a convoy.  It is dark on the way there so you can see the stars and light on the way back so you can see the mirages of the desert.Our trip was organised by MEMPHIS TOURS, an Egyptian based company who were great.  The guide spoke good English and had a good command of his subject.

This attraction is written in most tour books. But, it is actually underwhelming to see. There is no admission charge. You can quickly snap a photo and be on your way. Of course, there are vendors here also. It does have an interesting history and is the first monuments that you see upon entering the west bank. The two faceless Colossi of Memnon were each cut from a single block of stone and weigh 1000 tons. They are set in front of the main entrance to a funerary temple, the largest in Egypt, which are slowly being excavated.

I am a little reluctant to write this as I don't wish to spoil the magic. Ras Mohammed is an amazing place, so so beautiful and peaceful. I first visited 12 years ago and not much has changed. It is as beautiful on land as in the sea, and my 5 year old son was blown away by the snorkellingAmazing place, cant wait to return
We loved this temple. It was a picture postcard, and it inspired us very much.

stars were amazing, but if you go on the trip with the telecopes, take an Iphone as well as your camera unless very small as they will not work with the telescope if you want to take close up pictures.

In order to understand Cairo history, you must visit this mosque. Building is very impressive and has a lot of corners which are very interesting. One of the memorable place which should not be missed.

A must-see place: the 3 great pyramids are very close and the sphinx is not far away either. Kheops to do without hesitation the ascent from the inside to the tomb of the king in the heart of the pyramid. Rudimentary staircase, it is hot, elderly and claustrophobic people refrain. Allow 2 hours for the site

There are so many pieces exhibited in the Egyptian Museum that 2 full days would not be enough if you don't want to feel rushed and if you want to actually look at anything for more than 1 minute. A guide provided by my tour company seemed a touch annoyed that I spent more than 10 minutes inside the Tutankhamen exhibit. The whole visit took upwards of 4 hours and it felt very rushed. My advice is, take 2 maybe even 3 days... this is not a joke, unless you're not really interested and are there just to brag to your friends that you saw king tut's golden mask... Place is literally littered with artifacts, there are so many antiquities that by the 4th hour they all start blending together and you, kind of, start tuning them out, merely looking for the most famous pieces. It is however absolutely a fantastic place for those who have a passion for ancient history

Place is truly remarkable and you just wonder how Egyptians built them 4000 years ago. I used a tour guide and it was definitely helpful to have it too.You should beware of sellers. One of my friend was duped. They offered turban for free, and then secretly put a necklace in my friend’s bag. He demanded my friend pay for it. My friend tried to avoid/ignore him but he just insisted.
A historic place in Alexandria on the sea. the castle is very beautiful for a morning walk that takes you back to other times
We are fascinated with King Tut and mummies, so a visit to the Egyptian Museum for a few hours was a must.  There is a fee o go inside and you can sign up to take photos inside for extra cost.

Valley of the Queens is located on the west bank near Luxor.  There are about 80 known tombs of queens, princes and princesses here.  Many of the tombs are merely caves in the rocks as they are unfinished and without decoration.  Embellishment of these tombs mostly consists of paintings on stucco with a few reliefs.  Most tombs are closed to the public at the moment.  Tomb of Queen Nefertari (Qv #66, entry fee LE 1000) is now open and fully restored.  It is by far the best decorated royal tomb. The quality of art is extremely intricate and the colours extremely well restored. The other tombs in this area which are open for viewing are Tomb of Prince Amen-her-khopshef, (QV #55, entry fee LE 50)  a son of Ramses III, which contains well-preserved colours on its wall paintings, and Tomb of queen Tyti (QV #52, entry fee LE 50). Photography is prohibited. Regret that tourists cannot stay for more than 20 minutes to see this most beautiful monument.
We enjoyed strolling through, and paid extra to see the mummies. They were out of maps but were able to google where to find things. Definitely glad we went.
Quite incredible that they managed to move this from its original site where it would have been flooded. It is an easy flight from Aswan.

Our cruise ship sailed to Qena from Luxor (40 miles away) and we journeyed to Dendara (5 miles away). There were no others at this beautiful temple situated on a large walled site. This is were the ancients believed the goddess Hathor gave birth to Horus child. It is the temple of birthing and love. The ceilings have a rich tourquise colour and are stunning. This is considered to be the most beautiful and best preserved temple in Egypt. It is not in the usual Nile/Luxor itinerary as the River is often too shallow for cruise boats to reach it. There are walls surrounding the the temple area. See it if you can. There is also a Coptic church that was used later in history. We were able to access the roof of this temple as the it is one of the few that are still intact. On the roof is a chapel which holds a replica of the Zodiac of Dendara, the original is in the Louvre.
I love this spot...the huge Colossi of memnon stands regally overlooking the valley of the Kings as if its guardian.
Loved this trip, whole day out on a boat with 3 snorkelling stops! Was the first time I had done this so was very nervous but the guides on the boat were fantastic.  Great for getting a good tan!

In my opinion this is the richest museum i have ever seen. So beautiful with excellent collection and for different periods and locations in Egypt.I was really impressed , very rich contents, big sizes, however it is needed to be more organized ,it is too many for the place, To be a better displayed it need much bigger place with a specialist in displaying.Overall this is one of the best i have seen as i was so impressed and when i go toCairo again i will re-visit the museum again.Excellent.

A must see maybe more than once. We saw the pyramids with a camel ride. A must-do once. Know that you have to hold on with one hand leaving the other hand to hold maybe a camera. There is no place on the camel to store your water, your bags etc. Our guide for this ride was Mohamed and he was great. He rode a horse, we rode the camels and a man walked and led the camels. Mohamed knows how to take excellent pictures and did so many times. We got off and on about three times and had a walk-abouts to sit on the pyramid and take pictures. For us it was a relief to get off and walk. The pyramids are of course awe-inspiring and there are many theories about how they were built.We visited via a camel ride which was well done, different and part of the culture. The “saddle” left us both with raw spots on our seats. I’m not sure what kind of pants to suggest to wear.
Wonderful historic landmark, with a breathtaking view of the port. Be careful of your step as the stairs inside the castle are tricky.
Beautiful history to Saqqara that makes you appreciate Giza even more - go with a guide who can explain the history

The Egyptian Museum houses some of the oldest and most significant artifacts in the world. Some of the items pre-date the pharaohs. Most impressive is the statuary which reflects artistic style changes over the years.The Mummy Room is upstairs on the right, and although small, it's a must-see. As I recall, there were two mummies (Ramses II and Thutmosis Iv) that were unwrapped and a few more still in tact. You must pay an extra fee for the Mummy Room when you buy your ticket outside the main entrance. Patrons in here are asked to keep quiet and be respectful as would be expected for any county's ruler "lying in state" for public viewing.On the way out on the main floor, there's a small gift shop containing a lot of books and small  items. Prices are high for the books and reasonable for the other items.The museum is surrounded by a lot of heavy traffic in the streets. I hate to see people trying to dash through the cars as they come from The Ramses Hilton Hotel and the Talaat Harb area. This is a good way to kill yourself. Taxis are cheap in Cairo, so be sure to use them when going to the museum. You don't have to pay extra for the cab driver to wait for you as there are plenty of cabs in the area.

This is a series of temples with different characteristics from each other. A guide is a must to appreciate it all. It covers quite a large area. We went back for the sound and light show but wouldn't recommend it. But DO go for a tour during the day.

We went 2 days to RMNP for diving with Diving & Discovery Dive Shop (Iberotel Fanara Club).  We were on board the boat all day; didn't get on land.  Great dive and snorkeling spots!  We stayed on the East side due to sea conditions, but saw some pretty incredible coral and sea life.  Definitely worth the trip! :)

We came for a day trip whilst staying in Cyprus. It was a great day and we saw some amazing sites. The pyramids were beautiful and so historic and magical. There is a real sense of mystery when you get there. I didn't go in the pyramids because I am claustrophobic but I was assured it is amazing. haha! Go!

It is an amazing place and especially since it is over 3000 years old. It is best if you hire a guide (there are ones for hire right near the site). There is so much they can explain to you that you would never figure out on your own.

Impressive avenue, which is even more great if you imagine yourself in the Age that it was built.Most of the Sphinxes are really well conservated.It's located between Luxor and Karnak temples and shows, once more, how great were Egyptian Pharaons

The tomb is small but decorated with some interested paintings and you have to bear in mind  that is is there that was found the colossal and famous treasure of Tutankhamun which is displayed in Cairo Antiquities Museum ! That's why it is one of the most visited tombs and there is often a long queue at the entrance...

This is an amazing temple and well worth a visit as it has some interesting aspects as well as being well preserved. We particularly enjoyed the perfumery room Here you can see the recipes for the perfumes that were made so many years ago. And another bonus was that there were only a few visitors compared to the crowds In Luxor and Karnak
Got to see it was amazing, You have to go inside if you go see the pyramids, its tight once you get in, but its worth it. I can't tell you how I felt after getting to the tomb area, it was amazing.

Went to see the Tomb of King Tutankhamun while at the valley of the Kings - Had to pay an extra fee to enter the tomb but was well worth it.The tomb is a great place filled with  History and Magic - To think it has survived for so long in time.

For me, the best part of this experience was the journey: first, the boat ride from the center of Aswan up the Nile with a massive sand dune to one side of the river and rocks and reeds to the other side. On a mid-April morning, it made for a beautiful, enjoyable journey. Once we landed, we rode camels from the shoreline to the village. This was my first camel-riding experience, and it was a great place to do it.As for the town itself, there are a lot of colorfully-painted houses, plenty of little shops in which to buy trinkets, and houses where you can stop and have a drink and maybe even see a pet crocodile. We went inside one and thoroughly enjoyed the people there, as well as the bright decorations and sand floors.The scenery is fantastic! All in all, it was a fun way to spend a morning on the Nile.

This place is a must and the story behind it is truly amazing. You need a tour guide to truly appreciate how remarkable this temple really is. You will need a minimum of 2 hours here to go through the temple. Make sure you ignore the sellers on the way in if you engage them you will get stuck with them lol. Truly amazing!!!
If you visit Sharm, you have to go to Ras Mohammed.  This is th best snorkelling diving ever.  We have been here many times and it's just amazing, we never get sick of it.  Highly recommended

I took my children and a friend from USA, it was really nice for all of us to see  great historical pyramids. But, the biggest problem you are the people selling services there. You should not talk At all to any one from the people there. It is much better to visit through a tourism company and make it  early in the morning to visit all the places inside.

One of the best museums in the world. What the Egyptian Museum lacks in lighting, display, and explanatory text, it makes up for fully with the sheer volume and quality of items on display. The King Tut exhibit is the highlight but be sure to budget plenty of time.

The treasures located in the museum are fabulous. The statue with the life like eyes is freaky and mesmerizing at the same time. The craftsmanship of this and other pieces are outstanding. The Ancient Egyptians were on a whole different stratosphere with building. Wonderful place to spend one or more days in discovering! Unfortunately no photos are allowed.
These had been on my bucket list forever and they are just as amazing and majestic as you could ever hope for. Well worth it.

I was here when the dawn light ran down the temple hall to illuminate three statues as it does each October and February -- said to be Ramses birthday and coronation date.  The crowding is incredible so in addition to being there on that date, I was also there a day early when the visitors were fewer.  (On the big day I got up at 0100 to get in line to be about the fifth person in line.)

Mini egypt park is nice place to get insight in all monuments and tourist visit places all over egypt. Sure saves a lot of time and money ;)Park is also build to take a lot of pictures as a tourist. Our guide ahmed dak sure did a great job assisting with that and guiding us through the park.Only thing I really missed was a little more historical insight and depth of the background to all the different buildings and statues.The tour is about two hours.

It's hard to believe that the very next day after my seeing the museum that the rebels and looters came and ransacked this great place.  I had a chance to see it in all of its glory. Magnificent sculptures, gigantic statues and breathtaking pieces of jewelry were just a few of the many things that I saw.  Fortunately a few days later many of the stolen items were recovered.FYI I would recommend a guide when touring the museum so will can fully appreciate what you are seeing.  I, for one, learned a lot.

Incredible place. Well worth a visit but it is quite claustrophobic and a bit of a squeeeze getting inside the pyramid. Once inside we were hassled by a guy waving his arms around wanting money. Just ignore!Outside the pyramids do not make eye contact with local vendors or point your camera towards any one, as they will want cash for photos.Apart from that it is a once in a lifetime experience.

You need at least 2-3 days to see everything in this museum. I have been to a few large museums in my life and this is by far one of the top 3. It is at $10 USD to enter which is well worth the money. A definite must see!
Beautiful and historic museum in the heart of civilization. Get a good private guide, it makes everything so much more meaningful. The Tut exhibit is worth visiting Cairo by itself!

5 stars for the mosque view inside and out. We did Jummah here, and it was very impressive. The Wudhu area could do with some modernisation, but overall, it was adequate for ablution. Be careful as there are street beggers that linger outside.
This temple is huge! It's also right on the water so a very impressive and picturesque place to visit.
If you come to Egypt ,visit here! The building is so beautiful surrounded by amazing scenery When  you walk into the mosque its a wonderful experience like stepping back in time

Saqqara is a must visit place for people visiting Egypt, especially those who live history. Not as popular as the Great Pyramids of Giza, it has great potential to be even a greater place given the fact that only 20% has been discovered till now.

There is so much to see here you could spend all day easily. Possibly even 2 days. Google says it's open until 7 but don't be fooled, it's only open until 4:30 so get there early. So many things to see here though. Well worth a visit

The Cairo Museum is essentially a preserved time capsule that now allows today's people, and will allow future generations, to view aspects of the lives of prior ancient Egyptian civilization. The exhibitions leaves one wondering if whether the ancients intended to memorialize their societies and existence for later study and review.

I spent about one hour and a half here and found I should have had at least one hour more. The Great Hypostyle Hall took the better part of my attention. The scale of the building is really impressive. Colours are unfortunately gone (only  below the roof supports, where there's always shadow, did some of the colours survive).
go and feel the royal life of our the pharos and kings of egypt , worth going and investing your time for 5hours

It was first temple we visited when we were in Egypt. So the experience was just amazing. Before coming here, we only saw these statues in movies and documentaries. But experiencing them in real life was out of this world. All these stories we have heard, read so far, felt so real. The bigger than life statues and the stories behind the temples mesmerised us. It is a must visit for anyone travelling to Luxor.
Beautiful old temple spread over a large area, The main part can be seen in 2-3 hours but more time needed if you want to see it all.

Just down the road from one of the biggest and grandest mosques that I have ever seen this church is a wonderfull piece of modern structure with the old fashioned church feel which makes you feel very welcome--well worth a visit
This is the first recorded pyramid and although not as big as the Giza ones it is just as impressive and less crowded

Our group was fortunate to visit the pyramids eary before the general public. Many of us climbed the Khufu/ Great Pyramid.  Wow! Steep and somewhat difficult; especially since someone forgot to turn on the electric lights. A couple of flashlights and light from personal cell phones was all there was to guide us.  In many ways it made it more interesting.

we chose to take a coach trip from our Nile cruise, and I guess it took about 2 and a half hours through desert. We had started the day early , getting up at 0330, so we slept most of the coach trip, but i hadn't been in the desert for a while, so I managed to stay awake to see some of the rocks, pyramidal shaped by the windI had been told that this trip was well worth it, and it was!There is a good visitor centre, with pictures and information about the moving of the monument (to prevent it flooding) from its original site. Since I came home I have found a picture of it in its original place, to see how it compares. If you are interested, it is worth doing thisThat siad, the present site is magnificent. it is a good walk from the visitor centre to the monuments, but thee is a buggy availableYou can take photos of the outside, but not the inside, but you can buy a pack of photosHaving seen many photos of this, it still does not prepare you for the 65 feet high statues of Rameses II which greet you.Inside the wall carvings are truly amazingIt was 39 degrees, on a March morning, so you need to be wearing sun hat or sunscreenIt is an absolute marvel when you consider how it was moved, and the exhibition is well worth finding time to look atI would have loved to see it in its original setting, near the river, but this is amazingIt is the picture of Egypt we all see on brochures etc, but unbelievable in its actualityThere are the usual bazaars on the way out. We were advised not to buy because of 'fake' goods, but they did look attractive

Been on my bucket list for years and finally got round to visiting. Wasn’t disappointed. It’s humbling to stand there and realise that while,they were being built my ancestors were still building mud huts!Went in the morning as it’s cooler and quieter. Our guide was brilliant and we weren’t hassled by as many hawkers as I’d been expecting

We could not believe it took just 2 artists and 2 years to paint the inside of the church. Every painting is a story from the Bible. This is a must see.I have never seen a church like this one anywhere in the world.

Egypt is not only a Muslim country. There's also a well-nourished Coptic community, and this interesting church, named "Hanging" after its location above a gatehouse of the Roman fortress known as "Babylon Fortress", is one of their most important worship places. A legend tells that Joseph and Mary, with Baby Jesus, hid there when they escaped from Palestine.

My family and I visited the citadel not knowing that it would be as fascinating as it turned out to be. Even though the Military Museum was shut at the time, we had endless fun in the Mohammed Ali mosque, the National Police Museum, the side mosques and the Citadel fortress itself. One tip: ask for a certified English guide, it will make your visit rife with new information!
Ras Mohamed is a great dive site location. I did I day trip there and the amount of fish and coral I saw was truly amazing. Don't forget you need a visa from the airport to vist this location

Almost universally, before and during our travels, the advice had been - don't bother with King Tut's tomb, it's very small.But since when has size been the be all and end all? It may be a tad on the bijou side, but when you walk down there and see the man himself lying there, you cannot help but be whacked by a rather surprising emotional punch.To set eyes on the boy king, and to see, on the other side of the compact tomb his gold sarcophagus, sat within a stone container, is remarkable.We travelled on a day when the sun was sensationally hot in the middle of August. The result? Hardly any crowds in the Valley of the Kings. The fellow on the gate of King Tut's tomb was snoozing when we got there and as we gave our 100 Egyptian pound tickets (about £10) over to be stamped we peered down a long set of steep steps.You could almost sense the sense of adventure Howard Carter must have felt all those years ago.At the end of the steps you enter the tomb - two small rooms, one with the mummy of the king, the other the only other valuables left in the tomb.We stood there with just one other couple and drank in the experienceThe paintings on the wall maybe a little more basic, but the history in which you stand is just overwhelming.Don't make the error of missing out. Pay the extra and visit the tomb - you will never regret it.
Ras Mohammed offers some truly amazing diving with visibility that goes on for miles and stunning corals. It is rich with life. In all, an incredible diving experience.
I liked very much the walls with laces around the windows. Outside. End the view. Inside I liked everything. I felt blessed.I didn't like that at the entrance was a long, long line.

I have to start this review by thanking President Sisi for improving the condition of the Pyramids in Giza, this has nothing to do with politics but where credit is due credit is given. When I visited the Pyramids two years ago, it was chaotic, and there were so many people hustling you for money, tours, camel rides, etc. two policemen inside even asked us for money! Today, there were so many tourists and locals at the site, and it was so orderly and clean. I hope the Egyptian people will preserve this site so people from all over the world can enjoy them.

When you think about Egypt, what pops up to your mind first is its mysterious ancient civilizations. But we should not forget that its modern and contemporary history is really interesting too. This mosque, located at the peak of the Cairo Citadel, has been built to remember Muhammad Ali, the founder of modern Egypt. Its minarets and domes make it a pleasure for your eyes and for... your camera.

I purchased the luxor 5 day pass  with seti 1 and Nefertari  for 160usd.I was allowed to visit Nefertaris tomb once a day for 5 days.If i had visited the tomb on a single ticket the cost would have been 50usd per visit.The sign states that you are only allowed 10 minutes in the tomb,yet i was never told to leave on any visit.Each visit was as enjoyable as the first.It is like walking into a museum of masterpieces.Ramses 2 must of really loved his wife.The guardians expect tips,some speak english and can act as guides and others no next to nothing.One guardian told me to take photos,yet they can report you and a fine could be levied.Some thin air at the bottom of the tomb.Seti 1 tomb is another great attraction.

Awesome temple! It was moved as part of a UNESCO restoration plan, after the Higher ASWAN Dam flood plane was agreed.Great job moving it or it would have been lost to future generations forever! Fabulous representations - Temple internal photo's are currently banned, with great tourist packages available from your guide.

I bought a camera ticket which was well worth the outlay.  Impressive building from the outside but falling into disrepair inside and lacking some displays due to pieces being moved to the new museum.  Still worth the entry, many amazing artefacts from mummies, to Tutankhamen displays and sarcophagus.  Some great statues, furniture and everything else you can imagine.

If you go as part of an organised trip make sure you get there early in the day. The museum opens at 8.45am and we were there till 11am by which time it was really hot inside the building and absolutely packed. Despite having 2 hours in the museum it just felt like a whistle stop tour. Also the golden mask of Tutankhamun was not on display as it is being restored. It also costs extra for the mummies room.

We were suprised to be given a history lesson about the national parks. It wasn't expected but was very interesting. The captain was knowledgeable and it enhanced the journey. One of the swims was rough and children weren't alowed to swim- it's for their own good so listen to him. The food was good and the stops secluded

Okay - we read up on many places of interest before we embarked on our Nile Cruise back in May, but nothing prepared us for this amazing - and almost humbling - experience.   As we arrived, the first thing that hit us was the size of the Temple and once inside it had an almost haunting atmosphere around.   We heeded advice, and took plenty of water with us as it was extremely hot, even for May, and before we knew it we had been walking around for several hours.   We would recommend this tour to anyone and would love to return - it's just one of those places you will never tire of seeing.Thanks to Adam at Tempting Holidays for recommending this "must see" - Fabulous.

The Temple of Kom Ombo is very close to the bank of the Nile, we walked. At 4pm, however, the heat of the sun feels heavy and the movement is unpleasant. Kom Ombo in Arabic means pile of gold. I don't know how much gold was here in antiquity. The city, however, remained famous for its temple. The temple was built during the Ptolemaic period, between 180 and 47 BC and is totally atypical compared to the others built before it. It is a double temple, built ”in a mirror”. Along the east-west axis, on either side, there are the same number of rooms, corridors. The southern part of the temple is dedicated to God Sobek, the crocodile god, god of fertility and creator of the world along with two other deities, Hathor and Khonsu, while the northern part is dedicated to God Haroeris who is none other than Horus the Elder. A lot of things the guide told us in front of the reliefs in different areas of the temple, but not being familiar with Egyptian mythology not much remain in my memory. However, there are two special aspects here that I remembered: on one of the walls of the temple there is a relief with the calendar of festivals. Based on it, it was possible to identify how the ancient Egyptians kept the calendar and how they marked important events. On another wall is a relief depicting a surgical kit. The tools out there aren't very different from today's ... or perhaps it would have been more appropriate to say the opposite.

The museum it’s mostly been replaced by a newer museum and most of the mommies are moved. It has many valuable pieces of history but the place could have been better cleaned and weather proof. There is no paper in the restrooms and many women waiting for a tip from you to provide you what should be inside.

Mount Sinai is quite high at 7497 ft. When Moses climbed up to received the Ten Commandments, he was around 80 years old. In present day not many 80 year old can climb up there. Even with  camels helping half of the way, you still have few hundred steps, but the experience is yours and yours alone and it will last for a lifetime.Happy Travelling!

It has one of the best collection of artifacts belonging to Pharonic era. It is next to Tharir Square which is one of the well know area for public gathering. Just near the Museum a burnt building belonging to a political party and also an Army tank with few army men can be seen, indicating that the country is still undergoing change and unrest. Inside the museum there is humongous collection of artifacts and you can spend minimum if 4 hrs or more depending on your interest. Our tour guide explained most important exhibits and the first floor artifacts of the well known King Tutankamun are kept.Entry to the Royal Mummies room is separately chargeable, since I came across few mummies outside the room we did not opt for it.
It made loads of difference to use a local tour guide from the city of Aswan, they know the history of every piece of stone.

Karnak temple in Luxor is a must see; built over a 2000 year timespan by various dynasties of Pharaohs who added to the already existing structures to tag onto their immortality. Ram statues lining the entrance, hundreds of carved columns and obelisks some with original colours.In a word -AWESOME.
Great restaurants bars & shops. A "must see" place when in Sharm El Sheik, drink in the athmosphere whilst watching the dancing fountains. Quite magical!

Years ago we didn't visit the catacombs along the Old Appian Way south of Rome. So this became our big chance to see catacombs firsthand.  Quite intriguing, these burial chambers, because the painted friezes and sculpted bas reliefs depict Egyptian deities during the Christian era.  Also the artwork combines symbols of Egyptian, Roman and Greek cultures.  On opposing sides of the exit doorway, the Egyptian jackal god of mummification, Anubis, stands guard wearing the garb of a Roman legionnaire. At the burial chamber entrance two Greek snakes of good luck, Agathodaimon, stand guard.  But on their heads they wear the Egyptian double crown and in their hands they hold a kerkeions, or winged staffs.  They also hold staffs topped by the Greek topped by the Greek pinecone symbol.
