While above their heads sits an orb of the Greek goddess, Medusa, warding off ill-doers.

We hired a driver and guide from the reception of our hotel (Le Meridien Giza) and can honestly say we had no problems at all. My husband went into the pyramid with the guide (way too claustrophobic for me I'm afraid) and I waited at the foot of the pyramid alone and was not hassled at all. Once I was joined by my husband the street sellers started to approach us but a shake off the head and friendly smile was enough to see them off!I can't see how you can visit Egypt and not see the pyramids - they really did take my breath away when I first saw them.
Fantastic, such an impressive size, so interesting. Especially the holes in the side of the rocks where the workers used to live, shading from the heat.

It includes the bent pyramid and others nearby. Its on a dry and dusty section of desert but very impressive to see and not to be missed if youre in the Cairo area. It is one of the least visited places with pyramids so you wont have crowds of people.
Cairo Tower is a nice place where you can have a nice view of Cairo and enjoy a snack, a beer, or a meal.  If you sit at table 7, you can see the pyramids of Giza in the distance.

Its such a beauty to see a church built on top of a fortress. This relic place is amazing and I must see. It is very holy so please be discreet with pictures and other people praying. You can feel the good vibes in there.

If you like ancient times, you will love this place. We had a nice time here. The only issue is exit. If you want to go out, you have to pass a local market, which is awful. Security won’t let you out through normal gate, because then locals won’t earn a dollar for useless stuff.

Picked up from the hotel in Sharm at 2130 to get to the monastery ready to start climbing about 1am. Our group had a really nice Bedouin guide who spoke good English and was full of information. It's a nice walk up the mountain with coffee shops every 1km or so to rest. Though if you're not very fit you might find it a struggle. Annoying things are the camel guys following 2 foot behind you the whole way asking "you want camel?" every 2 minutes and the amount of litter. Plastic bottles and sweet wrappers everywhere. There are bins all the way up so use them people! The view from the top at sunrise is spectacular. An orange glow slowly appearing over the mountains followed by a fantastic sun rise. The walk back down is easier in day light but it gets very hot very quickly so take suncream and plenty of water. There are toilets at most of the coffee shops but they are very basic long drops.

The highlight of our trip! To actually get to see these was amazing. To think that these have been here for over 200 years and are still in really great condition. They are the only thing you can see from space too.  I have always dreamed of ascending to the top of the pyramids, but you are not allowed to. So if you are like me and want to climb up them-be forewarned. You can't. I even offered to pay them and have one of the Egypt people carry my bag to help me but they were having none of it. However, as I had travelled a long way and was hot, my sandals and socks were full of sand and   my  food rations were getting low I thought that I could sneak around the back and then climb up without anyone looking. Well, you can forget that because these things are much larger than they look from far away. Also, the Egyptor people are very friendly and although they can't speak English really good they are happy to try. It is funny to hear them speak with their weird accents and my wife and me had a good laugh at them. The final thing which I had wished I had known was that, if you dont know already-the guys all wear white dresses which is hilarious! I asked one of the Eygpy men what does he wear underneath it and he didn't like me saying that. My wife then. reminded me that I was thinking of Scotland men and their kilts. That was my bad.

It was amazing seeing the collection of treasures found in King Tut's tomb. Also, the Mummy Room where you can see the mummies of kings and queens was also fascinating, although you do have to pay extra for it.

The boat trip to the temple in the middle of the Nile is refreshing and you get to see a lot of the little rocky islands as you pass by. The temple itself is big and incredible! Definitely worth the visit. There is also a little restaurant where you can relax and have a coke or an ice cream.

This is one of the absolute MUST VISIT places when you're in Cairo. You can easily spend a day walking around inside and still not have time to see all the displays in detail. The unique thing to do is visit after sunset when the fare is slightly different but the crowds are far lesser and the contents of the museum sort of come alive with nightfall!Do remember that as with every other place in Egypt, you will have to watch out for hustlers, fake "guides" and people offering information about tickets/hours at the entrance. There are guides who are officially authorized to take you in for anything between 200-300 EGP. In my opinion, the guide we had was too full of his own accomplishments and spoke more about himself than the rich history of Egypt. It was not worth paying so much if you have the time to research the floor plan, read up on the different kingdoms and carry a Lonely Planet or similar book with you. The guide is only useful if you would like to observe smaller nuances such as folklore, reading a Kartoush, a particular missing piece in the display, etc.Do try and visit more than once to cover all floors and exhibits. The tickets are exorbitantly priced for outsiders compared to locals but you will have to buy extra tickets to the Mummy Rooms if you'd like to see the Royal Mummies. All in all, a definite attraction for tourists and travelers alike but there is definite scope for the authorities to do so much more with the place (lacks eve basics such as display tags, cordoning off from people touching exhibits, condition of signage, etc.)

It was my first night in Cairo and I wanted to get the lane of the land so booked a tour for the Cairo Tower at night. I met Michael in the lobby of my hotel here he had a sine with my name on it.  So we met the driver out side and we were off to the Cairo Tower he gave me a bottle of water which was sweetIt was every cool because it was close to sundown and you can see a good sunset and see most of the city.Next we went to the Nile river to take a nice sale boat ride which is was just what I needed because I was jet lagged. We went down and back blown the river. You can see city lights.Then we went to this Mountain I forgot what it is called. You can see a grate view of the city from the top of the hill. Michael explains everything thoroughly. Which is nice to learn how to town thinks.Next stop we got some local food let was so good I could have had more Michael ate with me which was nice because I am in Cairo solo. Next we went to the market. All and all is was a grate time seeing the city at night.  I would ask for Michael.
The temple gets very busy, if possible arrive as soon as possible after it opens at 6am and there will be only a few people there.
I found the museum to be a little disorganized when we first arrived, but as we moved deeper into the museum, I completely lost myself in the artifacts and relics

The boat trip to the temple was itself enjoyable & very entertaining, with 'SWIMMER-SELLERS' swimming upto the boat offerring items for sale or singing for a tip.Once we arrived, we were greeted with by a small number of market traders that were NOT too pushy, so we could browse with ease.The temple tour was very interesting (We cheated by following another random paid guide to evesdrop on the rich history).After relaxing in the coffee shop area with luxury ice cream, we felt very satisfied with the entire trip & LOOKED FORWARD to the water entertainments by the 'Swimmers' and returned with fond memories of the day.
We offered our Friday prayers here, listened to the most beautiful recitation of the quran, the atmosphere was great and its right next to Khan Al Khalili.

Visiting any of the pyramids is fun, but this one has an interesting history. There is a temple that you enter to get to the site, which in my opinion is beautiful inside for pics. The terrain is dirt with rocks. There are some stairs to climb once you are on site if you want to view the whole area, but not necessary. It can be very sunny so you may want to wear a hat and sunscreen.

This beautiful, monumental temple complex had been inundated by the lake at it's original location. It was dismantled and rebuilt on this island. The scenic boat ride was included in our excursion and provides some great views of this must see site. We also bought some beautiful Nubian jewelry and a shawl from the vendors at each end of the boat ride-some of the best deals of our trip.

We visited the Colossi of Memnon as part of our Egyptian tour. There is no cost to enter and there are markets in the car park.They are two massive stone statues of the Pharaoh Amenhotep III, who reigned in Egypt during the Dynasty XVIII. Since 1350 BCE, they have stood in the Theban Necropolis, located west of the River Nile from the modern city of Luxor.

We walked to the Komombo Temples from our dock with just about 45 mins here as it closed by 7pm and our cruise sets sail again at 7.30pm.This is a temple of 2 gods – Horus (Falcon) and Sobek (Crocodile). Very impressive open concept structure not quite like the ancient Egypt ones we’ve seen in Abu Simbel despite some similarities.The carvings are very intricate, depicting the king’s coronation, the calendar (made up of 12 months) including the 3 seasons (flood, agriculture and harvesting), surgical tools of that time and extensive coverage of the Sobek God, essentially a god with the crocodile head and used to protect the worshippers from crocodiles.Here we also saw ancient measuring system in the form of a deep well which they used to determine high and low tides of the Nile with markings by the sides of the tide meter.Entrance fees apply and includes entry to the Crocodile Museum just next to it.
Very good collection of ancient Egyptian relics including King Tut and many mummies. take a guide to help you navigate

Seeing the pyramids has been a long-cherished dream for me – now we have been there, and I am still feeling the wonder and awe of the moment. These wonders of the ancient world are even more impressive in “real life“ than I imagined them to be …During our stay in Cairo, Khaled Abdo was our guide, and I cannot recommend him enough. There are so many guides in Giza/Cairo, so I am even more happy that we found him! Already in the communication before the trip I had a good feeling: he completely listened to our individual wishes and ideas and came up with many valuable suggestions due to his experience. We had booked Khaled for two days and long days they were: we started in the morning and went on until we didn't need him anymore - or rather: were exhausted by the many new impressions. This means that you don't tick off the five agreed places from the coordinated plan as quickly as possible and then call it a day; if it takes longer, that's okay, and if something else comes up on the way or a reprise makes sense in the evening (see above), that's included.Khaled is highly knowledgeable and explains everything well in very good English. Sometimes, however, he talks unusually little for a guide, i.e. you should and must ask if you want to go deeper somewhere. This suited my husband and me very well, to look around in peace and quiet, without being constantly told what to do. He is also not someone who sees himself as an entertainer and has to make jokes, but rather a sober, friendly guy.With Khaled there are no "obligatory stops" in shops, workshops etc. for a hidden kickback; there are also no requests to buy anything, no more or less discreet requests to tip or add to the agreed fee. On the contrary, he warned us where the surcharges for tourists are particularly high and where we should not buy anything; he preferred to drive us to other shops with fair fixed prices, if we wanted.As a private, independent guide, he may not be the very cheapest option, but he is certainly worth every cent of the money. In Khaled you have an independent, reliable guide with integrity, who can take you to all the places you want to go in Cairo and always has interesting stories to tell. As he works as a self-employed guide and according to Egyptian law, Khaled has to buy a licence for each guest he guides. He pays the costs for these licences in advance with every booking; if the guest cancels the tour at short notice, he is left with these costs. Therefore, it is important to be really sure when booking that you are really going to be in Giza or Cairo on the day you have in mind and that you really want to do the tour. If the plans are so firm, you can only congratulate yourself on being guided by him. And we are already looking forward to another trip - there is still so much more to discover!Khaled can be reached under his numbers (002)01227448749 or (002)01156881389.

The "bent" pyramid shows you the Egyptian's attempts at building pyramids prior to the smooth, straight-sided pyramids of Giza. The best part about this site? We got to go INSIDE the pyramid, crouching down almost 30 meters inside to the bottom, before walking up a wooden staircase to see the tomb from mid-level. This was SUPER fun and neat to do! Can be a little claustrophobic for some, as the oxygen gets much thicker the further you walk in. Pace yourself! It's definitely worth the visit.

This mosque is one of the largest I've ever been to. It's up on a hill like a fortress with a remarkable view in all directions of Cairo. You can see the pyramids from here. It's breathtaking in its beauty of Islamic design and patternwork.

This is a must see for any visit to Egypt. I would highly recommend seeing these exquisite exhibits before they move to their new home in the Giza area. The building itself is historical and in downtown Cairo which made it very convenient for me since I was staying at the Ramses Hilton. I walked back to the hotel dodging the traffic nearly the whole time. My sister jokingly said that I ran like an Egyptian!!!  King Tut's mask was stunning and breathtaking along with his other finery. I walked thru the museum on my own but I think it would have been better with a tour guide to provide the significant and little known details that most of us do not know.

The museum was amazing. They have so much artifacts that they're running out of room (they are building a new museum in Giza). Sometimes you would see a statue/artitfact casually tucked somewhere (like storage) between other artifacts-- it felt casual, not like a formal don't-touch-this-or-cross-that type of museum, although many things are in glass cases. King Tut's solid gold mask (and his other belongings) were the highlight (for me) in this museum. We had an Egyptologist who accompanied us, which made the visit much more comprehensive and enjoyable. NOTE: If you want to see the "mummies", you have to pay extra-- but they meant the "naked" mummies, the ones without the wrappings. If you're ok with the wrapped-up mummies, don't bother paying for the extra fee; you'll see plenty of those.

No thig can be compared in Egypt to this Temple it's very huge very wonderful, you feel that you are walking on the same lands old Egyptians walked on, the design is wonderful, you will just need a good guide to explain everything to you.

The “Pyramids of Giza” are a once in a lifetime better in person experience. No question it is worth seeing. Here is what you need to know. Note when they drop you off you do not need a camel ride you can take the 10 minute walk up the hill.  Only get your tickets at the entrance!!You don’t need a guide but if you aren’t part of a tour you will want to pay a guide there.  If not you will get hounded the whole time. Note bargain for your guides between $5 to $10 bucks to show you around. Also bargain for the camel rides around the pyramids $10 to $20 per person.  The camel ride is worth it!! The pyramids are only a 20 minute ride from Cairo!

The Temple of Karnac is said to be the world's religious building, apart from Anchor Wat in Cambodia. The first thing one notices are the massive columns, which are a feature of the main temple. Not all of the temple is open to the public. The Temple of Karnac is not all that far from Luxor; it is one of the most popular sites remaining from ancient Egypt.

You can go to Howard Carters house and see the newly constructed replica of the real tomb, but if not busy why settle for second best. Ok it will cost you EGP100 to enter and it is very small, but you will always be able to say you have been inside. Then when you see all the exhibits in Luxor and Cairo museums from the tomb they will mean so much moor. The guard pointed things out to us with his torch and we were happy to press a few EGP in his hand on the way out
Another temple, but this is outstanding and wortth the effort. Take your time walking around. Ensure you have a good guide wth you who can explain the significance od the site

Other than the men who would dress up and sneak into photos trying to get you to tip them, this place was really cool.  The weather was perfect when I went in mid January.  Low to mid 70's so it wasn't too hot.  Definitely a must see if you are in Luxor.

It was amazing to see how this temple was right under the giant cliffs. We saw some archeologist working in/around the temple. We took the little train in because it was very hot out. Luckily when we were there there was basically no one there, so we were able to explore the area casually, but we weren’t there long. When we left, there’s buses of school children coming in. Whew, just missed them!

Beautiful, small tomb that depicts the ceremony of the opening of the mouth and has Tutankamon's coffin in place. The ochre colour and the baboons are beautiful.  We visited in April. We were the first tourists to arrive around 900 am. We visited for maybe 45 minutes and nobody else went inside. We really enjoyed it. There are parts that are unfinished perhaps, due to the fact that king died at a very young age and had to be buried fast. In the past, people spent hours in line to go inside the tomb; you don't have to do that now, reason why I think this is the best time to visit Egypt.

Took a 3:30am caravan (7 vehicles) from Aswan and was at the sight at 7:00am. We were the only visitor. The site is amazing and the story behind it is impressive. Guide told us that there used to be 6000-7000 tourist a day at Abu Simbel, now, lucky to have 50 a day...sad for the locals. The site is worth the drive.
Excellent condition, with restoration still ongoing.  Interesting history of Horus.  Accessible from Nile by horse drawn carriage. The shops have very aggressive sales people.  But, we felt safe.

The citadel has long since given up any military function. Today it houses a small naval museum, but it might be worth a visit to explore the inside of the fortress and imagine the huge structure that once stood on its foundation.The peninsula leading to the citadel is also a popular area with fishermen and families alike. It is usually crowded with a pleasant crowd enjoying the sea views, restaurants and ice cream shops that line the street up to the fortress.

To see one of the world's wonders right before your eyes was really an amazing experience. There are 3 of them, and if you go with a guide and a transportation, ask them to go around so that you'll be able to see all 3. The area is pretty big.TIP:1. Most people will only enjoy the pyramids and then nothing else, right? Here's a new thing to try. When you're right in front of the pyramid, try to look behind you and go towards the edge (be careful, though), you'll see breathtaking landscape of Cairo!!2. Be careful of those guys with camels. They will offer you camel rides and taking pictures with the camels and they can be VERY persistent. Some of them will try to get in your picture and then ask you for some cash afterwards. Also, if you ask them to help you take a picture, they won't do it for free.

I feel churlish giving this incredible institution anything less than 5 stars, but it is undergoing a multi-year move to the new facility in Giza, so is not at its best.  Many of the acclaimed masterpieces are on display, but much has already been packed up.  Of course, even at its best, the museum is subject to very fair criticism for its lack of adequate signage.Still, it is an absolute must see in Cairo.Most visitors will want to know if the famous mask of Tutankhamen is on view. Yes, along with a small selection of artifacts, two of his coffins, his famous lion chair, but the many, many rooms of artifacts ordinarily on display are gone.The artifacts from the tomb of Yuya and Thuya, one of my favorite exhibits from an earlier visit are now occupying much of the hall where Tut's artifacts were formerly shown.Many celebrated masterpieces - the Narmer palette, Rehotep and Mopet, Khephren, etc. remain in place but during my two visits (about 5 hours each, 3 days apart), I could see signs of more items being packed up.  Between the two days, Tut's mask had been moved to a different room, and the treasures of Tanis were not on view but apparently a new room was being prepared for them, so they might now be on view.Visitors should be aware of the transition, and the usual caveats about the museum - huge, not very clean (nor are the bathrooms), lack of elevators, can be insanely crowded (and then sometimes not), but at least a short guided tour of 2 hours minimum to cover the downstairs west wing, and Tut, Yuya and Thuya upstairs is an essential must do in Cairo, at least until the new museum is open.

I have traveled a lot but the emotion you feel being in this place has no words to describe this true heritage of humanity. At every hour of the day and night, every glance at every moment stops your breath. A unique spectacle and not comparable to anything else. How wonderful!!!!

Luxor is full of history and amazing things we greet you at every turn. Karnak Temple is awe inspiring and you can get lost wandering around and taking it all in. Don’t rush - there is so much to see and appreciate! Including graffiti from more ‘modern’ history.

Ok so here's the deal, the pyramids are freaking amazing, everyone knows that. With that said it's very easy to get caught up in the pageantry of the whole thing and spend waaaaaaaaay too much money. First thing: go with a game plan. Once past security (they don't allow pocket knives by the way but were nice enough to hold my knife for me until I finished) you will be swarmed by "official tourism guides", and they are, hired by the tourism department to make money. I definitely suggest booking a tour PTA (prior to arrival) to avoid spending up to 2000 Egyptian Lyra or $200 US. Half day trips on camel can be arranged for less than $100. The views will be spectacular and you will enjoy yourself. Also be aware of them including privately owned "museums" along the way, they will without a doubt be a way to get you to spend up to double on merchandise available elsewhere. Be sure and tip your guide, most operate purely off tips, and their bosses may even dip into that if you mention what you tipped your guide. Second, going into the big pyramid, it's offered at the ticket office for 200 Lira. From experience, climbing into this enormous iconic archeological structure is amazing and overwhelming. Just know, at the destination, the burial chamber, all that is left is a stone sarcophagus, devoid of any of the gems that were once there. The climb is easy (if you're in good shape) various parts are tall enough to only crawl. Overall great experience, if you go without a guide you WILL be hounded and molested by people offering their services...for a price.
Approached only by a boat ride. You also come across the Nubian people and some of their art work. Must visit.
Take time to really get immersed in the exhibits at the mini  museum at the entrance to Saqqara.  It will make your visit more interesting.
The visit of the Museum was part of the 1 day tour from Eilat Israel. the museum is absolutely must.

You don’t need this review to understand why King Tut is a must see.  Well worth your time after seeing the many numerous documentaries.  I would never recommend this elsewhere but ask the chamber guard to take a few pictures for you.  After spending days, and years inside they have some unique photo shoots.  Just ask.

Photos of Tut's mask are no where near the exquisite artifacts themselves.  The beds carved as lions, hippos and cows are so delightful.  But best of all were the alabaster collection of sweet cubs, donkey and canopic jars, lions, cows and hippos. Seeing Nofret's face up close was such a treat!  I didn't feel the least bit frightened as the workers who discovered her were said to have reacted.
Expect to need about 2 hours here (at minimum) to properly see everything. You will probably need 3 hours if you want to go down into the pyramids.

The temple of Hathor contains two images of the famous Cleopatra and her son by Ceasar, Ceasarion in magnificent relief. The Ptolomies, obviously keen to legitimise their dynasty with the local people, undertook the restoration of a much more ancient site. The colours still stand out today, much the same as the colour scheme you see during the day of everyday life. Hathor used to visit her husband Horus in Edfu, the story given by your required guide will flesh out the details. My partner (oh no not another temple) type person was taken in and entranced by it,  as he called her "Hathor was a party girl". The tourists as sparse, so you get to enjoy it with less crowds. Make sure you do tip the guardians who show you sealed off areas, they are truly mysterious. The disappointing part was after going up to the roof where the goddess would be taken to take in the sun (that stairwell is also amazing) - we were shown teh famous Dendera zodiac. Disappointing because it is a plaster cast of teh original that now sits in the Louvre. I know we can't change history but it would be a nice gesture to Egypt for the French to return the stolen goods, and make some amends. I am sure Hathor wants that back please! Maintenant, s'il vous plait!Seriously, get there, wander, check out the visitors and help the Egyptian people, they are proud of their past. Have lots of small change, take a camera and just check out the images of Cleopatra. They must have loved her.
Built in 1370 BC. These statues are huge. Just sitting out on their own. You can stop in and just go look. There is no fee.
The scope and scale are huge. TV and photos cannot do them justice. An experience of a life time! If you have the chance, see them.
A must see in Alexandria.  The views of the ocean are spectacular.  It's a very interesting fort and I recommend going with a guide to tell you the history.  A wonderful spot for artsy photos
Mosque was very interesting lovely peaceful building architecture was fantastic pleasing on the eye highly recommended to visit

The island contains the temple and many other constructions and excellent views plus the incredible boat ride to get there .. to get there you need a taxi to the port (about 150 Egyptian pounds), pay the entrance fee (60 euros), and The transfer by boat (50 ep / per pax with 3 people sharing the boat). The temple itself is a marvel of history. Of the things to see in life.
Impressive and awesome just to walk around the vast area full of statues and pillars of a past civilization.The imperfections that time has weathered on them only adds to the realness of it all.
These are a must see!  Words cannot describe the feeling of awe inspired by these ancient world wonders!  To be able to actually climb the lower reaches, walk all around them...breathtaking!!!

Did about 6 dives including Yolanda spot and many others. Don't miss it. And even if you don't dive go there for snorkeling...a fabulous place! You can check here a 2min video I edited from diving in Sharm el sheikh including Ras Mohamed.https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=xcUArmcODJ0

Well this was the common version of this queens name. This was one of the most impressive sites to date on our visit. From the moment you see it as you drive up it looks massive. This is what I expected I suppose as there are numerous steps leading up to the second and third levels and looks like something out of a film set. Again the carvings are incredible. Definately one to see.

This place was so peaceful. We were the only one's there with our guide. Paid 10 EGP for a 5 min boat ride which was cool. So sad that all of the Tourist are not coming. This place was beautiful and we felt very safe. Glad we checked this place out. I would recommend it to travellers.

This was part of a Luxor trip that we went on and it was one of the most interesting sights. Partly because its magnificent and well restored but also because of the story/legend behind it.Briefly, Hapshetsut ruled ancient Egypt for 20 years disguised as a man until her stepson overthrew her and destroyed her temples and figure heads. This temple had been fully restored and is glorious as well as being full of history.This place is a real gem and a must see if your in Luxor
Beautiful Temple, so much fun to tske a boat and reach this lovely place with amazing views. A must see while in Aswan.

Entrance to the Mohamed Ali Mosque was included in our tour of the old city of Cairo. I had never been inside a mosque before and I found it quite beautiful inside and out. The mosque is located on a hill and from the grounds outside there is a wonderful view of the city.

This is the place where time began.. you could wonder around Karnak temple for days and never get bored. If you’re short on time I’d suggest the night time sound and light show, buy your tickets at the temple as your travel company will charge around £22 and they only cost £5.I doubt anyone could fail to be awestruck by this incredible sight, prepare to travel back in time and experience the opulence and splendour of ancient Egypt.

The twin temples of Kom Ombo are a nice sight on the banks of the Nile. As this was my second time visiting this temple, my guide took me to parts of the temple that I had not seen before. This was aided by the fact that there were only a few visitors on the day in January. Most of the visit, I walked around by myself which any hassle. In the late afternoon, the light makes the stones glow a beautiful dull golden-orange. An easy place to visit and well worth an hour of visiting time.  An interesting point is that that Temple of Kom Ombo features in the original Death on the Nile movie as it is used as the entrance to Karnak.
I really enjoyed this temple. We stopped around sunset which made for some beautiful pictures. It was neat being able to see the temple from our river cruise ship and it was a short walk to visit.
Probably the best Egyptian museum in the world! Seeing the Tutankhamun mask up close is worth the ticket alone. And there's plenty more. An absolute must see in Egypt.
This place is wonderful to view both day and night.  If you are in Luxor, you must see the awesomeness of this place.

I mean, they are Sphinxes and there's not much to do other than view the Sphinxes.  However, it's beautiful! There's so many of them and it just makes you picture what the temple would look like back in the day!
Defiantly place to pay attention !!! Recently has opens . If you like to ticklish your nerves it is place to be))))))

We went in December with nice weather and good sun for photographs in the afternoon.  Crowds were light and the sellers are kept to the main entrance.  Best line on our way out was a seller saying "I can smell your money."  We had to stop for a laugh!  A tram takes you from the entrance to the main walking path up.  Take note of the poor engineer's tomb killed by Hatshepsut after completing her temple so he could't outdo it later.  Tough lady!  Well worth a stop and with the light crowds you can get some great photos without strangers in them or a ton of photoshop processing.  We went with a private guide which helped us absorb the history and ask questions about specific topics.  Our two young boys enjoyed the stop as well.
The Pyramids loomed enormous in all their magnanimity throughout the day, looking far more spectacular in the sunshine. The Pyramids' stones each weigh two to three tons!
A truly amazing place. So much to see.Needs a bit of updating but totally  worth a visit.Can get very  busy so expect to queue to get in.

A must see. You can't really appreciate the magnitude of the pyramids until you see them.They are also maintaining the site well.  The area is clean. The areas for the horse and camel rides is well organized. No animal waste was present around the pyramids when I was there. There are park security officers to maintain the security for the tourists.Wear comfortable shoes and bring a camera.I highly recommend the camel ride.
spent 2 days diving here deserves its reputation as one of the best five dive sites in the world so much to see whether diving or snorkelling a must if you visit sharm
there are 3 Camps where you can spend the night in there tents and wake up in front of the sea. it's really great

The “Citadel” as it’s known by locals, should be on a visitor’s Cairo itinerary. The place offers great views of old Cairo and of the Cairo skyline. The mosque itself is stunning and worth the visit for the architecture marvel it is. While the place is centrally located in Cairo, it’s not served by public transportation. So, one’s options are either to take a private tour with a tour operator or take an Uber/Taxi. I would suggest the former since it’ll offer you access to parts of the mosque that are not open to the general public. Moreover, if you are a non-Egyptian citizen, you will pay a much higher entrance fee at the gate, and most likely would be asked to pay “bribes” to the local police and groundbreaking crew. Luckily, we were with our Egyptian relatives so we were not subjected to that treatment. While the place is absolutely worth a day trip, one need to plan ahead to make it a memorable visit.
Visited this coral reef via a glass bottom catamaran. My husband snorkelled in this area. The reef is beautiful with an abundance of amazing see life and coral. An amazing area a must to visit.

Our Nile cruise docked around 6:00 pm at Kom Ombo where we walked short distance to the temple.  The temple looks beautiful under the yellow lights which added more to the grace of the place.  Wonderful structures full of beauty, Amazing columns and drawings which makes you wonder how the ancient Egyptians managed to do all this with the degree of accuracy considering the simple manual tools they had at that time.  A worth visit place.
Amazing color, and detail in the walls. Great place to visit and understand about Egypt civilization. Be ready to take great photos of this.

We scuba dived every day from a large boat and this is the best way to see Ras Mohamed national park area. There is so much life and so many colourful scenes underwater we emerged from the water with a grin on our faces after every dive.
Similar to a European Castle, the Citadel is definitely worth a visit. It houses an interesting police museum and several beautiful mosques.

We visited with the kids. There is a lot to see and learn.  Lots and lots of ancient collections. After going through the museum and taking photos I felt the visit would have been more productive if we had taken a tour guide to give us a summary of the museum. All we did was walk around and take photos so I guess we missed the stories around most collections. It’s difficult to read all the cards on each collection. The tour was good though and we enjoyed it

Giza pyramids are a must see. Arriving there early is the best since it’s desert.A short camel ride is a great idea for fun pictures band to just fully experience the pyramid experience.I paid 100 pounds extra ($6.35) to enter the second pyramid. You climb down steps underground, then up some steps to a burial chamber. I felt it was worth the money even through there aren’t carvings inside.

The catacombs here are interesting but, definitely not like other catacomb sites around the world. There are no human bones to see, just walking in and viewing the areas of burial. There are some steps to go inside and the flooring all dirt and can be some small rocks, so wear comfortable, sturdy footwear. They say no pics inside.

What can you say about one of the seven wonders of the world - spectacular.  To fully enjoy it, you have to do some up-front work though - don't just show up and expect it to be like a tour of the Statue of Liberty.  If you are not arabic speaking you should invest in a tour guide ahead of time - even if they just get you into the complex and help you get your bearings and layout.  Like any tourist attraction there are a lot of people there waiting to sell you tours, trinkets, and anything else for dollars.  A guide will at least make sure you get the right tickets and point out the pitfalls and give you a bearing on the prices of things if you do want to buy souvenirs or take camel rides.Try to go into at least one of the pyramids its a great experience - there is a limit on the number of people allowed in each day so get there early to do this.Camel rides are also fun - 15 - 30 min, they take you out a couple of hundred yards, take pictures and bring you back.  The view from the back side is better since you are on the side with the smaller pyramids.  We got a ride for $10 USD per person for 7 of us after negotiating down from the original price of $20/pp.Right now is a great time to go, since tourism is down and the dollar is very strong against the EGP.  We only spent 2 hours there as part of a group tour, but could have easily done a day, we did not go near the sphinx, the boats (2) or the excavation sites - set aside a whole day for this activity and do it leisurely - make sure to bring water with you.  Even if the temperature is moderate - the arid air in the desert can dry you out quickly - if you find yourself getting a headache for no reason its probably an initial sign of dehydration.I will definitely come back anytime I am near this part of the world.

This place is a must see although it took a while to get to, around 4-5 hours from Hurghada. However it was amazing to see all the ancient monuments and hieroglyphics still intact. Make sure you have your cameras/phones charged as this place is very picturesque. Our tour operator didn't let us roam around for too long which was annoying. During warm weather essentials would be sun cream, water bottle and sun glasses. Power bar to charge your phone for this trip is essential as well. I was in awe most of the time with the architecture and it was quite mind blowing thinking how the Egyptians assembled this place and others, which I am still baffled.

Not the biggest nor the best decorated tomb in the Valley of the Kings, but probably the best known, and one that you just have to see. Contains the mummified body of Tutankhamun  - the sarcophagi are in the Cairo museum along with 'the mask', another must see.

OH MY GOD!!!! I knew they were big - but nothing prepared me for how gigantic they are. I can understand why people think they were built by aliens!  How did they do it???? It was a tremendous thrill to see them, and touch them and climb on them.
In the National museum you can find so many historic statues. It is quite big, so be prepared to walk a lot there. No cameras allowed inside.

The artefacts which could have been looted from Egypt are best kept here. A half-day visit doesn't disappoint unless you're an art buff that you deserve the whole day to appreciate the archaeological remains, statues and history.The entrance is approximately 4 USD; however, hire a guide to better understand everything that you see. An hour costs 150 GP that is roughly 8.50 USD. In fact, one hour is ridiculous - It wouldn't cover much inside, though two hours would bereasonable. It is forbidden to take photos of the Royal Mummies and The Golden Mask in the room of the treasure of Tutankhamun.Meanwhile, some guards are rude when requesting your camera tickets for taking photos in the halls. They sound demanding with accusing eyes making you feel like you've done a terrible act in the open...and when you show them your ticket, they appear nonchalant and I find it offensive. The government should scrutinize these people as they tarnish the reputable image of the Egyptian Museum. The Museum staff should be decent, good-natured and welcoming.

To say that the treasures in this museum will overwhelm you is an understatement! We arrived at the museum prior to its opening and had 1/2 hour to explore the treasures outside the museum.  There are so many ancient treasures to look at, it is best to give yourself several trips to digest it all.We did get a photo pass from the "photo office"; no one ever looked at it though.  King Tut's golden treasures were intriguing.  We purchased separate tickets to the mummy room, but could have easily skipped it.  If you have students, make sure they bring their student id as there is a discount for students if an id is provided (ours did not).

we are grateful that are trip to the giza pyramids happend prior to the revolution scary things for the peaceful people of cairo...we decided to go on guided tours so that we could enjoy our visit in safety. we chose egyptian daily tours where owner operator hamdy was our host we dont usually go with a tour guide but we thought we should language barriers and such.we had a fantastic time at the giza pyramids man oh man what a place peace history and just a feeling of power.hamdy was an inspiration to me this young amn knows his country and its history way to go hamdy we so look forward to recommending you and your company to our friends.the only down fall we had on our trip was we did not stay long enough next time two weeks for sure..

Go now before the crowds get bigger. Egypt is totally safe and the tourists are returning in larger and larger numbers. Hire a local guide of Cairo for the very best experience. Feel free to contact me for a recommendation on the licensed guide we used.

Awesome and a must see. We had the best guide ever. I booked him through getyourguide.com. His name was Akram. What a fabulous experience it was to view one of the seven wonders of the world with someone as knowledgeable as he was. He shared his knowledge and his incredible passion so generously. This made the trip alive and fun.
This museum has way too many awesome things on display. So do remember to prioritize where you want to spend time. I spent half a day here and still had lots to cover.
It was great, beautiful view of the sea with that breeze of water , the only thing it's a bit expensive. But it's only for science and the view.

A childhood dream come true.To see with your own eyes this true wonder of the ancient work is a thing of dreams.To stand at the foot of the great pyramids of Giza is simply out of this world.7000 year old history.The only down side is the number of people who pester you for money or to ride a camel. This became quite uncomfortable at one point.I would recommend taking a guide in a taxi from your hotel.
Nubian village was a wow experience, looking at the way they live ,well worth a visit while in Aswan.
Not too busy here so plenty of room to explore. Well described by the guide. Good café for snacks and refreshments.
Loved the trip to Ras Mohamed only down side you have to keep up with proffesional divers so if your not a strong swimmer is a bit of a pain.
What a fan back story for only pharoh queen. A dazzling view over luxor . So sad earthquake damagedcy but can still get idea of how looked in prime.
if you enjoy seeing magnificent and beautiful architecture, this place is a must see. If you are a history buff, this place is a must see.

We had an excursion to Abu Simbel as a part of our Viking Nile pharaohs and pyramids cruise.It is an amazing site!  It wasn’t too crowded and we were able to see the temples, take ample photographs and appreciate the engineering marvel that it is.It’s not easy to get to, but if you have the opportunity to visit, definitely go!

I have some mobility issues so I didn't go in but my husband did.  It is quite a hike to the temple before ascending the multi levels of stairs of the temple itself.  I stayed in the cafe in the parking lot and had an ice cold coka light.  Husband said second level had some nice paintings.  Men!
The old design for the building of Islamic history more beautiful than we see of the New mosques , this mosques looks very beautiful
Breath taking place.Ceiling full of amazing drawings,Window Glass colored and drawings in an astonishing way.Deserve an hour at least for visit and meditation.

This is a necesssary stop by you need to do on your way. Very nice and imposing colosals. Very nice place to take a break and get inspired by this gigant structures. Really nice and recommended to go.
You would think after visiting so many of the ancient sites that you won't possibly be impressed, but each and every time...they are incredible! A must see!

The statue was in a building on the grounds of the ancient capital Memphis. It was very impressive as was the sphinx with the face of Hatshepsut.  Of course Ramesses II dominated the site as he did many others.

Love this boat trip, snorkelling, sailing, watching the dolphins play - never get fed up with it, it is different each time, just as beautiful, exciting and a fabulous way to experience egypt's array of nature.

This was built by Ramesses the 11 to impress his wife and again not at its original location due to the high dam in Aswan but put back together as found both temples stand side by side truly a must to visit.

All I ever wanted to do was visit the pyramids, and it didn't disappoint. I really can wright much because I truly am lost for words and you need to see it for yourself,  but I can give some top tips. 1) You get very pushy traders so you need to be firm and say no, don't engage in conversation and don't say you'll buy something later as they will follow you. But this doesn't mean you can't buy from them. 2) Don't let anyone take a picture of you as you'll have to pay to get your camera back 3) I went with a guide so if you want to go for a camel ride tell them so the can arrange a price and will know where you have gone, but I generally would avoid camel rides all together for peace off mind. 5) Have fun!
The site is huge and would tame a full day, the evening light show is recomeded.Would recomend a guide.if you want to understand the site through its history.Ours Shimma was extremely good.

The Pyramids of Giza are the greatest treasurers of Egypt.  We like the night shows at the pyramids when lights and sands turn everything magical.  Its like going back to the ancient times.  However, we found many peddlers annoying.
We've been diving in "Jackfish Alley" and "Ras Za'atar" with Sinai Divers. It's been a great experience and we encountered a manta ray as well as a dolphin (underwater).
It impressed!It can not be missed!The treasures from Tutacamon tomb are unique!The functional visit takes a good 2:30hs and is unforgettable!!

I can now tick one of the main items on my bucket list - the Pyramids of Giza - one of the seven wonders of the world - a must see if you visit Cairo - the Pyramids were amazing !!! . Having a knowledgeable guide  is key - the stories and details brings the location alive - i spent the time thinking about the manner in which people entered the pyramid after the hole had been sealed.The vistas were incredible - the camel ride is a MUST - with the ride you  can get to see incredible views with the Pyramids in the fore and background.One thing though - i had a image of walking in the desert  and coming upon the pyramids - that is not the case but I will not say more.The industry, ingenuity, creativity and workmanship of times gone past must be applauded  - nothing compares in present day world. I thought the pyramids must have been the inspiration for the Louvre.

I have been to Philae several times, and am always struck by how beautiful and tranquil (once you get past the traders) it is; but also what an amazing feat of engineering it is, as it (along with a number of other temples) was moved when the High Dam was built.  The scenery and history are amazing, and the little cafe is a great place for a coffee.  The sound and light show is also very atmospheric, and well worth seeeing.

Fantastic Temple and an absolute must to see if you are in Luxor!!  We were supposed to be going on an organised trip but as that did not materalise we just caught a taxi and went on our own - an Egyptologist guide was there and he took us all around for ages for LE200

We had an excellent stay, visiting the two churches with cellar church passing through Garage city.  Our escort Shady was very pleasant and very professional. I recommend, he makes us share the love of his beautiful country 🇪🇬 ♥️♥️

The Karnak temple is a must see in Luxor.Compared with the Luxor temple, it's a huge area.There a so many things to see that you can spend hours in this temple.A real highlight is the statue of Sekhmet.It's not official but if you ask the securities they may show you the way and for a small "thank you😉" you get access to his secret room.It's only fascinating...

Originally built by Nectanebo I during 380-362 BC on Philae Island, this temple complex was relocated by UNESCO to Agilika Island, to avoid it getting submerged when Aswan Dam was being built. The temple complex can be reached by a felucca or a motor boat. The temple complex also houses temples to other gods like Hathor, Horus etc.Beyond the entrance into the principal court are small temples dedicated to Isis, Hathor etc.

With the initiated move of some of the artifacts to the new museum in final stage of construction, I'm not sure how this old one will be organized in the future but a bit of dusting will be welcome. mummies museum is a must see.
Fab place to visit - spent a few hours wandering around the historic site and learning all about the history of Egypt.

Great site seeing in Alexandria , highly recommended for teens and families, citadel museum  next door , and maritime museum as well, try to avoid locals who organises boat trips , there a local kiosks for souvenirs , you must bargain!!!!

I traveled to the pyramids whilst in Egypt for two weeks as it's just something you have to do when in Egypt.I had a brilliant tour guide how was very knowledgeable which made the trip even more interesting!The pyramids of Giza were amazing to experience and go inside i would highly recommend to anyone!

Visiting the Pyramids of Egypt was a breathtaking experience! The sheer size and history left me in awe. Walking through these ancient wonders, you can truly feel the magic of the past. The views are incredible, and it’s perfect for families or solo travelers. A must-visit for any bucket list!
Visited the complex.  I was surprised by the size of the temples.  The interior monuments  were small  compared to the temple but that is Rames II style!
It was a very beautiful temple,with some of the colors still there.The only dislike was our guide who was lousy.

I have been wanting to visit the pyramids for many years and I was not disappointed. They are even bigger than I expected.Yes, the suburbs of Cairo and the pyramids are almost one but walk around to the other side of the pyramids and you could be in the middle of the desert and almost imagine it as it used to be.Take the opportunity to visit a tomb in one of the pyramids. We visited Khafre (the middle sized pyramid) and a cheaper option to the great pyramid.
The atmosphere here is really great!! You can´t miss this. But be aware of all the souvenir sellers, they can be annoying.

This really is something to experience.  The size of the buildings and the architecture is amazing. Much artwork still visible in many colours. You can only imagine what it must have been like. You need to spend at least two hours wandering around the site.It opens at 6am and is worth getting there at that time to have the place to yourself. After 9 the crowds arrive.Also avoid the coffee shop inside the site where the prices are negotiable and start at the normal Egyptian prices of at least twice the cost.

It was a great underground attraction, the catacombs are an interesting sight, though if you're not into enclosed spaces, you might not want to descend down into the catacombs.  The above ground tombs are also nice, just not so expansive. But if you're in the city, it's a nice decent stop.
Hatshepsut temple ( the beautiful temple) The design of this temple is very beautiful and its civilization is ancient and ancient.  I loved this temple very much
Excellent snorkeling trip!!Good on-board service and well supported and guided by qualified divers.

visited this as part of a nile cruise. Very impressive. Our Guide suggested we look to the rear as although it appears to be female head there is also indication they may be male .But whichever its hard to imagine how much work was involved.. work is now in progress to re-instate some of the missing ones.
This facility sure has stood the test of time.  The view was spectacular and the structure was certainly a fortress.

Saladin Citadel was very nice for an overview of the city.  The broken clock from France was interesting ( in exchange for Luxor Temple's obelisk), the fountain and the Mosque were worth seeing.  A good place to start your day!
You have to pay extra to get in here but it is well worth it - it’s fabulous- easily the best tomb with well preserved interior decorations.
Nice to see,  but all things are in the museum.  So yes follow  up in the museum.  It is very interesting.  That this tomb was left intact  as one was on top of it. Which was totally cleaned out.

Treasures from the past like you can only imagine. Walk around and soak up 5000 years of History. We only spent a few hours with our 2 teenagers but could have spent double that time. Its a must visit place and you don't have to have a guide although I would if I went back again.
Dahshur as well as Sakkara are two pyramids that you should visit if you have any interest on pyramids... The first and the third pyramids ever built.

After seeing all the skinny neglected horses waiting to transport people to the Temple, we refused to get off our boat. As much as I’d like to of seen Edfu again there was no way I’d be taken there by the obligatory horse and cart. The poor  horses …..
Compared to what there is to see in Luxor, this is only so and so. I came here on a tour. If you have only one day, go to tombs of. Nobles (Sennofer, rekhmire) and worker's village instead of this

Like many others on here i have waited all my life to see the Pyramids.  To see them in all their glory looming over from behind local housing estates as you draw nearer and nearer...then to stand at the foot of this majestic structures and take in the size and magnificence is a moment in one's life you (or i) will never ever forget, it truely is a great moment to behold these bohemoths with the naked eye. Unfortunately the locals spoil the experience somewhat by aggressively trying to make you part with money, be it through tricking you into buying items, trying to intimidate you, or simply trying to offer a service for you.Now please understand i appreciate Egypt is a poor country and people need to earn a living, im used to being hassled in India, and across South East Asia..however with the drop in tourism now it seems that the sellers rely on scams or being aggressive to make sales. We extensively researched the Pyramids so luckily we did not fall foul of the scams etc as we knew what to expect, however we saw multiple people falling for the same things over and over. Our guide pre-warned us that a lot of the people here are straight out of prison and so to watch out for pickpockets, true or not i dont know but i'm leaning towards it was probably true.  A few scams and things to be aware of at the Pyramids are:- Camel riders allowing you to take photos of camels or offering to take photos. These are NOT free, also some may take you to an isolated part of the desert and demand 50 pounds to let you off.  "free" souveniers will be attempted to be placed on top of you hand bag, in the folds of your arms, on your head, or into your hands. If you pick the item up, you have bought it essentially. A hand shake from a man "who watches over the pyramids" will result in a blue scarab being placed on the back of your hand and then will try to get you to pick it up or intimidate you into paying for it. Police and other sellers will offer to take pictures or jump in front of the your camera. You will pay for it if they have their way or you will pay to get your camera back. Basically what im getting across the guys at the pyramids are not like the Egyptians you will find everywhere else who are generous, courtious and proud people. To the majority here you are nothing but a walking cash machine to be intimidated and they have no respect for you - im nearly 6 foot and a very large build and they still tried (and failed) to intimidate me.  Don't let this review put you off your trip to the pyramids, but do research a lot what to expect then you won't be in for a nasty shock when you arrive and will hopefully still be able to enjoy the experience.

We were bombarded with touts before we even arrived at the entrance thanks to a rather enterprising one who followed us the whole way from El Gaza Metro Station. Posing as the likable old guy getting off work helping out a couple tourists, he ended up getting us off our bus early so he could take us to the back entrance where the camel and horse tours begin. We're used to touts and our Spidey Senses were tingling the whole time he led us behind the monument to the "cheap entrance, not the tourist one". Yeah, we're saps. An army of camel handlers and tourist police were there to insist the other entrance was closed and we'd have to pay 120 pounds each instead: 60 for entry to the site and 60 to see the Sphinx. Another 100 to take a camel the long way there. Right. We walked to the real, definitely not closed entrance with the camel tours following the whole time. Again we were told it was 60 more to see the Sphinx, and again we declined. Good thing, too, because your 60 pound ticket covers the whole site. Once inside, it quickly became apparent there were three touts to every tourist there - the site was almost deserted! Couldn't believe how few Westerners were there that day. The next five hours were spent sightseeing with touts always harassing us, including the tourist police themselves. Couldn't believe it, but these guys would tell us an area was closed when we approached. Of course, for a litte baksheesh...horrible. This happened any time we tried to enter a room ("No, no...closed! No, no...no photo! You pay me five pounds you go in. Take photo.") or just tried to walk somewhere. Fake police asking for our tickets, then walking off with them to some vantage point and asking for money (fell for that one just once - no one got our tickets after that), trying to put scarves on our heads, walking their camels beside us no matter where we went...it was just too much.Really ruined a moment I've looked forward to my whole life. Enough that we've canceled the rest of our visit of ancient Egypt in the Nile Valley and are headed north to Athens tomorrow instead. I know touts are to be expected at the world's most popular tourist sites, but the Pyramids easily had the worst we've ever encountered. Not cool.

I must say the Temple itself is great! The system around, how to get there if you are not on guided tour is horrible! The ticket costs 60 LE (15 for locals), in fact in all sightseeing places there are different prices for tourists, which is fine as long as a tourist price is fair. My experience here was spoiled by the fact that the ticket price does not include the boat trip! And the temple is on the island, so what is expected here? Should we swim? There are boats there which are apparently private. The price for one hour for up to 8 people is 120 LE, which is double of the entrance ticket, but manageable for a bigger group. We thought ok, we can join other tourists and share the fare, but that was impossible! Boat owners would not allow us to share with others! Why? They wanted more business of course and we did not really have a choice here. The guides of small groups were the part of the system and did not want to take us with them. My experience was ruined at the time we got to the temple. I will never go back to that place.

Let's be fair - The Great Pyramids of Giza are great indeed and there is no doubt in that.It's people who make the place horrible - from thousands of school kids who want a selfie or a picture to pushy sellers who want to make money on you no matter what. The only thing you want while you are there, is just to be left alone or leave the place asap!I believe the government or tourism department should look into the issue, as it is a shame that your own people are scaring the tourists away from such a beautiful historical place!On the way out we've made another mistake of taking a road through the real slum on the to the entrance. That was a truly appalling and shocking view to see how people right behind the touristy pyramids exist.Overall, it's definitely a place to see, but be ready that the experience won't be pleasing.

The tomb is amazing, what is particularly noteworthy is that this is one of the few tombs that was not disturbed in antiquity by tomb robbers and the furniture found in the tomb can now be seen in the Egyptian museum in Cairo.  However, due to the fact the Pharoah died young and unexpectedly, the actual tomb does not have the intricate painted carvings that most of the tombs you can visit have, but simply has the painting flat on the walls.Due to its fame you must buy a separate ticket to view the tomb, which we did.  The main problem comes with the lack of organisation and respect for the ancient monument.  The sign next to the entry says, "No more than 10 people at one time", but as no one is enforcing this there were about forty tourists crammed into the small tomb, it was exceptionally humid caused by the press of tourists, which as you can see from the picture connected with this attraction is causing mould to grow on the wall paintings.People were pushing and shoving to take photos and selfies.  What should have been a well managed and exciting, but brief visit, took a lot longer in order to be able to see anything and I felt guilty that my very presence was damaging irrevocably the ancient monument.
not recommended going to pyramids area these days as there is a bunch of unknown people dominate area and blackmail all visitor

I've dived here many times and it never ceases to amaze me how stupid many of the glass bottom boat operators can be.Many book these trips not to see the aquatic life ( which is in abundance) but to witness fellow humans diving. My issue is that the boats get very close to the divers and large items with moving props do not mix well with people, especially if the divers are novices.The sound of the constant revving of the engines can be unnerving to most and the fish don't welcome it either and vacate the area pretty sharpish therefore leaving only the 2' legged aqualung facilitated creatures alone and to the mercy of these silly boat captains.Ps...avoid and choose one of the better dive location local which does not attract sightseers !!!
Nothing has changed since the last time I was there 3 years ago.The same small fountain and the same horrible music..

Ok went to the pyramids of giza 2 days ago and im here to complain about it here is a list of problems with it.1. Locals are absolutely horrible2.Expect to get scammed left right and center (more about this later)3.Their are no officials anywhere nor tour guides (Although the locals pretend to be to scam some money out of a group of tourists)4.Over priced entrance fees to go into the grounds and into the pyramids themselves ( nothing but blank walls and stairs basically bragging rights is all this brings)5.Horrible journey to the pyramids themselves even if you pay for an excursion to the pyramids with its own tour guide (crazy drivers) but all the drivers seem to be in sync with there craziness6.The pyramids will not be there for much longer due to people climbing on them and the high amount of moisture from the very close city (at least i can say i touched the pyramids)So back onto the scamming, you had better have your full defenses up when in Egypt and good's safely stowed away. they have a long list of ways to try and rob you or get some kind of cash out of you i heard a story about a guy (just a week before i arrived) being took into the Sahara desert on a camel and having to pay 100 English pounds to get took back into town. well obviously i thought this wouldn't happen to me as you probably will if you ever go but a man asked me for a picture with the turban (directly in front of the smallest pyramid of the three main ones no officials in sight) on i said ok but i have to get to my bus they said ok it will only take 2 seconds so i stand next to the camel with the turban on trying to be friendly with the locals allowing them a picture and making myself look a bit of a dick in the process but i dident care, he then says sit on the camel i refuse politely but he insists so i say don't make it stand up (suddenly remembering the story of the guy being taken away) they agree completely but guess what happen as soon as i sit on it... yep..... he and a friend then start taking pictures i then get immideatly very annoyed of he then start walking the camel around 3-4 meters i then threat to jump of it getting in the side straddle position (after being completly fine with him and his friend this whole time) he then lowers the camel by hissing (also weird as f***) as soon as my feet hit the ground he asks for money for the camel ride.... this made me want to lay him out right there and then i ripped the turban of threw it in his face and started walking (i thought this would be the last of it NOPE) his friend then starts asking for my watch or my shoes to pay i stopped and said if you do not move right now imma lay you the f*** out with me being on the larger side of the spectrum he walks of. do what you will with this information.I mean all in all i cannot leave a rating of terrible since the sights were truly great and we got a couple of pictures before my phone ran out (remember to bring a good camera) i went during the Easter holidays exact date 17/4/2015 maybe there is more control in the summer holidays but the locals need to be moved out of there and their has to be more control not over zealous just more managable. the story above is one of three that i have this is only a 2 hour visit i learnt my lesson after that or i may have many more

It was very hot and we were hardly allowed to take pictures. Even in the places where we paid extra to enter we weren't allowed to take pictures...people judged by their citizenship and charge extra ( this is mostly every where in Egypt ) , very pricey in the gift shop outside of the museum . We liked the sight seeing but not the way how they managed , wish the government gave more care to get more tours.

Yesterday I went to visit the Pyramids of Giza, it was not a great experience and I would not recommend it and will never go back.  We got hassled and hustled from the moment we go there until the moment we left.  We did not enjoy it at all and couldn't wait to get out of there.  Will never go back or recommend it.We had a long layover in Cairo and we decided to visit the famous Pyramids of Giza.  As our driver was approaching the area, a guy jumped in the car uninvited and said he worked with the government and had a badge.  He told the driver where to park and escorted us to an area where there were some horse carriages, which they said this was the only way to see the pyramids.  We assumed this was how it worked there and he mentioned that he would be taking us to the entrance where we would pay.  After leaving the driver and on the way to the entrance of the pyramids, the man who jumped in the car and the horse carriage operator stop the carriage when we were in a more deserted area and started talking about prices and packages.  When we were hesitant and questioned him about the high cost he got angry and had an aggressive attitude.  It made us feel very very uncomfortable.  We asked him to take us back to the driver and he wouldn't, he just wanted to keep negotiating.  Horrible way of doing business.  Felt forced on us.  Feel sorry for all the tourist that have gone thru this experience and would never send a friend there.  Luckily we had an Eyptian friend we could call and help us out a bit over the phone and we ended up coming to an agreement on price which in retrospect I think it was still too high (660 Eyptian pounds for two people), but we just wanted to get on with the tour because we also didn't have a lot of time to waste.  We thought once that haggling was over we would get to enjoy the pyramids, but we were so wrong.  That was just the beginning of the hassling.    Once inside we got hassled at every stop from every person that seemed to apparently work there as the park guides, I think our horse carriage driver was taking us to his buddies to try to scheme more money out of us.  You try to be kind and polite and they just force things on you.  The insist on taking a picture and that you get on the camel.  Even thought you really don't want to, you don't want to be rude or unkind.  They just push you and force it on you.  Then they pester you for a tip for something you really didn't want.  We could not get a moment to just enjoy the pyramids, they were in our face the whole time, very annoying and completely killed any enjoyment out of a place that could be really amazing.  You didn't have an peace to really reflect on the magnitude of the place and the pyramids.Unfortunately, the way we were treated at this place in particular doesn't not leave any desire to come back and revisit.  And definitely do not recommend.  Women do not go there alone, even in small groups.  This place did not leave me feeling good.  I also felt horrible about having to be on that horse carriage and seeing the way the poor horse was treated.  It wasn't something I ever want to be part of again.  The horse looked like he was exhausted and didn't want to continue walking especially uphill.   I don't think he was given much water or food, he looked very thirsty.  He looked unhealthy and beat up.  I felt very sorry for the animals there.  At some point I wanted to walk out and leave the horse carriage but our driver was quite far and I wasn't sure how to get back to him on my own.  It's sad that a place with so much history has turned into a place where I believe many people will not want to ever return because of an experience like mine.  With the Eyptian economy being in the state that it is it's unfortunate that don't take care of the business they have.  The people that we dealt with unfortunately are cause for people not wanting to ever go back.
Price on website only states 60 EGP price, but this is only for Egyptian citizens, otherwise 225 EGP
2 hours waiting for a 5 minutes visit.....The view is pretty good but the whole thing looks like a "industrial cattle farm" guards making sure you leave after 5 minutes. Best view from below ;)

My wife and I arrived at the Tower mid afternoon on a Friday. The line didn't seem too long, so we bought our tickets - approximately $9 U.S. It took FOREVER for the line to move. My guess is there 100 people ahead of us and it took over an hour to go up the elevator. The elevator is the problem....it holds only 8-10 people at a time and is VERY SLOW going up and coming down. The view at the top is nice, but if you're staying at a highrise hotel like the Sofitel you'll get a very similar view. We have certainly seen much nicer views from similar towers around the world with less hassle getting up to see them. Oh, one more thing..........Egyptians pay 20 pounds per ticket whereas all other nationalities pay 70 pounds. I realize this occurs in other countries, but hopefully policy makers in the USA will finally wake up and similarly charge visitors to our country more than they do us residents! Fair is fair!

I went to the tower with a friend who was showing me around her home country Egypt. The view was great, but the security was not. I do not speak arabic (luckily for that security officer), but upon entering the tower, the tower security police asked my friend in arabic if she was a tour guide, she said no she was just a friend showing me around. Then the security officer started asking her if she met me at a hotel and that he could report her. He even brought his manager over and started gesturing at me to his manager before grudgingly sending us on. I felt powerless when I heard her recount this later and was very upset. I guess I shouldnt be surprised from a conservative country ruled by religion, but apparently that includes being insulted to your face. I do not recommend Cairo Tower for couples or even friends of the opposite sex. Imagine if your wife was accused to her face of being a working girl while you were standing right next to her.PS: we were asked if married at the police museum the next day when we visited as well, but fortunately no further inappropriate questions from security officers.
A long trek at night with no safety rails, and substandard bathroom facilities.This is an activity suitable only for the religious, or very fit adventurer. 7km up and 7km down!!!
i liked the view, i liked to see how it was built but i didn't like the neighborhood, the way it presented, and the way were walking and around us all camels staff is under us

I feel very angry when think how bad we managed just a great landmark. ..Not clean...very crowded. ..bad behavioured citizens make your visit as hell ...harassment  expected if you go with female. ...The building itself is a treasure. ...but we lost its essence.

The Pyramids are amazing, but poorly maintained. Cairo is getting poorer and poorer by each year, and this is evident in the way they maintain their landmarks. Sad to so say, the experience is also ruined by the hustling vendors. The push and push until one either gives up, gets stressed out, or just decides to leave. This is not the experience I was hoping for. Maybe one should wait until Egypt gets back on its feet.

Magnificent but unfortunately impossible to enjoy the magic of the site because of the touts who at the ticket office think they are guides. Oppression and scam every 5 meters. There is no way to enjoy the ride, you have to systematically escape the incessant demands.The pyramids have lost their prestige, so it is neither pleasant nor beautiful, it is dirty and polluted.

A bit boring to be honest No facillites in different places we visited and not very interesting We had an hour on the beach  with neither shade or a toilet. I t was a good job we took a packed lunch as we didn't stop anwhere to get even a drink  I would give this trip a miss.

We did this tour.  Pretty miserable experience.  The climb is long and boring in the dark.A camel ride for $20us makes it a little more interesting because you can admire the stars as you climb.The ascent requires a local guide who constantly sets you up for sales opportunities.  That gets irritating.Plenty of places to rest on the way up but you need to be with a group at your level of fitness.  The guides aren't patient with slower climbers.The guide's goal is to get you to the top quickly so you can rent blankets and by food and drink from their pals.I have no objection to this. Every one needs to make a living but the aggressive nature of the guides seems to rankle everyone.I stayed at the last bedouin stop before the stairs and had the sunrise to myself.  My partner climbed to the top and wished he hadn't.  Too much jostling for position for pics of the sunrise and lots of singing, chanting and whooping made it too much of a mob sceneI was able to capture the sunrise and then walk down the mountain without any one else around.  I had the mountain to myself during my decent and was able to watch the mountain wake up without any crowds.I hadn't planned it but a bad knee left me with a better experience than if I had climbed to the top.Still at the end of this I wish I hadn't wasted my time on this experience which took up an evening and most of the next day.
Too much waiting, a little sloppy and the restaurant too expensive with a value for money that we found excessive.  The views are nice but I don't think it's worth it

FULL OF SCAMMERS!Beware of scammers, everyone is out there to steal your money, check my picture. This man said I cannot drive straight and forced me to a sketchy parking lot near the entrance, he said don't worry about price, when we parked he told me to get on the camel and instead of taking me to the prymids he took me to a corner and 10 random men showed up and they robbed me of 200USD. They then blocked my car with their car so I couldn't leave until I paid .  The police didn't even care they passed us. I had to scream and scream until they moved my call

Place is extremely dated and poorly organized. Signs are hard to read. No AC. Poor collection of Egyptian antiquities. A lot of the artwork was missing during our visit in June 2019.The very few interesting artwork requires additional fee and pictures are not allowed (even though you’ve paid a photo fee at the entrance). Surreal.Looking forward to visit the new museum in 2020 tough.

locally referred to as the dancing fountain...... whoever programed it didn't do a good job. I found it surprising people surrounded it filming with their phones... when nothing much at all was happening.
Place is ok, locals are awful!All around is full of scammers, beggars, etc... Every single person that you will see around is looking for picking your money. Terrible experience!

You‘re working at the only remaining wonder of the world. A truly magnificent site. So live up to this greatness.Feed your skinny animals better. Make sure they don‘t have open wounds. It's no fun to flinch all the time because whips bang on horses. You can here the whips 200 meters away.Stop harassing tourist and aggressively sell rides and crap every 20 meters. I understand that you have to provide for your families but you will attract more tourists and do better business if you stop spoiling the magic of this place.Administration: establish and enforce standards for people working here. Like dedicated pickup points for camel and horse rides. Stop children working here. Ban all animals treated badly. Care for acceptable toilets, shade, some benches and remove all the rubbish, please. Make sure that cars and busses are banned from the site (allowing your people to sell more rides). Deliver value for the entrance fees!
