Lots of ancient artefacts but the organisation and presentation of the exhibits was very poor.  They did not accept credit card payments for admittance fees.

As usual another Egyptian attraction that is not well maintained. Cheap stalls, beggars in a nutshell. The mosque's carpet is filthy!!!! I don't know why those cleaners are paid. You'll hair, feathers, sand, dirt, everything you can imagine on the carpet - how do people pray on it?!!

The sensation you have in here is that you are in a mafious village where you have to pay for everything, without knowing exactly what you are paying for. Everyone tries to rob you, cheat, do anything just to take money out of you. When I say everyone, I include the Tourist Police, that makes as if they don't understand English just to ignore your complaints. Moreover, there is rubbish everywhere and zero infrastructure. So you may wonder what you are paying for. I recommend not to go here alone to avoid fights (in my case almost physical). It's more interesting to watch the light & sound show, at least you can stay in peace. If you still want to go here on your own, ignore everyone. If they touch you, just shout at them and warn them not to do it again. Bring water and anything you need from your home.

a poor visit; I did not like the way the horses were treated at this attraction.  They are in poor health and are clearly dehydrated and not properly looked after. Open sores can be seen; it's a shame to see animals treated in such a way.

Karnak temple is a fantastic place to visit, full of so many things to see.The fairly new visitors centre is a good place to escape from the hot sun.However the toilets in the visitors centre are definitely to be avoided at all costs.During our visit there I went in to use the facilities and was asked by a man on the way in for money to use the toilet, I said I would pay on the way out.The toilets were worse than filthy, had no flush box so could not flush.The wash basins were not connected to water or waste so were unusable.I just walked out before I caught some kind of disease from the germs.There are no where near as many visitors to this beautiful temple with the lack of tourists but with the few that do visit the toilets are a disgrace.

The local kalash drivers have apparently taken to stoning coaches and taxis, forcing people to have to take their rides to get to the temple.  Their horses are generally in terrible states of health and so these horse-driven taxis should not be patronised.Anyone taking a ride is also likely to be taken to their "cousin's" shop and anywhere else they choose.  Some of the carriages are also unsafe and wheels have been knwon to drop off, thrown the passengers into the road sustaining injuries which require hospitalisation.N.B.  If your tour operator advises against going and you still decide to go, your travel insurance may be invalid, making those possible hospital bills your own responsibility. BEWAREThis is all a great shame as the temple itself is fabulous.
Been waiting years to see pyramids, very disappointed due to all the scams, and I mean scams. Don’t trust anyone. Big scam

Don't go there, unless you like noisy, dirty, unsorted places. It is very crowded with merchants who wants to steal you. "A cup of team may cost you 15LE (3$)". The castle itself is not amazing, but you may like to go to the museum of fish, and it is a real good. So, what I recommend is to go by daylight to see the castle and museum, but don't go in night as you won't like it

I have just returned from the Egyptian Museum in Cairo and would strongly recommend not to go, although if this is your only ever chance to see Tutankhamun’s Gold Mask, I guess you should go anyway.It’s the worst Museum experience of any country I have been to, probably due to the fact they no longer care as the new Museum is due to open at ‘some point in the future’.3 security checks probably conceived to employ more people than they should, otherwise why should they do it 3 times in a row at a distance of 10-20 m from one another? Makes no sense.Chaotic and disorganised queuing system (or none at all), waiting and sweating under the sun before entering a museum which is NOT air conditioned, nor any attempt is made to cool it down with portable units or fans. This is in a hot city like Cairo and despite the obviously huge amount of money made by the museum with the sale of many thousand of tickets a day (give or take).The art on display cannot be faulted in itself, but the dirty and dusty museum, the terrible curation, the old cabinets, all of which not clean, the complete absence (99%) of labels that tell you what you’re looking at make for a bad experience.It was so hot and humid in there that I sweated more than in my excursion in Aswan where it was 45C.Also, many cabinets are empty because many items have already been moved I reckon;  in parts it looks like a cabinet parking space. The museum feels unloved, confusing, rather ugly to walk through.The Tutankhamun’s main exhibits are obviously amazing, but displayed in a tiny room that gets very crowded and the others in old cabinets so dirty I wanted to clean them myself.The experience ends walking through a badly laid out store that sells mostly ugly souvenirs at extortionate prices and very few books, mostly rather overpriced and old brochures of various tourist sites.Won’t comment about the cafeteria, which is awful.Leave the museum and cross the road, if you survive the crossing there’s a lovely coffee shop called La Poire Cafe with the most delicious biscuits and coffee.

Soho square itself is just a glorified over priced outdoor shopping centre. The musical fountain starts every hour and lasts for about 5 mins. Its extremely over hyped. If you have seen this type of thing before then you will be disappointed as I have seen a lot beter in Salou Spain.

I was walking to the Egyptian Museum in broad daylight when someone yelled at and followed me for now reason. The area appears safe because it's cleaner than most of Cairo, but I'd still advise vigilance at all times if you do not look Egyptian.

Firstly, I want to say that the Pyramids itself are impressive and marvellous. And the desert sights are incredible and make you feel in another time. It's a beautiful emotion... while it lasts.But overall it was a horrible experience. From the second you go in, you are hunted by touts and hawkers that try to sell you stuff and "services". They harass you so much that you forget about the pyramids and start "defending" yourself. You have to start walking away missing a possible nice spot to enjoy the visit. They don't understand the word "NO THANK YOU". They do everything to create a "debt" so that afterwards you are morally obligated to pay them. Camel/Horse riding is a nice experience, but the treat the animals horribly. Not to mention that they change the agreed price in the middle of the ride. They keep pushing it until you get mad and aggressive. Then you feel terrible because of the ugly situation and forget about the site you went to enjoy. I ended up upset and stressed out. Another thing I want to note is maintenance: What is that? Now that I can say "I've been there", I wouldn't mind not returning at all.

One of our stops on our shore excursions from the Viking Sun World Cruiser was a private tour of Egyptian Museum with an Egyptologist. He knew the ropes and how to miss some of the crowds.The facility is in transition to a new one billion dollar Grand Museum a few kilometers away near the pyramids. The state of the transition is fragile at best. Exhibits in the current facility are poorly lighted and labeled. Crowd control is almost non-existent and the restroom facilities are ill equipped to handle the number of buses arriving hourly. We were lucky in having George as our personal guide giving us access to “pass the lines” priority.Many exhibits are being crated for the move. If you have flexibility look for the opening of the new museum. It is supposed to be exquisite.

If you like mass tourism and being surrounded by large groups on guided tours thus is the place for you. If not, don’t go. The whole town is a tourist circus. There’s nothing authentic about it. And I also found their use of animals pretty unpleasant. There are camels that shuttle tourists from the river bank to the village and it’s like a non-stop camel highway. I wonder if they’re overworked because they are not friendly like all other camels I’ve ridden in other parts of the Middle East. Also in the village, there is a traditional Nubian house which functions as a tourist market and café as well and in it are kept two young crocodiles each in a concrete tank with a bit of water. Horrible.

I went to Egypt to see the pyramids & learn about the Pharaohs so Fort Qaitbey was not on my bucket list, but it was on our tour. It was interesting with a tour guide but to see by yourself - not so much.

Well where to start, guys pretending to be working in the place picking you up before you even enter. Police and staff of the place  do not care at all. Nobody speaks English from the staff so you are actually left with no option but ask these crooks.Do not trust anyone in Egypt, unless you’ve already paid him (ie hotel, shuttle service, uber, certified guides). Book a nice hotel with pyramid view and do yourself and your pocket a favor and do not go. You’ll end up spending 100$+ or literally get robbed just because you are a tourist. This pyramid experience alone made me hate Egypt and Egyptians, because unfortunately there is no exception.If you end up going afterall, hire a professional guide from TripAdvisor with real reviews that you can somehow identify if something happens. But still just don’t go. There are so many beautiful places in the world where tourism is appreciated and tourists are considered a blessing and being treated as such, Egypt is nothing like that.
wrong address. if you folowing the directing you must lost, shall you correct. i love the place but i can not follow the direct.
The artifacts available to view are extensive and worth the visit. The facilities are run down and in need of attention.

I have no idea why people come from Hurghada just to run up and down the temples when they are not even able to properyl dress and respect these places. The Hatchepsut temple now looks like the entrance to a train station with people running all over. I only enjoy the view from the Balloon in the morning but I dont take my clients there anymore, it has been totally ruined by the millions of bad behaving tourists.

Worst experience ever.On their website it’s says 9-17 everyday but during Ramadan they close 15:45 and not 16:00. We entered the museum 15:15 just for tutankhamun and at the ticket counter they didn’t inform us or their wasn’t any signs on changed openingshours worst thing was that they closed tutankhamun chamber at 15:45.We came back 2 days lather and explained the whole thing and asked politely if we could just se tutankhamun for 10minutes with same old ticket. The manager apologized about, and i told him that there is no sign nowhere that you close att 5PM.The manager wanted bribe/money from us just for 10minuter.To sum it all, Egypt sees tourist has money machine they want to take money from tourist in every possible way. Even in places where 90tourist visit they have stuff who can’t speak English.

Too long way and too much actions about Abu Simbel. I found a lot of another tempels in Egypt more beautiful and spent there much more time than in Abu simbel. Not excited at all. maybe my expectations was too high, maybe I have seen a lot before. no doubts, it's big, great and with history. Maybe it will be better to arrive the Abu Simbel not by car from Aswan like me but by cruise from Luxor. How I said before the way it's too long and Abu Simbel impressed me not that much that I would advise it.

What a disappointment.  You are the Egyptian Museum of Antiquity.  You are a complete and utter joke. The dirt, the broken cases, the hand written information cards. Pathetic.  People, go to Luxor's Museum. It is 100% better, plus they take pride in their museum. So disappointed.  What a travesty.

From the tomb, you only have access to the undecorated corridor, to the also undecorated antechamber and to the funerary chamber which contains only 4 frescoes, one of which is particularly difficult to see since it is only visible with an angle of 10-15°. The treasure room and annex are closed. One of the stone sarcophagi is presented in the burial chamber while the pharaoh's mummy is presented in the antechamber. In the end, you can quickly get around it for a fairly prohibitive price of €15.

These statues were built here guarding the Valley of the Kings. The statues are built by stacking several big stones together, leaving slight gaps so that it creates a hallowing sound every time the wind passes thru, scaring away unwanted visitors / tomb raiders from the Kings’ tombs.It was a quick 5 mins photo stop along the way, not much too see here. No entrance fees required either, it's almost like just 2 giant stone statues along the main road.

Hard to tell whats real and whats reconstruction and it is far from finished either on the excavation, reconstruction or conservation side. When it is it will be fabulous to walk along, as it is you can't really avoid it anyway as its just there, and very interesting too.

Visited 3 pyramids yesterday, nasty looking guys jumping all over our taxi demanding we take horse or camel. We could not get rid of them. Tried to get police to intervene but they were not interested, presumably complicit in this scam. We finally relented and had an unpleasant ride around as they whipped the poor horse and barked orders at each photo spot. Eventually paid them off and had a much more pleasant walk around. Shame, beautiful sight spoilt by scumbags. Hope the police will crack down once new government takes shape. For now its not worth it, stay away or take an organised tour.

The Colossi of Memnon are two massive stone statues of the Pharaoh Amenhotep III, who reigned in Egypt during Dynasty XVIII. For the past 3,400 years (since 1350 BC), they have stood in the Theban Necropolis, located west of the River Nile from the modern city of Luxor.The twin statues depict Amenhotep III  in a seated position, his hands resting on his knees and his gaze facing eastwards (actually ESE in modern bearings) towards the river.
Expected more for what we paid for the  trip. Really disappointed and could see better fish at the hotel beach than what i saw snorkelling here.

The most stupidest rule that you can’t go up to panorama because you are alone, but it is allowed for two or more people who are together. Again the most stupidest rule, I’m very disapointed, don’t recomment to solo travelers.

As tourists we had to pay 6 times over the regular price for entrance. The view is ok but I dont think that it worths its money. Also consinder that in the cafe there is minimum order cost per person, but I am not sure if that rules is applied only to tourists. In general I don't suggest a visit.

There is a new museum under construction, close to the Giza Pyramids. That can’t come quick enough. The displays have not been updated in years. The display notes are yellowed and curling up with age. The notes on exhibits are terribly inadequate and provide little or no information. Without a guide it’s a boring visit.

The concept is good but needs major uplift in setup.  After paying £20 GBP for each person we were left disappointed. The guys at the entrance were lovely but once we entered we were left to wonder around by ourselves. No tour guide, no audio, no paper map with history to walk around with. Just a little text written in front of each monument. We had walked through in under an hour, maybe half an hour.The place has the potential to be so much better. Hopefully they can at-least set up timings for tour guides which every visitor has access to. Wi-Fi access would be great for visitors as it’s a nightmare to arrange a taxi from there with no internet or phone network.
You've never been to a museum like the Egypt Museum.  There's no climate control, heat or air.  Precious treasures deteriorating, worn from constant touching.  Dusty and dirty.

The citadel is an exceptionally beautiful place, especially the big mosques, but it is being left to decay without proper care. The mosque's carpets smell terrible, and people were too interested in the expats with me as if they were movie stars! there is no parking inside the citadel like there used to be. It is a shame really.

Anyone searching for the ancient colossi of memnon on the way to the hot air baloon ride will be sorely disappointed at the exhibits.The huge temple is believed to be completely destroyed by a massive earth quake and only two broken-up statues are still visible.No signboards either on its original splendor.Waste of time.

The place is relatively ok but honestly it is extremely expensive and not worth it.. we had to pay 20 Euros per person for a tour that takes 1 hour, it has no guide, no map, no audio guide, only a small and very obvious description of each place... on top of it you have to take an uber that may be around 120 EGP each way..For the same 20 euros of the entrance I got a full day tour in Giftun Island, lunch, transportation, snorkell and beverages included... miniegypt is a tourist trap completely different from others much better (such as miniaturk).

Like a typical Cairo place, inconvenient for tourists. Guards didnt let careem car to go to next gate although taxis and cars were going. It was a slope of a kilometer and we had to drag a baby stroller to the entrance. Guards and everyone were looking and like typical Egyptian hospitality, nobody bothered or offered to help or allowed car to go.Cairo is generally a very tourist inconvenient place. We got the same experience here. Nobody knew English. Not mush written for tourists, no guidelines. Even when you are inside there are no directions that where is the exit. There is no place to find out which parts you should visit in Citadel and where are those. Nobody helps or tells you. We visited 2 mosques and then got out. We opted not to see military and police museums. We have no idea if anything else was there. I guess we should've hired a guide for it.
I know someone who has a bigger One In his backyard.The fountain does not follow the music.If you expect to see something like Dubai or Morocco mall fountains this is nothing

It was totally disheartening to see the King Tut exhibit in such a shabby, hot place after seeing it in such a fabulous museum in Houston years ago. Indeed, the entire museum had so many treasures, but one had to be a hearty soul to even look in the extreme heat with no places to even buy water, much less a souvenir. I made the mistake on going inside then figuring out that I had to pay to photograph items at the front gate. I have gone to museums all over the world and was just extremely disappointed. It was like going to a warehouse. Whoever is responsible for this mess should face some consequences. Tourists and the Egyptian people are being robbed.
Very bad with the horses there.. so sad, I will never travel to Egypt again and will absolutely not recommend it.. just grey

This was the great disappointment of our trip to EgyptThe building is interesting but the displays are in poor condition and the antiquities have been damaged by visitor contactThe building has little ventilation, not even fans. It was in the 90 degree range at 9:00 in the morning with no air movementA new much larger building is being built near the pyramids, I hope it will provide protection for the wonderful collection and a place you can enjoy the viewing

TLDR; Pro tip: don’t trust the “government tour guides” and definitely don’t let them lead you to the papyrus museum where “the museum shows you how to make papyrus paper”As soon as we entered we were encountered by “official government tour guides” which are “included in the price of your ticket”. He told us he would accept any tip we had to offer at the end of the tour, but that amount was completely up to us because he gets paid by the government to give these tours... Don’t fall for it like we did. By the end of the tour he had brought us to a “museum” where they showed us how papyrus paper is made. Except it wasn’t a museum, it was a papyrus gift shop. While there, we were pressured to buy papyrus art work, and he demanded 80 USD from us as his tip and wouldn’t accept anything less. The papyrus store and the guide seem to be working together and even have someone blocking the doorway out of there who magically disappeared after payment was made. Very intimidating situation. Be smart and be aware. Learn from our mistakes.Also, would suggest against the horse carriages and camel rides. You can walk everywhere you need to go for good pictures. These poor animals are whipped until blood is drawn.

Pyramids and Sphinx are very great. When you come to this place, of course, you what to enjoy with this excellent history. But, so many persons want to deceive and get money from tourists. It’s so pity, when people overcome a long way and can not calmly take picture for memory with their family, friends.

I visited the pyramids last week.Firstly we arrived here by taxi from Giza train station and it took us straight to a tin pot tourist office which we later realised was scam. We were told that there was no vehicular access to the pyramids and that the only way up there was on camel or horse back. After a considerable amount of haggling I got him down to LE 200, £20 sterling. The taxi driver that brought us there said he would remain and we reluctantly got on two horses. I felt dreadful about this, the horses were treated so badly, they were clearly malnourished with their bones showing and they were wipped constantly. I would not allow this to take place whilst I was on it and I my made my feelings very clear to the guide. We were then walked up through the desert on the horses and out to the pyramids, I was consumed with guilt because the horse I was sat on kept coughing and was not at all well, the amazing view was completely ruined by how upset I was. The desert around the pyramids was full of rubbish and it was very unpleasent trudging through it. We got off the horses a short distance from the khofu pyramid and the guide said he would wait for us. It was of no surprise at all to see that there were roads and a car park there full of buses and taxis. We never returned to the guide with the horses. We took several photos then got in a taxi and left the awful place, resulting in our original taxi driver who scammed us not being paid - tough luck Mr! . I would like to say that my visit here was completely ruined by the awful local people that are allowed to hijack this place.

I was so excited to see the pyramids but upon arriving I was shocked to see so much sand covering the ground and surrounding area. Now I was under the impression that they would clear this sand to make way for us but they did no such thing!!!! Instead we had to walk through the hot sand and it got all over me,,ruining my new dress!!! Horrible customer service. Don't recommend!!

Big waste of time and moneyA great temple in terms of the outside, but considering the time and effort it take to get from Aswan, in addition to horrendous crowds it’s not worth a visit.Don’t take the time of you’re on a cruise, it’s miles away, takes 4 hours if you are lucky and is in line with other temples in Luxor and worse than the pyramids…  let alone the cruise crowds ruin the atmosphereSave your c.USD100 and chill out in Aswan would be my view
Its an impressive building..its totally neglected and can be of more value than its current.Very crowded and not clean.

I visited the pyramids which were absolutely amazing!!However, there were a lot of horses and camels with their owners at the entrance and what you saw was absolutely horrible!  the animals were extremely thin, you could see the ribs on them and the scars from the beating they had had to take. Riding these animals is animal cruelty and it is perfectly possible to walk the area.  You can walk around the whole area in less than 2h if you don't stop and take pictures. One of the seven wonders but as far as we are concerned it is hell on earth for the horses, donkeys and camels lugging tourists around endlessly to the detriment of their lives! Animal exploitation like I’ve never seen it on such a grand scale!EGYPT!!!  You should be utterly  ashamed that you allow this to continue in the 21st century! And as for the tourist who are too stupid to not recognise the suffering in front of your face when riding or being drawn then you’re as guilty!!! Parasitic humans, it’s a bloody disgrace!!!If tourists don’t pay to fuel this cruelty then it won’t exist!! Help be the change and STOP using innocent sentient beings for your own gratitude!!! 🙏Thank you to Good Karma rescue for all they do to help as many of these tortured souls as possible. very beautiful church, but it's a pity that the guide talked about Islam and not Christianity, which ruined the whole experience of the trip
Save your money and breath. This place is underwhelming and overhyped. To make it worse, the guard inside will want a tip.

Nothing special. Some fishes. Not a great impression. Small reef. You swim around one place.Old boat. Expensive underwater photos of a very poor quality.Lunch isn’t delicious- no seafood on the boat. Some cheap products.

It was a total waste of time. I felt it was a real scam in every way. We felt like we were visiting a lower income neighborhood with decorative wall art. Nubian i think its more lost then preserved. We were polite and left quickly.The owner of the house felt bad for us watching the guide and his behavior. He was very hospitable kind and the kids loved his hospitality and crocodiles!!!Apart from that we were not impressed as we’ve been to authentic villages in other countries.Please judge by pics you might like to go. - In the museum, there are many exhibits that will satisfy any visitor.- Things that I didn't like:- The museum is very dirty as everything else in Egypt.- Many exhibits were lacking a description.- Some of the racks where the exhibits are placed are full of dust or insects. They seem to be left there since the 60s-70s and none takes care anymore.- I had to pay extra in order to allow me the camera in the museum.- The museum's shop is expensive, they could offer more friendly prices. Tourists are faced as ATM machines!

Nubian Village in itself was fine and worth seeing. Boat trip with 100 other boats is notan amazing experience. Take a taxi.The trip there often includes a camel ride (or something you can decide). I hope the new museum will be ready soon (it is under construction, and MASSIVE !) so all these treasures can be stored and displayed in a suitable fashion.As it is now, this place is a dusty dump, sorry to say.Bit is WAS on my bucket list, and glad I saw it. When the new museum is ready, I'll go and check it out for sure !
Very interesting place that is packed full for knowledge. I could have spent an entire day there and still not absorbed everything.

The pyramids of Giza is the worlds most epic.... Tourist trap! And that is the actual cultural experience you will have there. Sadly, Egyptian authorities has allowed the city to creep right up to the feet of the Sphinx (literally), and the whole place is immersed in scams and haggling. Tour buses form a constant traffic jam on the paved between the pyramids. You will never have a moment to yourself, because the hordes of tourists, camel riders trying to scam you into a ride and souvenir sellers all chase you down wherever you are.One of the most fascinating experiences I had there was when being approaced by a camel rider and being very aware that I was about to be scammed when he took out his scarab amulets, currency from my tiny european country asking to change, welcoming me to Egypt and so on and so forth. I decided to play along just to be able to observe his technique: It was like watching a sleight of hand magician working his tricks, leading me into moral corners where I could not refuse to hand over my money. I was kinda impressed with myself when managing to bargain down the amount that he was going to hustle me for.Aside from that, my recommendation is to better buy a ticket to the real thing: Disneyland, which is what the pyramids of Giza has become, just without the rides. If, however, you are interested in postmodernist theory and Baudrillard's notion of the simulacrum, this is the place to go to experience a fascinating mind bending case study. The Pyramids of Giza has literally become a simulacrum of themselves. But the mystery and majesty of the pyramids themselves are sadly lost to tourism mega-industry.

First, we were asked 4times higher fee than Egyptians. Second, we had to wait 1,5 hour until allowed to enter the lift. Third, we were asked to put face masks (we had nothing loke this) or to buy for extra cost (after discission with manager they gave us masks for free but had to weat just in the lift). Fourth, we had to wait again until allowed to exit. Worst experience ever over two weeks in Egypt
There is nothing great about this place. Are 2 broken statues in the middle of a road. Not really worthed the time.

Bunnik Selected; Hotel Quality OK Poor Views MostlyThe Hotels that Bunnik Tours selected were by my assessment average quality with on some occasions very high priced meals and drinks well above AU$ prices. I found surrounding same star rating hotels of a higher quality with prices lower for food, drink, and gifts lower than AU$ prices.  I ventured into same star rating hotels surrounding the Bunnik hotels and found them on most occasions higher quality, better maintained and staffed.Bunnik Selected; Room Quality & ViewsIn out first hotel we had a view of the Pyramids, (No Balcony), All other hotels we had views of Rubbish Bins, Water Towers, Trade Entrances or the sides of buildings that were in some cases falling apart and not attractive. On one occasion we had a river view but the drains were backed up and a terrible stench in our room, guest relations advised us it was only for one night and they had no spare rooms so we put up with the stench. Then on the last night we had a pool view again, it was OK, not many nice views for a 3 week Egyptian holiday of a lifetime.Bunnik Inc, Tours; Tour Destinations, Photo's/Video's ChargesThe guide appeared knowledgeable and indeed appeared to read Egyptian hieroglyphics, he also appeared to from what I noted charge us well above the posted prices for photos and videos prior to arriving at the tour destinations as he insisted to collect the money for this prior to arriving at the Bunnik Tours destination.  In some cases three times the price of the posted amount outside the places we were visiting always insisted that we pay him. So gradually most people stopped paying to take photos and very few took video due to the costs incurred.Bunnik Hotel Food/Drinks; Food Quality & AU$ CostThe food quality in some was good and taste, but some were poor, and very expensive. In one hotel we arrive at 12.01 and the hotel closed the restaurant at 12.00 and we were advised that snacks would be served at 2.00 pm, no one in our party had eaten since 4.30 am. Most ventured out of the hotel as did we to the hotel next door for lunch, I found this poor. Many guest got sick with the food on the River cruise and I believe that the food was to my mind very repedative and appeared to be re-using left overs.Bunnik Advice; GiftsAt the start of the holiday the tour guide advised us that we should wait and buy  gifts in the last week of the holiday as the gifts would be genuine and we very nice.  Most members of the groups including my wife and I complied with this advice. In the last week of the holiday the tour guide took our party to shops with extremely highly priced items; A small 8 inch Alabaster vases was AU$370.00, Piranhas Art post card size was AU$160.00. Some purchased at these prices feeling they had no choice as we had all said no all the way through the holiday. I had seen similar items in other hotels and markets so waited and purchased an identical Alabaster Vases in the Airport for $AU10.00 and Piranhas Art AU$20.00 from a 5 star grade hotel next to the Bunnik hotel in the final days of the holiday.Waste Of Time Offering FeedbackI went into the Bunnik Tours store in Adelaide following our holiday and explained that I was a little disappointed and for the reasons I have detailed above the staff member scrunched up her face and said 'sorry you were not happy, that's unfortunate you should have discussed this with the team in Egypt and filled out the feedback form'. I explained I did, then explained that the feedback form was very limited consisting of very few questions and no way to offer constructive feedback. Weeks passed and nothing then I received the stock standard email requesting feedback on our experience, this was a long survey and I gave an objective feedback account and still hearing nothing, so I popped in to offer my feedback in person to Bunniks, who showed little interest and fobbed me off.My Assessment of the Bunnik ExperienceHotel Quality; 4/5 Star (with Cheaper Rooms) Resulting in 2/3 Star Real AssessmentAverage Guest Ages; Fifty Years to Seventy Years PlusI would advise anyone to visit Egypt, my wife and I found the Egyptians people, friendly and helpful and on the whole we felt supported and safe through the holiday, just a little disappointed with the guides and the Bunnik response to my attempts to supply feedback as personally the holiday had a feel of being cheap attempt at a quality holiday provider, and that disappointed me as the brochure advertises this holiday for over $12000 for less than three weeks break.I would not recommend Bunnik Tours and we are open about our experiences with Bunnik Tours. I recommend going to Egypt JUST DON'T BOOK with BUNNIK TOURS, Unless you Seek Average Hotels with Expensive Food and Drinks/Soft Drinks,  Few Views and Hefty Charges to Record Your Holiday.
A couple of mostly broken statues in a field. That's all there is. With so much to see and do in Egypt, I don't know why anyone would stop here.
Yes, the fountains are nice but I don't understand what it so special about this.The fountains don't even move to the beat of the music.I wouldn't waste your time and go to this.

No aircons working inside museum, they allow too many people in at one time. They should allow for bookings to better manage the flow of tourists.

Appalling treatment of the horses on the horse and carts that meet the Nile ships. I don't think I have ever seen so many horses in such a disgusting state. Open sores, bone protruding through mal-nutrition, being whipped frantically. Something really needs to be done about this lot. It's a mess. I wished I'd stayed on the ship and not gone to the temple. You don't realise the extent of it until you are in middle of the trip. It's just appalling. There are plenty of other temples in Egypt.

I paid online and never got email confirmation .  I have shown bank amount deducted but person in charge made use purchase another tickets . It is being 20 days now and I have not received any refund . I am finding it even difficult to contact someone to request for refund .

I have been here many times before. Now they have subcontracted security to a private company and they seem to regard their job as stopping tourists from visiting anything. They are rude, intrusive and completely unnecessary. The whole experience is ruined by them.

Worst experience ever.Zero tourist expect few Chinese and mainly locals.Very big dissapoitment, the site is not kept clean.Expect people and scammers to harasse you for everything from camel's ride worth 100 pounds but asking $160 to souvenirs. People won't stop being on your tails.Horses and camels are in very bad shapes and treated like crap.Pyramids and site are falling apart as it ain't well maintained.Very disappointed, workers and scammers there are some the worst people on the planet and still have so much to learn in order to treat people nice and fairly and keep having those tourist back.

it's just a medium size fountain, with some music around where the water and the music doesn't go together, the change the music every time but the water stays the same boring movements. nothing to see

the pyramids should only be visited on line. the experience of being targeted and ripped off by everybody is not worth it. i found the experience dishartening. I felt vulnerable, disappointed and mostly disgusted by the deceit displayed. we got a taxi from the airport to our hotel and he suggested he drive us around for the day for US$55 which we agreed to. My buddy signed us up for a trip over to the Nubian Village. I told him I was sure there would be nothing authentic about our experience. I was right. Its a nice boat ride, but if you want that, just hire a boat instead. The village is just shops, shops, and more shops. All of which you can find in town, cheaper. One guy has some alligators he keeps in a cage in his yard, he'll let you poke them with a stick. We passed on that. Nice people, just not really anything to do there other than be sold spices and cloth.
I know you gotta get the pics, but in person this was a bit dissappointing.

Historic site ruined by everyone hassling and trying to scam you.My husband and I visited the pyramids and from the moment we arrived we were followed and hassled.People trying to get you to ride their camels/horses, telling you the site is so big you couldn’t possibly walk it on foot - this is not true it is possible to walk around.  There were  people waiting in the tombs to get money out of you, we walked into one of the tombs and looked around, a man was inside and pointed to a random wall as we walked past, we ignored him and left, he then proceeded to aggressively follow up asking for money and our constant no was falling on deaf ears, he eventually started pulling out of my husbands and my T-shirts. We had no choice but to give him money to get him to stop touching us.We were pestered so much we eventually agreed to let one man guide us around - this was a big mistake. He had a parade of people constantly following us trying to get us to pose for pictures with camels, buy cheap hats and souvenirs, buy drinks from the man selling bottles of Coke and take a horse ride. It was exhausting constantly saying no and the man guiding us was getting more and more persistent as we said no, as we continued to say no an aggressive undertone started to come into his behaviour. At the end when we had enough we went to pay him and his attitude completely changed, he became aggressive and extremely rude to us, he demanded a huge tip and  was touching my husbands wallet to see what else was in it. We gave him a tip for his time but he wasn’t happy and started questioning us on what other notes he could see in our wallet.  Overall the experience of being in such a magical historical place was tarnished by the endless pestering.If you do go, I recommend having a tour guide booked in advance who will meet you as you arrive or meet you at your hotel beforehand.If I went again I’d hire a guide and tell them if they can keep all the people trying to hassle and scam us away and let us enjoy our visit I’d double their tip.My husband and I have visited many big historical sites world wide where people try to sell you stuff but what we experienced at the pyramids was a whole other level.

The site is speechless, the history, worth the visit, but what demerits extraordinarily in our case was the harassment of the vendors, we know it is part of the work to make a living, but out of control, they trick you and pressure for pictures etc, give you thing or pressure to give saying is free and the you are requested 20 -30 usd for a photo that was supposed to be free, our group ended running from them, regretfully we had to leave the guide took us back to the van, very intense vendors at least in this moment that low tourism. Hope government could regulate this situation, site is totally worth it.

This area is hell on earth for carriage horses and camels! We where supposed to have a wonderful trip to the pyramids at Giza. But I started crying the second we got there and it was impossible to enjoy! The pyramids are amazing but seeing such animal cruelty certainly dissappointed me. It’s impossible to enjoy the view and the pyramids seeing all of this horror and knowing what is going on here. This is a world heritage site and this should not be happening.I was so heartbreaking  to see the extremely poor conditions the horses have at the giza pyramids. Many of the animals are clearly injured and are underweight, with their bones showing and with visible wounds. They are forced to work taking tourists around the pyramid sights with no water or food. Some horses slipped and stumbled on the smooth tarmac surface. Despite the heat, there was no water supply. This area is hell on earth for carriage horses and camels. This horror must be stopped!This place which at one time must have been magnificent, now is unfortunately one of the filthiest places on earth and looked after by the most heartless people.
The place is dirty toilets disgusting  the exhibits - the mummies & Kings treasures are worth going for but little else We were looking forward to it Disappointing

Don't go here.  Not because of the temple. The temple is a nicely preserved one apart from the fact that the "Christians destroyed all the faces of the gods" with chisels.  While this is likely true I think the muslims also did a good job on this as most of the temples in Egypt have been similarly defaced by successive generations.There is also a small temple behind the main one with gorgeous, undamaged, carved reliefs, you can peek through the door at it.When we tried to leave the carpark the police, armed with semi-automatics, refused to let our car, or several other cars, leave.  They made ridiculous excuses but it turned out they were after money.  The taxi drivers and hired cars do not pay, and should not.Apparently this is common at this temple and at Abydos and the many road blocks in between.I don't appreciate being held up at gunpoint.Only by threatening to report this on trip advisor would they let us leave.If people avoid this place maybe it will stop this problem.  There are plenty of other equally wonderful places to go in Egypt and we didn't have this problem elsewhere. It did sour my view of Egypt as the citizens are very friendly, helpful people suffering under this corrupt system.  It would be better if they taxed tourists, and paid the police a decent wage with the money.

I would like to talk about  a bad experience i got in Giza, a bad experience that, eventually, Was managed in an excellent and careful way from Violetta Nikopolidou, the manager of the hotel le Meridien airport Cairo.The day we arrived at the hotel we had asked the reception to organise a tour to the Pyramids (Giza) for us. We were suggested a package, offered by a company that work with the hotel, ensuring that the price (90 dollars) covered the transport and the guide.So we left in the Morning As the driver came and took us.But the driver (his name was Samer) brought us to a horrible perfume shop where a woman As horrible As the shop started covering us with perfume without we had even showed any interest in her and in her stuff (her name Was Angie, pretending to be an aromatherapist).We wanted to start our tour to Giza but Basically we were obliged to stay there for Almost a hour...until my friend bought two bottle of a crappy perfume at the price of 1500 Egyptian dollars (almost 140 eur...crazy!!!!!). Today I know that the honest Samer as many like him take percentage from the shops. If you go there don't pay more than 150 Egyptian pounds.So the "tour guide" came: his name Was Michael (so he told us) and he asked us to choose between the short tour and the long tour. I didn't know Why he Was asking us something that should have been already planned but since we had to take our flight to marsa alam in the afternoon we chose the short one.Then he asked us if we preferred horses or camels without telling us the prices. Well i Was so naive to believe everything Was included in the package.But I was wrong and i found out i had to pay other 280 egyptian pounds only When i Was on the Camel. Basically the trip plus everything (also the ticket entrance that he wanted to anticipate) we did Was on surcharge: we ended up paying Almost 100 euro per person for the camel trip, the visit of Two tombs, the admission and the tour guide (!!!!!!!).Basically we had to pay for everything this Michael suggested us included his fee that We had been told it was included in the original price.Well i think we correctly had to pay for the camels and the tour of tombs, tough i would have appreciated a more transparent behaviour but, honestly, giving the tour guide 500 egyptian pounds sounded really a theft.Moreover the skills of Michael in history and art were the same I have in engineering, medicine and chemistry (I'm a lawyer...): he didn't know anything except that Pyramids are very ancient. Thks Michael for such precious information! Anyway he was pretty good in doing pics with iPhone and iPad. At least...Moreover Giza Was invaded by all sort of beggars and sellers that have turned this ancient place in a real Siege to the turists. Even the guards at the monuments ask for money to let people visit the monuments, while they don't give a crap about the people climbing the Pyramids, trespassing every limit, not caring about damages to ones of the most monuments of the world.When we were back we were obliged to go also to a Papyrus shop: other crap. But this time we had learnt the lesson and we left the shop and the other woman without a penny and without buying any of her crappy prints.All The problems Were caused by the driver, of course, mr. Samer Who brought us into the shop and left us in the hands of the "tour guide Michael" although he was supposed to assist us.I was really upset because this company had been suggested by Le Meridien Cairo Airport (who I suggest to check the companies they work with), so that I thought it was reliable.When we got back to the hotel we talked to the staff Informing them about the bad experience we had got. We booked a snorkeling trip to Ras Mohammed National Park with Blue Lagoon Diving.  The boat trip was $75 for two people but everyone had to have a snorkel mask.  Since my wife and I had only one mask between us, we had to rent an additional one, and rented one set of fins as well which bumped our total price up to $85.  We were taken to an office where we got the equipment and signed papers basically holding the company harmless in case of bad things happening.  Afterwards, we took a short bus ride to the wharf to catch our boat.  As we understood things, the boat would make three stops presumably all for snorkeling.The first “stop” was near Magic Lake and was not really a stop.  There really was not anything to see there.  Wind can blow at three speeds in the Sharm area—very windy, extremely windy, and “my hat is in Hurgada” (the other side of the Red Sea).  On the day we went, the wind was bad and the waves in the park were close to a meter high in unsheltered open water.  I would guess that about 60% of the people on the trip were experienced snorkelers and were not really bothered by these waves.  I, however, found myself being punished by these waves and had trouble keeping water out of the mask and air tube.  We were not provided with any flotation devices.  After fighting the waves for awhile and wondering how long we were going to be out in that water with the strong currents, I started wondering if it would not just be better to go back to the boat and forget seeing the famed coral.  After fighting the waves some more, I finally grabbed on to the life saver that the leader was pulling.  About 3 or 4 people finally clung to that life saver, and it made all the difference in the world.  Just by hanging on with my pinkie finger, I was able to get back into the snorkeling mode.  After swimming away from the boat for about 10 minutes, we finally got to the point where there was something to see.  The water up to that point had been very deep.  One could see flashes of light from fish that were very deep below, but none of the coral that we had come to see.  At this point, one could see that the water beyond towards shore was maybe only 4-5 meters deep and then there was a huge drop off.  On the wall of the drop off was the nice coral that we had come to see.  It was definitely very pretty coral and some interesting fish.  We then swam parallel to the coral about 25-30 meters.  At that point, one of the swimmers not hanging onto the lifesaver was in trouble and people were yelling for help.  The leader got another lifesaver onto the person in trouble and calmed her down.  The leader then came back to us along the line of coral by the drop off and told everyone to head back towards the boat.  We had to swim against the current.  I found that I could snorkel back much better going towards the boat than the approach to the shore.  However, the current was very strong and it was difficult swimming against the current.  The Blue Lagoon people on the boat threw out a very long rope with floatation balls to keep the rope afloat.  People were supposed to pull themselves on the rope against the current instead of swimming back to the boat.   While this was not a problem for me, it did take some strength to do this, and I was happy when my wife and I both were able to finally get back on the boat.As the boat started going back in the direction where the wharf was and headed for our third stop, I initially was not sure that I wanted to get into such waters again.  As the boat kept on going for about an hour, I found my courage growing and was ready to try it once more.  However, the boat actually went past our wharf and entered a shelter cove with calm water for lunch.  We anchored and had lunch for the next hour or so.  Afterwards people could swim near the boat for another hour or so, but there really was not anything to see except for minor fish and no real coral.  Most people did not use their snorkeling equipment because there just was not anything to see at this stop.  It turned out that this was also our last stop.On board, there was an official photographer who was a very young and pleasant man.  We told him that we had our own pictures and we were really not interested in getting pictures from him.  He did persuade us to have him take “one picture.”   But as time went on, he kept taking more and more pictures.  He showed us a dozen or more pictures of us on his laptop computer.  While 3 or 4 were very nice pictures that would have been nice to have, the company wanted $40 for a disk of all of the pictures of us plus pictures of fish, coral and the ocean.  Without the fish pictures, the price was $30 and later it went down to $20.  My wife offered him $15 and was turned down.  We did not have any more money with us anyway and they would not sell individual pictures.The next day, we went to The Coral Sea Resort which is near the south side of the airport and found coral that surpassed anything we saw at Ras Mohammed. (In walking distance of Sol Sharm, Sol Cyrene, Jaz Belevidere, and Barons resorts.) The wind was still blowing that day, but there were few waves.  If one goes out to the end of the 200 meter jetty and then goes about 100 meters parallel to shore towards Barons, a beautiful coral reef opens up.   Rather than waste money on Ras Mohammed, I would go to the Coral Sea.  I am sure that there are also other “free” reefs that one can explore in the Sharm area that surpass anything one can see at Ras Mohammed.

In Egypt with our Exodus group, we took an optional excursion to a Nubian village a short distance from Aswan.  The boat ride - done in the afternoon during beautiful light was an absolute treat.However our visit to the Nubian village (which was actually just visiting one communal house) was far less interesting and - not surprisingly for Egypt - had it's own souvenir shop, selling mostly the same stuff you can buy anywhere.  The trip was about £25 per person so was a bit overpriced, we thought - you can charter your own boat trip for far less.The main problem however was the crocodile kept in a small cement pit in the communal area of the house.  I'm sorry, but I don't care how 'traditional' or 'part of their culture' this is, it's nothing but unnecessary cruelty.The poor creature is denied all aspects of it's natural life - it's unable to swim, unable to have any contact with others of its species, is unable to hunt and unable to bask in the sun like all reptiles need.  It spends it's entire life staring at grey cement walls, being occasionally poked by idiotic tour leaders (I must point out that our Exodus tour leader did NOT do this, but we saw two others from other companies do so).And for what?  For absolutely no reason at all that we  were able to determine.  Apparently Nubians keep a mummified croc in the house but keeping one captive and severely confined for it's entire life just for this purpose is an obscene way to treat any living thing, let alone one so powerful.It is not 'cute' is not 'a nice treat' and is not something any responsible tourist should want to have any part of.  We were absolutely appalled and have written to Exodus to request this element of the trip be removed.

The previous reviews do a good job at explaining and describing the beautiful temple of Philae, however I feel like most people visiting on a tour don't have to deal with the difficulty of getting there. We took a microbus from the highway off the wonderful Nubian museum from 2EGP each instead and walked from the old dam to the ticket office along the shore.After that, the trick and scam is that you need to buy your tickets to the temple before you are allowed access to Boatmen.There is now a sign that says the price should be 120EGP per boat for up to 8 passengers including waiting times but despite this already highly inflated price (locals pay 10) boatmens try to charge first 300 each way claiming the boat can accommodate only 2 passengers. While the boat is really run down, we had pictures of the sign that said up to 8 and after a solid argument they accepted to honor the 120EGP total price including one hour at the temple.Be firm and clear that the price is per boat and no per person or each way and only pay once you have returned from Philae. Considering Egyptians themselves don't tip and only pay 10, you should just walk away knowing you've already paid an unfair price for the boat and catch a microbus back to Aswan for 2EGP.
