I stayed at the Ramses Hilton right across the museum. For all my sightseeing I arranged in advance an English speaking tour guide (I used emotours which I can highly recommend). Because my hotel was situated just 500m from the museum, I chose to go there on my own, by foot. This was a bad idea for numerous reasons:1. The official Egypt tourist website stated that the museum closes daily at 7pm. Based on this information, I ventured there at around 3pm. However, it was around 4:30pm when an employee of the museum told me to leave the mummies exhibition. Shortly thereafter, at 4:45pm, the entire museum was closed.2. There are hundreds of large and thousands of smaller artefacts, most of which lack signage except an incomprehensible, numbered code. The artefacts that are actually named include explanations that will not help you much in case you are not familiar with the thousands of pharaohs, their wifes and families, priests and officials. A typical explanation goes like this: "This necklace is believed to have been worn by X (some random name), wife of Y (another random name). It was made out of gold and copper."3. The actual ticket prices are higher than advertised on the official Egypt tourist website. The website shows a regular entry fee of EGP60. If I remember correctly, they charged EGP75. For the official website type into your browser egypt.travel and search for the Egyptian Museum (TripAdvisor does not allow me to post the url).4. If you want to see the mummies exhibition inside the museum, you will have to buy a second ticket. According to the offical website both tickets will cost you a total EGP160 (around USD20), though this will be higher as their website has not been updated.5. Should you be a student, I recommend you bring your student card that allows you to get a discount of around 50%.6. There is no map of both floors (I only saw a rather unhelpful map for the first floor hidden in a dark corner). I wandered around through three-quarters of the museum and had to ask several museum employees before finding the famous Tutankhamun mask.7. For the privilege of not being allowed to photograph artefacts that belong to the public - not the government or the museum manager - they expect you to pay a "gratuity" to a man who stores your camera at the museum entrance. I paid EGP2.8. As mentioned before, I walked the 500 meters from my hotel to the museum. The problem was that this involved crossing a highway intersection without zebra crossings or traffic lights. Often you have to wait for minutes either until no car passes by, or the traffic has come to a standstill. Even around the museum there are no sidewalks. Technically speaking, there are sidewalks, but they have been fenced off with barbered wire.9. When I took a video of the horrendous traffic situation and continued to return to my hotel, all of a sudden a man appeared who told me that I should not take photos of the street. When I refused to give him my camera or delete the video I took of a public street (which is 100% legal), he assaulted me. I left, proceeding to my hotel in a quick pace. The man followed me. He eventually caught up and then violently grabbed my arm. It was only because I was just 10m away from the fenced entrance of my hotel and my threats to call the police that I was able to get away from him to security. I do not want to imagine what would have happened if it was dark and I was not around my hotel.If you still want to visit the museum, here a re a few tips:1. Hire a guide, preferably online (many of them are on trip advisor). Someone who picks you up at your hotel and guides you through the museum may cost you $60 but you will get more out of it and it is secure. I can highly recommend EMO Tours which I used for all my other sightseeing. Feel free to read my review on them.2. If you are a student, bring your student card. This way I qualified for discounts on the entrance tickets of up to 50%.3. Go there early, do not bring your camera (unless you are prepared to leave it with museum security) and bring your own water.

It was truly amazing to see all of these artifacts.  But calling this place a museum is quite a stretch.  It seems a lot of it is just piled and stored in nooks and a lot doesn't have any kind of placards explaining what it is.  There is no option to rent headphones or anything to explain what you are seeing, but there are tons of people claiming to be Egyptologists who will aggressively try to sell you their services at 200LE per hour.  Make sure you take your own toilet paper/tissues and wet wipes, because the bathroom had nothing and it was very, very dirty.  You will also be expected to tip the guard who sits outside the bathrooms even though he provides no service.
The original tomb contains only the body stripped of its finery, and sealed in a glass box. Better to spend the time on other tombs in the valley of the kings.

We have recently returned from our holiday in Sharm El Sheikh . We booked an excursion to "see the spectacular coral reefs in the crystal clear blue waters of the Red Sea. One of the best snorkeling places in the area".When we arrived at the first snorkel site the surface of the water was covered in rubbish, no doubt from other visiting boats. The experience was ruined by having to swim between the plastic cups carrier bags and assorted rubbish. The fish were beautiful, it was a shame that the site was not carefully controlled.
This is just a tourist trap, it lacks much authenticity the best part was the boat ride to get there

We wanted to learn more about the Nubian people and their lives & culture, so we ventured to the Nubian Village - and found it to be disappointingly mostly Touristy stop selling curios & offering home visits & meals ( we respectfully declined.) Later we learned that their are some smaller Nubian settlements which might provide what we were seeking, but did not find. Avoid!

nubian village can be reached by a boat from aswan. the boat ride is quite good however there is no wow factor in the nubian village. all u find is some shops selling local spices clothes souvenirs etc. painted houses schools and animal mummies on roof top. there was one house that had pet crocodiles in enclosure. that’s all. it can be skipped

Seeing the pyramids was a great experience, but my main concern is about the way the camels and horses are treated there. There is a camel and horse riding ride service there, they will take you around the pyramids to see, but they are treated very badly, one in particular was cut, they are also occasionally whipped and are not taken care of. This is extremely upsetting to see and really took the joy out of visiting the pyramids.
Luxor has many other very nice places to visit. I don't think it worth the time. The statues are not very well preserved.

We visited the pyramids and had no idea that there is more than the three large pyramids.  Several smaller ones are behind the large ones for the wives.  Be prepared to encounter an army of street vendors that have a highly developed a skill to follow you and attempt to sell you something or take your pictures with your camera or phone. $$$   It is all part of their effort to earn a living and you have to learn to ignore them the best you can.  This practice occurs throughout the country in every market or tourist attraction.It is difficult to describe emotion you realize when your standing near or sitting on a pyramid is amazing.  The construction, tools and methods used to transport the stones and erect them with that amazing precision is hard to comprehend.  We have seen many well known architectural sites around the world, but the pyramids of Giza are the gem.  Don't hesitate to plan a visit.  For an additional fee, you can go inside.  We did not and elected to use our time to take pictures and wander.  Those few that did go explained that entry is physically difficult, very hot and all you see is an empty room.  One couple turned around and exited without attempting to crouch and try to go further. Your call.

The statues are awesome, and more things being found in the fields next door. Can be seen from afar. But tried as we could we couldn't hear any screech, moan, scraping noise. Just silence of a civilization long forgotten among the howling sands !
The museum very hot no air conditioning no cafeteria to serve any kind of beverage.They mentioned that the new museum will launch soon

This is a bit of a let down if you go to soho square just to see the Musical Fountains?If your British you have probably seen better in places in the UK. it's more impressive  to watch a clip on Youtube of the Bellagio las vegas. that's what I call Musical Fountains.

We went to pyramids today April 2022. My husband stupidly said yes to horse and cart ride with a local tour guide. These poor horses are abused and worked so hard in unsafe conditions. The horses are made to canter up the slippery cement road. Our horse was loosing grip under its feet and its feet were sliding. So I asked the driver to let him walk. On the way down, the carriage was too heavy and the horse was slipping again, the horse was trying to crouch down and bend all his legs to stop the carriage from sliding over top of him. I was so horrified and I can’t get the sights out of my mind. PLEASE BRING AWARENESS TO THIS! If you have had any other experiences, please post to bring awareness!!I can barely remember the pyramids, because I was so scared for the horse the whole time.

If you are a solo traveller you can still access the Tower, HOWEVER you cannot access the very top of the tower (that’s only for 2+ people). You can only go to the 61st floor (if I remember) where you can observe Cairo from behind the glass window. Technically, there is nothing wrong with that.I will compare this to my visit to the Saigon Skydeck (in Vietnam) which is on the 49th floor of the Bitexco Financial Tower. Here, you also observe Saigon from behind a glass window. That said, the Saigon Skydeck completely trumps the Cairo Tower for two main reasons: 1. The windows of the Cairo Tower are grimy so you cannot really see out of them. This is made worse when the sunlight reflects and, 2. There is hardly space to walk around because most is given to the restaurant and the chairs, whereas there is PLENTY of space to walk around the Saigon Skydeck which also has a shop and an exhibition.I spent about 15/20 minutes in the Cairo Tower because I forced myself to stay that long. After 5 minutes, I was bored. I guess you should visit if you don’t mind eating there with “somewhat” of a view, buuuut this is definitely something you could skip.I asked one of the workers why solo visitors are not allowed at the very top and she said it was for their own safety incase they wanted to jump off…so I guess a positive is that they are just looking out for the safety of their guests.One suggestion could be going at a busy time (I went around 9:30am which was definitely not busy) and asking another group or couple if you can pay with them so it looks like you came as a group, but that's a gamble.Entry price was EGP 250 for solo visitors.

Waited for almost 3 hrs, and as drinks are not allowed through security, it was 3 hrs in sweltering heat with no water.Furthermore, despite most queuing in an orderly fashion, several locals  filed in without waiting (and without being part of another group waiting)  and then proceed to make rude comments when I wouldn’t let them in.Then after almost 3 hrs, I was eventually let into the building only to find out that it was another 30 mins to get the lift!So for a VERY expensive 180 pounds, 3 hrs of my time, being rudely treated by individuals that cut the queue to begin with, I left before wasting anymore time.I instead ended up at the Nile View hotel with a stunning view spanning the nile, and for the same price of just admission to the tower, I not only had an equally beautiful view but also a delicious meal for 2 included in a hospitable environment.General advice on Egypt in general based on my experience and those I have spoken with, come to Egypt but spend as little time in Cairo as possible. I recommend staying only a night or two (someplace like the Nile View hotel/Boat) and do a tour of the major sites (highly recommended Emo Tours), and then explore the rest of Egypt. It is a far more hospitable and charming place /country than Cairo will led you believe.

First the careem driver took us in backside very narrow streets and then we had to walk to come back to the front gate which was closed. Guards ask everyone first from where they come and if they want to visit for ziyarat then they open the gate.There is a huge history of al azhar mosque but nothing like you will see there. There are no guidelines for tourists, nobody understands English, no plates or historic stuff placed. It's just like an ordinary mosque. Guard pushed us to the main hall, didn't allow to go anywhere else. So couldnt see anything else. Even history of mosque isnt written anywhere.

A trip to see the pyramids was spoilt from beginning with difficulties getting tickets with crowds then rude man demanding money at ladies toilet followed by pushing crowds at security check. Once on plateau horses pulling carts race each other where you are walking. The horses are cruelly whipped as well as being in poor state of health. There is no water available to them during the day. The constant hassle is also dangerous as they ride so close to you some driven by young children. It is impossible to complain to tourist police as carts have no number to identify them. Inside chamber of great pyramid 'guards' seriously hassle you to take forbidden photos then demand money. Not a pleasant experience. Great shame.

The pyramids themselves are an amazing wonder to see, there’s no doubt about it, however you may struggle to enjoy the experience - like I did - due to being constantly harassed, from the moment you purchased a ticket to enter the site, by “guides”.Aside from the persistent harassment, the camels and horses used by the guides to extort more money from you are in poor shape.The infrastructure around the site is awful, roads are near impassable in places approaching Giza.How on earth has this “wonder of the world” become such a catastrophe?Suggestion? Charge more for tourists to enter, rid the site of beggars, tidy the litter up, let the animals go and allow your customers to enjoy their experience.I’m glad I was able to see the pyramids during a business trip, and most certainly wouldn’t return on a vacation.
It's really sad seeing the museum in such a poor condition. The lighting is terrible and a hell of items have no description :(
Not much to see and just not worth your time when there is much more worth while. I'm just disapointed, I know they are big but no detail and just plain boring.

Snorkeling is okay but the hassle is too much. First, let me say that there are better places to scuba and snorkel. A LOT better places! But because it is unique I gave it a poor rating.Bad things: First, the oil balls! Be prepared to get your gear and feet covered with an oily goo. Seems to be a problem with so many tankers going buy here. TERRIBLE mess on our equipment and in our vehicles.Next, no supervision or caretaking. You drive around to the "sites" and you can barely read the signs, there is nothing around to help you out except some faded directional markers. So, don't plan on being WOWED by the sites here, just go for the reef and WATCH OUT FOR OIL BALLS on shore!
Good viewView to two huge mosques which in one the former Shan of Iran is buried.View over destroyed neighbourhoodBe aware that everything is highly over priced and you will be charged for anything

A lot of damaged coral and disturbed wildlife. Thomas Cook excursions tell you to get a Visa but then avoid areas of the reserve where a Visa might be needed. House reefs on the Straits of Tiran are in MUCH better condition-fewer people and more sealife.

This would have been a nice attraction as the 2 statues are so huge and old, but the place is ruined by the touts. It's a small area to view, so they are relentless in hawking their goods. Honestly, it's best to just view from the road. You drive right by these on the road to the Valley of the Kings area. If you stop, be prepared to be mobbed.

Be prepared to remove your shoes (bring socks) or pay small fee for paper coverings for your shoes.  Women do not need to cover their hair since it is not a working mosque.  The interior ceiling and walls are magnificent.  Great views of old Cairo from outside.  Another site where it was worth it to have a local guide share historical stories and significance since there was no literature or reading aids to supplement the visit.

Do not go to Soho Square to see these fountains. See these fountains if you are in Soho Square by all means, but they are nothing to write home about.They are as exciting as watching paint dry, and the music is simply annoying. Cannot see why anyone would even stop to watch these, look and walk on you silly people, you're causing a roadblock for some lights and a bit of water.

Very touristy and not very truthful. If you don't go with an organized group where the guide has already paid the locals, they will ask you for money for practically everything, from photos to tea, to everything they try to sell you as soon as you get off the boat. They asked us a lot of money for everything and if you are not willing to give what they ask for they treat you badly. It's not a truthful experience. To see the real Nubian population it is much better to visit Elephantine Island, where the people are much kinder.

an awful lot of walking is what you remember most, really not much to see, lots of steps down to the tombs after a trail up hills to get to them! no good for children or wheelchairs or people who struggle to walk.top tip, take a hand held fan on the holiday with you

Quite frankly, I can understand why Great Britain won’t return the Rosetta Stone. Egypt doesn’t deserve to be the guardian of these wonderful treasures. This museum is awful. I hope the new one will treat these antiquities with the care and respect they deserve.

We went to the opening for 7:30 in the morning.Arrived at the entrance a 30ene Egyptian man who looked at us badly and waited for us to sell us fake tickets and scarves.Hard to find the ticket counter. The counter is to the right of the entrance hide behind a wall in a corner.After we got the ticket, we got harassed for scarves.We went back into the Pyramid Lock.And there a man took the phone from our hands to take a picture and asked us for money in exchange.In the pyramids, we are constantly asked to ride a horse-drawn carriage or camel.It is a country to flee.

I loved the sight of the great big pyramids but I couldn't ignore the crying of the camels. It was heart breaking and it ruined my whole trip. I did take photos but in every single one of them there were scammers trying to talk to me. I kept saying the only word I knew in Arbic "la" which means no. But obviously their experienced scammers and knew what to do and say. But anyways the pyramids were nice but it's just in the wrong country and place.4/10 LEAVE THE CAMELES ALONE

It is small, dirty and full of stalls with vendors trying to sell you something from China. Skip this stop and spend time with the alabaster shops. That you will go by on the way back to your hotel. They are much more friendly, they want you to buy, but at least it is made there. Offer half the asking price and settle for a 40% markdown.

Frustrating experience for ancient Egyptian history lovers... everything is covered in dust, dimly lit rooms, pieces put on display facing the walls rather than the audience. Filthy display windows, missing information tags... murals, sarcophagi and status are left for the public to touch, lean and sit on
Boring and nothing to do. Not worth your time. The harbor is nice with nice restaurants! So that is highly recommended.

Lack of any type of explanation of the artifacts. We visited the museum on a Thursday night, which was much less crowded. The set up of the museum was quite confusing. Because there were pretty much no signs or directions, we wandered about inside for about an hour and finally gave up and left. The only thing I can remember after the visit were rocks and coffins.

Hi thereI did travel around the world many times.In cairo and especially the pyramids i felt very unsafe.At the pyramids you will not be able to walk 5meters withoutbeing harrassed by thugs.I had a private guide from my hotel (5 stars)even he tried to rip money of me on several occassions.When we got to the pyramids in our private car,security guy opened a gate and asked for money.my guide insisted that i pay or tour is over.(you dont need to pay anything)Then when you get out of your car,even with your guide, thugs literally fall over you.From camel rides (please dont do it, camels are beaten severely etc and horse riding.)here you find animal cruelty to the extreme.No body lets you enjoy the sights.i had a guy trying to punch my friend for no reason.he just didnt want to buy some worthless crap.Please stay in your hotel close by and see the pyramids from there.I would never go back therr.it is just terrible and dangerous.Ps update bus just got bombed,at least 4 dead
If you are a solo traveler, plus you wait more than 4 hours to get on; you are only allowed to be in a super expensive cafe; You cannot enter the viewpoint as such. I would not repeat the experience.
Saqqara is a perfect example in how to destroy history with filth and bad administration. Hungry dogs are everywhere, all filthy and in bad condition.

The pyramids are awesome... someone should see them once in their life... but unfortunately the people there don't let you enjoy it! They always ask you for money for everything! The horses and camels are malnourished animals (don't support this disgusting sight) also they don't let you pay with cash only by card 30€ the entranceInside the pyramid it is unbearably hot and very tiring if you can't stand it or have health problems I don't recommend it

Have visited Hatsheput many times before - did a day trip to Luxor to show a friend. Have never previously encountered such aggressive and rude touting of tat - some members of our group were tearful from the treatment. Gangs of touts would close in on tourists, ESPECIALLY SINGLE WOMEN. AVOID. Note Karnak & Valley of the Kings were not as bad as Queen H's temple (hence Karnak/Kings gets 2 out of 5, not 1/5). It never used to be anything like this, and I've been coming to Luxor for over 25 years....

The horses on the carriages are mostly rushed until they have hardly any more power! Many horses are injured and emaciated.Please take a CLOSE LOOK before deciding on it.Pyramids of course magical, but overall a lot of magic is lost with misimplemented tourism, very sad, if not disrespectful to the ancestors.

Was nice to be able to visit the pyramids although it was never really a bucket list thing for me. I was just totally overwhelmed with the number of people that try to scam people. I would recommend that you go with a reputable tour company to visit. I went in on my own, big mistake. From the moment I walked through I felt harassed. A guy working for the government that had an official badge came (what I thought) to my rescue and showed me around. After I paid $120 to ride on a carriage to see the place (too far to walk in the heat of day) he still conned me out of a tip at the end. He insisted I give him $50 and LE2100!! So in total, including the LE200 entrance fee I paid $170 and LE2100... I do feel a bit like an idiot for falling for this but being a woman on my there they successfully managed to intimidate me.... certainly left me with a bad taste in my mouth.

This massive citadel offers a wonderful view over Cairo up to the pyramids. Apart from this, the mosques inside are much less interesting than others in Cairo, the Police museum is totally boring and the military museum was closed for renovations. Paying a steep L.E. 140 to get in is really not worth the visit... unless you really want to see the view on a clear day.

Our trip to the pyramids was a fulfillment of a lifetime dream.  Go with a licensed guide, and I stress licensed because there are all sorts of shady sorts handing around pretending to be "official" guides.  Do buy the additional ticket for the solar boat museum - it is well worth it.  Do NOT pay for the additional ticket to go into the pyramids - it isn't worth it.Cons:The whole site is littered with garbageOn site vendors are aggressive and abusive.Toilets ( if you can call them that ) are filthy and reek.  If you think you may have to use the facilities,  bring your own toilet paper and paper towels.Do NOT get one of the locals to take your picture - it will cost you.  You may not get your camera back unless you pay "their fee for taking your picture"Although there are "Tourist Police" on site,  they serve no purpose other than to serve in the overall mayhem of visiting the place.Bring your own water - buying onsite will cost you 3 to 4 times what it should

Te cobran más de 10 dólares la entrada por ser turista.Tiene solo un ascensor con capacidad de 10 personas max.Hice una hora de cola para subir y media para bajar. Un desastre!So expensive (10 us dollars entrance fee) and bad organized. Had to wait so much for going up and another half an hour to go down.It has just one elevator for 10 people maximum.

The Good:Visiting Saqqara is a bit better than visiting Giza. We went on our own just walking around, quite a bit of walking but it’s not too difficult we could see it would be a nice walk around in cooler months.The Step Pyramid of Djoser was under construction. But it was still interesting to see. Tombs were all quite interesting and better than Giza in our opinions.The site was mush quieter, and cleaner.The Bad:lack of information on the tombs and the pyramids. It wasn’t easy to find our ways around. Also, like Giza there wasn’t a lot of information on site about the tombs and pyramids.Tour guides type of people hanging around, honestly we properly would not know where to go to see the tombs if we did not have the guy insisting to lead our way. But it was utterly uncomfortable for us to have him around, as his english was not very good and he walked us through each site very quickly, almost rushing us through. All along he tried to sell us camel and horse ride, we did not buy it. At the end we gave him 100 LE as we just tired of uninvited service these people want to provide. But he insisted for us to at least give him 200 LE per person. We told him we are poor and we did not have money, and gave him an other 50 LE.The Ugly:The staff on site were bad, just like Giza, they ask you for tip for letting you in to see the site. They made us so so uncomfortable as all we wanted to do was to take out time to see the tombs. Those guys took us through each roomAnd just rush to take pictures for us. Also told us pictures not allowed but for us it’s ok. But there was no sign to indict pictures not allowed. They all rush us through and just ask for money at the end. We gave them 50 LE each but they were all not happy. They asked for more, they wanted Dollars or Euros. We just told them we did not have any money so they let us go.Also the way the guys there treated the sites were just heart breaking! They had their torches shining on the walls of the scribe or whatever interesting on the wall, they glazed the torches on the artifacts, touched the scribe with their hands. Where there were a stone coffin one of them asked us to lied in it to take a picture (we refused). The guy at the entrance checked out tickets and torn a bit out, just crumbled it and dumped it on the floor.Summary:We did not enjoy any part of this nor the Giza. Even the pyramids were amazing. Cairo gave us a very bad experience, certainly as tourists we felt unwelcoming and cheated. We just did not want to leave our hotel as every where we go we could not have one minute peace to ourself. Cairo is definitely a one off trip. We will never come back again.

After years of dreaming of visiting Egypt, the opportunity finally presented itself.  Naturally, we were concerned about security but what we wish is that someone had warned us of the TOXIC environment.  There are piles and piles of garbage everywhere you look.  Some of it burning.  So combine that with the horrible traffic in the Cairo area  and you can only imagine the horrific air quality.  Yes, the Pyramids of Giza are the only of the 7 Ancient Wonders left.......perhaps a modern day wonder will be a pyramid of garbage.   I would advise postponing a visit until thecurrent government can recognize that people need to live in a clean environment.

Unfortunately, the beauty of the pyramids is overshadowed by a bunch of men who have nothing to do but snatch the Uralubians and tourists. You are kindly asked everywhere at first, then pressed to pay tips, even though no service was provided. For my wife, it was hell in between. The men are outrageous and make it almost impossible for any foreign visitor to go there again. we were at least able to take some photos. NEVER engage in an offer until you know what it costs, because in the end you are asked to pay tens of times as much money. They are not your brothers as they imagine Also Guided Tour is useless. Just visit and leave. No one around is your friend. Everyone is the same. The men see the women as fair game. I don't want anyone to believe the many guards can do anything about it. On the contrary, the guards help the tour sites, but after that you also demand money.
Monument in poor condition and not much to see so coach stops are short.  Local vendors of junk are particularly obnoxious.  Stay in the coach.

So we made a trip to aswan to check  out this temple  and all was good untill we made a trip the the temple of philae. It all started with  the rip off cab ride to the temple. Whe. You negotiate  the price of the cab make  sure you include the charge for the taxi to get in. They  charge 5le we took a one way  trip because  our cab smelled like gas. It was an ancient  Peugeot with raggedy  seats. The back windows did not roll down. It started smelling like gas was leaking. Then we arrived at the temple to a swarmed like piranhas to buy shirts also make sure you bring water they try to charge you 10 - 20 le.Although  This  is not a lot of money for tourists we mess up the econmics and cause them to raise prices for all foreigners including the ones working in egypt thar get paid in egyption le. So we did Not bring water so we had to buy some. I got them down from 20 to 10Then i purchase  the tickets for like 35 le per person. The price did not include  the boat. After reading the sign it states the charge for the boat ride is 84 lè for 2 hour.This site  seems to not take student ids.Once you get to the boat they tell you 190 for 1 hour. We nicely explained  that the sign says 84 for 2 hours. We went back and forth  for 20 min ans then  gave them a final  answer. We said  100 or we will return our tickets to the ticket booth  and notify  the tourist police. They  through  we were joking  so we walked  to the booth and stopped  us. Total rip off but after we got the boat we had a great time 2 hours is more than enough to see this site. DO NOT PAY THE BOAT DRIVER  TILL YOU RETURN.Once we got back we got swarmed at the docks to buy junk. Then you go through  security  and wait for cab. Dont use the cabs at the location  if you took a one way they will rip you off badly. Its best to call a cabnor have your hotel call one.I gave  this 1.start because  by the time i got to the island i was already pissed. I think the tourist  police are not doing their  job and making sure tourists  are not getting  ripped off. I bet they know the boat drivers are making  thier own prices.  Let me put into comparison. It took like 6 min to get to the island and thet wanted 150 to 190 forthis. A boat rental for 3 to 4 hours cost 150 to 180 a car ride from luxor to aswan  costs you about 550 to 650 le and irs.a.long way.no hpe this turns around or this will be bad for  business for  them

Beware : This is the only place where Arabs other than Egyptians are treated as expats with an Entrance ticket that costs 70 EP instead of 20 EP.Avoid the restaurant .... Overpriced ....average food....small portions...Goodluck

the fountain is exactly as stated, its plays music to coloured lights a music at around 9pm every night.  the place is huge with shops and restaurants.  ok if you have nothing to do one evening.  the food is quite expensive.  its hot there, obviously, but even so the oriental restaurant we dined in, didn't allow sandals , t-shirts or shorts.  they provide you with trousers and shirt and stocking socks.  but the air con wasn't used and dressed in 2 layers of clothes; we were wet thru, too hot and couldn't wait to leave. it spoilt the experience really.  I don't know if all the food places were the same, but be forewarned.
Hmm.   Went on a tour and saw this place.   Very dusty and not clean.  The fount is not working.   It's a shell of a mosque compared to many others.
Weather choppy, seen far more fish whilst snorkelling at the hotel. Really wasn't worth spending such a long time on the boat for the little time you were in the sea

This is a must visit. If you really want to see everything and learn, then one day won't be enough. Experts say Egypt contains 60 percent of the ancient remains of the world but you must not confin yourself to that. Go visit the sites.

We went with a tour guide fortunately and he gives us great insights to some of the key pieces. And will recommend you engage one.The place has an amazing collection but poorly presented. The description on most pieces are not clear, or some even without description. And most have poor lighting and were disappointed how they even had some statues wrapped and placed on the floor-likely is to be move to the new museum.I was told there is no audio-guide which is such a pity!Toilets were extremely dirty and smelly! Sadly this is a national museum!Bookstore, poorly presented and poor choices of books. And most had even a layer of dust on it.Please do better! What a great and rich history but so poorly presented.

Place is beautiful, culture of scam is awful. They will try to extort you by any means. Sadly Egyptians think that all tourists are stupid ( rare exceptions)  and they will try and try to take you money by any possible means, even the police. Trust no one and learn to say NO. Don't pay tips, don't pay police, don't buy anything of you think the price is not adequate to the country. I won't come back.

In spite of the historical significance of the pyramids, I don't recommend going there in person. The Giza Necropolis (as it is officially called) is full of individuals trying to make money in dishonest and disrespectful ways. Their usual technique is asking to see your ticket (even though they are not ticket inspectors). The man who was the very first to approach me referred to himself as a "watchman". After a ticket inspection he asked me personal questions and followed me as I toured the pyramids, even though I told him several times that I didn't want him to escort me. Another individual even approached me saying "I'm the guide, I will be with you". A third one yet asked to see my ticket. Then, when I refused, he offered to take my pictures. The police are present on site but they aren't doing their job. It's hard to get sight of a policeman and call one when needed. If they really care about the prestige of this site and the tourist attractiveness of their country as a whole, they must put an end to this harrassment. They must walk around (perhaps in plain clothes) and listen in to the conversations that are taking place.While walking around the complex, you are never left alone: camel and horse owners constantly approach you offering their services. Even when told that you're not interested, they keep following you, shouting out their offers, asking where you are from and saying that you look Egyptian. The sand around the pyramids is convered in camel/horse poo, and the smell is awful. All in all, in my opinio it's not worth going at all - the pyramids look much better in pictures and in documentaries and travel videos.

It is so unfortunate that this place is surrounded by the most dirty surrounding and served by a bunch of rip off and extremely dishonest people who would try to get money from you in every way for absolutely NO service whatsoever!This place which at one time WAS great, now is unfortunately one of the filthiest places on earth and looked after by the most worthless people.

I hated my trip the Pyramids.  I didn't have any time to just look and take it all in as I spent my time running away from the constant hassel of the sellers.  My boyfriend even got punched in the back by a small child because he wouldn't buy a postcard.  Charming.  In the end my otherhalf and I got back on the coach and enjoyed the view from there.  Awful Awful Awful.  I've written an email to the Egyptian Tourist Board as I just thought this was so sad to be so spoilt.On this visit to Egypt I did a trip over to Petra in Jordan which had little sellers and no where near the hassle.  I know where I'd be recommending my friends and family go and it won't be the Pyramids.

I thought for the money it wasn't worth it. There are so many other tombs to see that are mush better than this one. Yes I know people are intersted in the king tut but spend the money going to Cairo and seeing his collection as the money would be better spent.
Just two huge broken statues. Can be seen from the raod itself. Donot waste time in parking and getting down here.

Like all monuments in Egypt, the organization leaves something to be desired. Fortunately, some places by their natural beauty or by the means put to highlight them save a potentially flattering picture. If you are from the Maghreb you risk coming across some suspicious reception agents, who in the flow of no less than fourteen cruise ships full of Spanish or German tour operators, will put you aside to check your identities. Specifying that you have already passed several control filters. In short, the behavior of the Egyptians is not great. I won't allow myself to talk about the rest, leaving you free to discover it.

If you go to Valley of the Kings and buy the main ticket you can visit three tombs, but they don't include the tomb of Tutankhamun.the ticket to the tomb of King Tut is available there for 100 EGP per person, I would not recommend it because it is very poor in details since this tomb was not finished before the death of Tutankhamun, the interesting thing about Tutankhamun is the beautiful collection was found inside his tomb but all this collection  is displayed in Cairo MuseumThe only thing could be interesting is his mummy which is the only mummy displaying inside a tomb in Valley of the Kings.
Sad, shabby, dirty, miserably hot museum filled with amazing, priceless antiquities. What a shame... this museum could be astounding.

I have little respect for everyone who argues that the pyramids is a must to see because "it's the last wonder of the world". It does have something special, but the settings are so unsettling that it takes all the joy out of the extraordinary moments.I cannot enjoy the sight anymore when I see horses slipping down the mountain road with a carriage full of heavy tourists. The horses and camels were in general very skinny and mistreated - we even passed a dead horse lying in the side of the road covered in trash. The little kid who dragged our horses should have been in school and had yellow teeth, holes in his clothes and were treated quite badly.The touts were very persistent and didn't leave us time to marvel at the sights.

We were at So So Square for a couple of hours and this central fountain goes off every hour. We made an effort to be there for 9pm and sat at a nearby table for 10 minutes without being served before the show started, probably a good thing as I noticed there appeared to be a cover charge more than the cost of the drinks we were going to order. Once you have seen a few seconds you have seen it all. The music was terrible, perhaps Arabic? and we swiftly left. By chance we were nearby went it started again at 10pm and at least the music was more bearable on that occasion.

While there are many antiquities housed in this museum, it was tragic to see the way the museum had curated most of the exhibits. Many of the pieces are broken and simply laying on the floor. This was a big disappointment for us.

So we went to the pyramids , some shops, a museum. The guy on the bus said after the museum we are gonna head back to hotels and then unexpectedly made another stop for boat trip. Only 8 people wanted it. What about the rest of us that didn’t want it? we had to wait in the bus for over 30 minutes for them to do the boat trip. next time you do something like this at least tell us because it’s 6 hours away from the hotel and people got places to be. Also to mention the guy in the bus that was talking he was talking way too much he was repeating the same thing over and over again.
My expectations were high for the Egyptian Museum, but it is poorly set up, badly lit and antiquated. I will be excited to come back when the new museum opens.

the place is the same since i visited it in the 80'S, totally unorganized, filled with beggars and thugs. the tourist police does not exist or does not want to exist. no amenities or clean shops around, no restroom. if you want to go be part of a group....
We did not enjoy any of the experience.  Felt like part of a flock of sheep being herded around for the day!!!  Much preferred Sharks Bay.
Do not go if Solo Traveller as I had big argument at the top.. You cannot see the view if solo as ‘family’ area he kept saying!! Very Rude.. Avoid !!

Sure it is the tallest building in the Cairo skyline but there is not much after that. I was really hoping the restaurant on top would be good. Went there for my anniversary and was quite disappointed. A VERY expensive meal (compared to better restaurants in Cairo) and a meal that I could have bought for less than a quarter the cost less than a block away. Seems you are paying for the view and not for the food.Disappointing.

I took a boat with family for 500egp to visit batonical garden and nubian village, the boat ride is amazing and batonical garden is beautiful.. but nubian village was just a place with lots of stores and merchants who keep chasing you, nubian house with few caged crocodiles. Nothing special other than the colorful houses.

Animal abusePlease dont ride on a horse or camel or use  a carriage. the animals are mistreated, get hit often. Horses cant walk on the slippery street , they fall terribly and break their legs. They are way too bony and thin and the legs are wounded. They are not in the shape to carry tourists.Thats what we saw. Dont Support animal abuse, walk by foot, its possible, the scammers try to tell you its only possible  to go to the area on a horse or camel,thats a lie!
We went snorkeling by boat, and the hotel's reef (Halomy, Naama bay) had more fish and corals.

I have to say some of the artifacts inside the museum are simply amazing. My "decent" title applies to the museum itself. It was built in 1901 and they're in the process of moving to a new location "within the next 2 years" (which, in Egypt, could possible mean anytime during the 21st century). There is very little signage to tell you what you are looking at. There are numerous columns, sarcophagi, statues that you can touch, but not marked as to whom they belong to. So it's best to have a guide. The highlight of this museum is the Tutankhamun treasure room which houses his gold death mask and the 2 innermost coffins. But, you are not allowed to take photos of these and if you're caught you will be yelled at and your phone or camera grabbed and made to delete the photos. Trust me when I say during the 10-15 mins we were there we saw this happen no less than 5 times. There are many many items from Tut's tomb on display in another room that you can photograph. That being said, when you buy the ticket to enter the museum you must purchase a photograph pass as well to take pictures inside the allowed areas of the museum. If you are caught taking photographs and do not have this pass you will be fined a hefty price. In the two separate times I've visited this museum I never saw anyone checking for the photograph pass (which costs almost as much as the entrance ticket), but photograph at your own risk of you don't buy the pass. Another very interesting room is the Mummy room that holds the mummies of several major pharaohs (Ramses II, Seti I, among others).  There is an additional charge of $5 USD to enter this room and you pay as you enter. Again, as with Tut's treasure room, ABSOLUTELY no photographs of the mummies. Yet, this didn't stop anyone from doing it. And while you are yelled at, people continue snapping away. While it's possible to enjoy the museum just wandering around on your own, I feel the benefit of a guide would be so much more educational. I have been with and without a guide, and will say the guide was better. (FYI Tut's mummy is still in Luxor on display at his tomb. He was too fragile to move)

Too bad for the Egypt's history as this museum is in a poor condition. Of course, you start by being hares by "so-called" guides and then...well...dust in each and every place of the museum, has no organization, no captions, poor information, very badly lit (even in Tutankhamen section there's NO LIGHT to see the massive gold sarcophagi. If you want to see the mummies, well...you have to pay another 100 L.E extra along with your 80 L.E entry ticket. Even when you enter the museum, there are all kind of thugs-guides who waiting to scam you with some poor information for which they ask a lot of money.
There are other beautiful Mosques to see, this one does not have a good peaceful feeling, it is dirty inside, and very poorly run.
So so so niceee.couples.. Families, tourists, business men... This place is bazodee... I really loved it's nature.... It's one of the best places I miss while am out of Cairo/aegypt..

The pyramids are nice, but all the overall experience is terrible. The "tours guides", that annoy you constantly, grab your tickets and follow you around for hours, even when you tell then to leave, to the garbage around the side, to the constant offers of camel/horse rides following you everywhere, people asking to help you to take pictures, but only wants tips, and many harassment if you are a women..The tombs and inside of the pyramids are a waste of time and money, there's nothing to see, the site is "small" nothing like the people will tell you, a far distance is like 1 km walking at most...They should care more of the site e make a more pleasant experience for the visitors...

Its a petty that this place has all these antiques and has a such bad old building its nice from outside but inside is a disaster old little lights hot place not much air conditioning in there!You will need someone to guide you and talk about things in there. You may spend min 1h 30 min in there. But you will see some stunning old antiques in there.
The avenue is closed. They are rebuilding it. There is a rope blocking you from walking on it. You can see a few Sphinx from behind the rope. Once it’s done it should be very nice.

Website says it's open till 7pm. This is not true. My first attempt was refused by guard at 4pm sharp. I chose another day and entered the museum. But at 5pm all guests were kicked out of museum. The stuff said, go buy another ticket (with a higher price) and you can enter again starting from 5:30pm.No proper lighting.I don't remember seeing fans instead of AC in any of the museum I visited in my life. My grandma could possibly saw one before.If they switch half of their effort selling camera tickets to manage this museum, it could be a lot better than now.

We decide to go to the Nubian Village as we had a boat for the entire day. We visited the Botanical Gardens first then here. We went for the colourful houses, which were plentiful. But we spent the whole time avoiding vendors trying to sell you trash and trinkets that were made somewhere far from here. It is a big tourist trap! We did have a nice lunch on the water.

One of the ‘Highly recommended’ tours of Cairo, but I found the experience highly unorganized… from confusing ticket purchase options, ( make sure you pay for the EXPRESS tickets, as even so, you will wait 20 minutes in line for the one elevator which only holds 6 persons. ) Normal ticket holders continuously get pushed behind EXPRESS ticket holders.Once at the top the view is remarkable and we caught our first glimpse the pyramids.

The fountain located in the middle of Soho Square with plenty of cafes and restaurants around.Shows have low quality compared to the fountains in Cairo, dance design is not matched with playing music.I didn't enjoyed it!

HORRIBLE STAFF. HORRIBLE POLICE. HORRIBLE PEOPLE. we were short on Egyptian pound because we were refused student tickets. We asked for USD exchange, but the guy wanted to take 90 Egyptian pound in his pocket. So we refused. We asked to just see and take a photo of the temple from the car park, which is public. The police shouted at us and said no photos. This is Luxor.

Many people may say this is the only reason to come to Aswan.  Well, that's not true.  Aswan has many other things to discover.Abu Simbel is 3 hours drive from Aswan, and these days there is no real reason to join the 4:00am convoy of mini-buses unless you want to see this on a budget.  An early start, say 5:00am is still recommended, especially in the summer, but if you take a private car that your local hotel can organize, then you can get there and back for 85 euros.  (plus the usual tips).On arriving at Abu Simbel, you first run the gamut of the touts to the ticket office, buy your ticket, and if you are honest, the EGP 300 for the licence to take indoor photos.  The entrance looks impressive, but not nearly as impressive as it would have been before it was moved back in the sixties.  The temple guards will give you the key to hold whilst they take your photo and collect the obligitary backsheesh, and then you enter.  Inside, the large temple is most impressive, and if you didn't bother spending 300EGP for the photo licence, slip one of the guards EGP20 and start clicking way.  It makes a bit of a mockery of it all.Go outside and do the same for the small temple, and then wander back and run the gamut of the souvenir shops and the relentless touts.  These are the worst that I had seen in all my time in Egypt.  I was relentlessly pursued by one trying to sell me his books, to the point when I got back to the cafe that the tourist police had to intervene.  One lady told me she would have liked to look at some of the things for sale, but was scared to show any interest.  It is all sad.The coffee shop sells ice creams and coffee etc at exhorbatant prices.  EGP40 for an ice cream which will cost you EGP4 back in Aswan.There are better monuments to see in Egypt, and if you have come here and stayed overnight, then you've really wasted your money.Interestingly, if you go to the Nubian Museum back in Aswan you can see a large model of where the original temples would have been, and where they are now.  They would have been very impressive originally, but not any longer.

Impressive for a drive by but no need to make special trip. You cannot spend more than 15 minutes there and is a stop off en route elsewhere. Do not let a local guide suggest as a distinct visit. Free of hawkers as is a small site so nice for that reason. Good to visit at night if happen to be passing
Sorry but this is not even close to NUBIAN village, this is only "village" of shops for only tourist purposes. If you expect to see nubian people here, turn around and walk away.
overpriced for the size of the place you visit. You basically are paying to go to a very small designer outlet.

We went to the Pyramids on a day trip, Thankfully with a very experienced guild, the sellers by the road are very pushy and I don't just mean in the way they trade they physically push you if you say no thanks, our guild came to our rescue and helped to get rid of them, they follow you around the whole time your trying to enjoy the site, it totally ruined the day for us, there is no security up there and it was quite scary, I would highly recommend to go only with a guild as if we we're by ourselves we would have been robbed or would have entered into physical violence, Pictures are not free to take even though they tell you they are, if anyone says ( it's our culture to give you free things) Don't listen because they then say ( it's your culture to pay) and they will not let you go until you give them pound notes as coin is not acceptable!!! Toilets are disgusting and have to be paid to use, the money is suppose to be used to keep the toilets clean and if that was the case I would happily pay but believe me they are far from clean, there is rubbish around the Pyramids, my advice is to get across the road as quickly as possible and never let them take your camera to take a picture of yourself, the 2 smaller Pyramids was not too bad for the street sellers so you can take more relaxed photo's, KFC and Pizza Hut are not as close to the Pyramids as people make out in fact our guild had to show us where they we're as we couldn't see them, the horses are in a horrendous state many are lame and just strapped up n made to run people up and down the streets they are very emaciated and we saw one being wiped for what we could see no reason not that there is ever a reason to wipe any animal,  although we had a very scary and bad experience there seeing the Sphinx was the Highlight of the trip if not the Holiday, the street sellers are not as pushy and we we're allowed time to just take in the magical feeling of ancient Egypt, would we go again yes we would knowing what we now no, but would ONLY go with a guild by our sides, it's a real shame as the street sellers are ruining it for many tourists and many would not return with other family members because of this reason.

My expectations for this Museum were set very high. There were only a few mummies on display (Yuya and Thuya), and most everything was being packed up for the new Museum in Giza closer to the Pyramids. Some highlights was the areas dedicated for King Tut and the larger statues and granite tombs. Social-distancing was not always possible with all the other tourists packed into the museum so be cautious.

Don’t waste your time. It’s old and broken down most things are shifting to another new place.We took a lot of photos but we don’t know of what. You can get a tour guide but they could be waste of time. Not sure if they are real tour guides or not.We paid to take photos then the best places they said we couldn’t!!Also people not dressed like they work there would tell you no photos and seems to be rude!Anyways here’s some of the photos we took enjoy save your time!

Traveled to Nubian village by boat in the afternoon, this was the best part of the trip and it went downhill from there.  Overall not worth the effort and just stick to a boat ride on the Nile.  The village is poor and the people are even poorer, they come at you from all sides trying to sell you something and don’t take no for answer, our guide took us to a Nubian house which is someone’s house to look in their bedroom ( very uncomfortable) and drink nasty tea.  After that you run to your boat before being overrun by locals.  Not a place for kids… everything else is Aswan was good, but skip the village.

Quite disappointed in the mosque.  Badly in need of cleaning and upkeep.  Interior light are a mess.  Nothing in comparison to Istanbul.  But the view from the patio - would be fantastic - if you could see beyond the dust and air pollution.  Not worth the visit.

As everything from the tomb is now re-located to the Egyptian Museum in Cairo, why would you spend any money to visit this tomb (I did so no matter in 2011 Arab Spring, but this was my first time here and it was only 100 EGP then!).  Stay away!!

Pyramids are impressive. I think it is the "must have" place to visit if you are in Egypt. BUT shame on Egyptian authorities who are totally indifferent to the way this ancient wonder is maintained. Actually it is not maintained at all. There are a lot of thieves and cheaters around the pyramids, territory is full of rubbish. People here make money by selling Chinese crap and cheap souvenirs and by cheating people. Seems like it is not hard to build some air conditioned tourist center near the pyramids where guests can order water, take a meal, buy official finely crafted merch, take an audio guide or simply have a rest after walking or visit wc. Entrance to the pyramids area itself is very cheap, I think a lot of tourists will be ready to pay more money for a good service with great pleasure. But for some reason there is no service at all. Moreover there is nothing around besides vendors with crap it is very hard to find a good tour. We bought a trip in Sharm. They organized a tour next way - we traveled by plane from Sharm to Cairo, then our "guide" took us to the museum in Cairo (there he tried to guide us, but in fact told only stuff from Wiki), made a stop in some dirty awful place (which he called a "restaurant", we were hardly even able to try something there), cheated us two times by telling he will take us to the "papyrus museum" (actually it's a store where besides items they have a girl with a hammer showing how they press it) and "perfume factory" (again a simple store, nothing is manufactured there, they only sell oil with x3 price), organized a boat trip for extra money (told we MUST go and pay because it is a part of route, but actually after boat trip we returned to the same place, so we could skip it indeed) and finally took us to the pyramids by their own bus. We paid $200+ per person!!! I described all this trip because we saw exactly the same route hundred of times while surfing for a tour. So I really expect this trip is sold to all guests by others providers. Please, if you read it and would like to see the pyramids in Giza, and you spend your vacation outside of Cairo, do organize your trip by yourself - transfer by plane will cost you around $60 per person, then you can take a taxi for a group of people from the Cairo airport to Giza. It will be much more cheaper than $200+ per person and your overall impression will not be spoiled. I rate this place as "terrible" not because of pyramids, but because people in Egypt were not able to setup proper service.

Crumbling statues, not in good shape, crowded site because all the tours stop there because it's free. Not worth going out of your way to see. Looking out the window of the taxi as you drive by is more than enough.

So you decide to go, you get up full of vim and vigor, then wait for the driver (he's on Egyptian time)you get in, then you wait, cause the traffic is deplorable, finally you get the eager to get out and experience the delights as described in the brochure, but, the filth and pollution on the ground, the reality is not what you will expect, go and see the oil on the surface of the main harbor its more exciting.

They're big but barely recognizable. Compared to most of the sites these haven't survived the test of time well. There's very little at this site other than the statues. We spent less than ten minutes there.

Me and my girlfriend had booked three months ago a tour via internet to Sakkara pyramid, Dashour and Giza pyramids. After first two, we reached with our guide at Giza. There we were told that will have a tour either will camel or horse without our guide but with a new one from another company. There we saw an abandoned area with 50 horses and camels, really exhausted, waiting for us to decide what tour we need. After thinking we decided to have camel riding as my gf is in love with them and she thought it’s a must. Agreement for 55$ per person for the long camel ride for pyramids,Sphinx and panoramic view mountain and there we go. We start with Problems with the camels as because the tour guide hit strong them and the horse he had, so the animals didn’t like us and tried three times throw us down. We asked him to stop it otherwise we would go back. After what he did the whole time was two things. Photographs for me and my gf for 1 minute at the panoramic view “mountain”, and then showings us which pyramid is the first, the second and the last. Only this. At the end, he asked 4 times for tip, really unpleasant and rude, and of course they didn’t accepted card payment. We had to go to find cash mashine. We paid 2000 Egyptian pounds, extra the entrance fee, and When our first tour guide got back, we informed him for everything. He insisted for us to take money back and the boss of the useless camel guide, gave us 600 eg pounds back. Really bad service from that guys and it’s a pity for Egypt to have these people in such a beautiful and important place.

At the entrance control we were advised that each camera costs 300 EGP admission. That's why we left them there for safekeeping. At the ticket office we wanted to buy a normal ticket for 300 EGP per person, after which we were made aware of long waiting times. The express ticket was too expensive for us. We left the premises immediately.

They would not let us enter the car park which was reserved for groups, tours and employees. We had to park in a backstreet and had small children and a pushchair. This was bad considering the car park was empty. The Palace and Military Museum were closed but you did not know that until approaching and by then had paid 60 EGP fee. The Mosque was interesting but very dirty. Gardens were well kept

The ancient temple and monuments are worth seeing but when you plan your visit, please, please don’t use the horse drawn carriages to get to and from the river to the monument. The appalling starved state of the poor horses and the cruelty inflicted on them is highly distressing and will detract from your visit.  Please insist your tour company or guide arranges a taxi or minibus to get you to and from the temple.
Very small and not worth the money.Although to say you have seen it, is worth the money maybe?

The "houses" are basically cafés where you can take a picture of little crocodile with band around it's mouth so it does not bite, and then put it back in the tiny cage. The "village" is like any other Egyptian city's outskirts with markets selling the same tourist kitch as everywhere else. Yeah, the boat ride is nice, but you can get 1 hour sail on a felucca for 100 EGP after bargaining a bit with any of the captains that hunt you on the riverfront in Aswan (Corniche street). Paying for this as a organized tour (I did, $30) is a rip off.

It is small, dirty and full of stalls with vendors trying to sell you something from China. Skip this stop and spend time with the alabaster shops. That you will go by on the way back to your hotel. They are much more friendly, they want you to buy, but at least it is made there. Offer half the asking price and settle for a 40% markdown.

For what it was I think it was overpriced . The tower is small and it was crowded.  We got the fast pass for an extra 100 Egyptian pounds and I would not have paid that for it, but then there was a line . Overall after going up there for the money I probably would have passed.  The view is nice from the top, but you're literally up there for 2 min.  The elevator only holds like 6 people at a time and with COVID packing in was a bit uncomfortable.  Think being from Chicago going up to a tower like this with the view doesn't quite compare to the Sears Tower nor Hancock building, but nice in it's own right, but this one seems to be a thirst trap.I also was not allowed to take in my camera and they made me leave it with in a locker, I begged not to leave it and that I'll leave the SD card along with the batter as mines a very top of the line DSLR camera and they said no and that it would be safe. When we returned it was fine, but then we were asked for 10 pounds to pay off the guard?   Definitely not worth the trip for the money.

We were very surprised by the poor restoration work performed on this temple.  While it is spectacular from afar, as one gets closer it is clear that much of the temple is restored material, about 40%, and in a way that resembles more Disneyland, than a proper historic monument.  However, Hatshepsut has a an interesting history and appears to have been a clever politician.

Was clearly once a great museum but at the moment is being emptied of products which are going to the new building across town.Still charging full price, though.However, if you are happy to wander around amongst scaffolding, fork lift trucks, ladders and builders packing up the antiquities you have paid full price to see it IS the place for you.Careful of the training wires, painters and floor finishers though.Sadly, what is not yet boxed and polythened is rarely annotated so you have no idea what you are actually looking at.They just seem to have lost the plot. What could have been a wonderful experience was a waste of time and money

This museum, while updating is desperately needed, has all the things one wants to see of ancient Egypt.  The items are enough to astound, (King Tuts Tomb), and so much more.  What is horribly missing is signage.  Many of the items have little or no descriptions.  We had a guide with us, so she explained many of the things we saw.  They are in the process of building a new museum to house all of these lovely treasures.  However, I would not have missed this museum for the world.  Definitely worth seeing.  Perhaps when the new museum is finished, I believe 2020, it would be something not to be missed and may require a second visit for us.

Currently the main event, the Step Pyramid, is under renovation. Covered in scaffolding.Completely overrun by the most aggressive touts I've ever seen. We normally smile, thank you, keep walking. We were literally cornered and angry.Most tours take you here before Giza, but I'd scrap it altogether and just go to Giza. Then could get there earlier in the day (cooler temp) which would have been a much better option in the 45+ summer heat.
smelly camel poor experience with rubbish near the site no respect for the culture or dead don't bother save your money

As impressive as the pyramids are, the constant badgering of the hoards of touts gets very irritating, to the point that it's hard to really enjoy being there. Even inside one of the pyramids a guy tried to get money from us because he told us a couple of facts about the pyramid. Spent more time telling people I didn't want a camel ride/my photo taken/them to take my camera out of my hand to photograph me, than looking at the sights. Basically don't engage with anyone or you've had it.

We've just back from our vacation. A day of it was going to the Pyramids & Sphinx. First of all, you do not want to do this on your own. Go with a tour.  When you get to the pyramids, there are quite a few street vendors that will come up to you constantly and try to sell you their stuff. The Egyptian police are thick here, and will keep running these people away from you but when they run 2 away, 3 more come around. You will see police on foot and on camel.  My husband had to put his fist in the face of one of the vendors & threathen to hit him if he didn't leave us alone. They will not leave you alone no matter how many times you say no.  At the Sphinx, you won't have that problem and there are no police here because they are not needed.   Make sure you have a hat here. We were here in mid May and it was already 100 degrees.   Other than that, these things are just awesome.

The museum being reviewed here is NOT the new, Grand Egyptian Museum which is on the Giza Plateau but the old, dusty one in the centre of Cairo.  This city-centre museum has the more humble title of The Egyptian MuseumTripadvisor has been peddling this error for several years and it is high time that it stopped misleading its readers.Huff-Huff!I look forward to seeing revie

Don't be fooled that this is anything other than a chance to pester and sell you more worthless souvenirs, something we avoid whilst in Egypt because tourists are CONSTANTLY mobbed to buy the same tat. It is no more like a 1000 year old Nubian village than Disney. People didn't live like that; and certainly didn't  have the mobile phones and laptops that you see in use everywhere there.We got tricked into visiting this place, as part of a tour, albeit a small one. The itinerary was meant to  include a trip to the Botanical Gardens which we were looking forward to, but which magically got "changed". We were in Egypt for 2 weeks on an R&R- combined- with- Egyptology basis. By the end we became intolerant of finding that almost every tour(no matter how small or upmarket a group) diverted to unwanted and unannounced tourist traps like this. Examples in Luxor included an "alabaster factory" or "papyrus factory" and so on. If you like this sort of thing then go; but we don't and object strongly to being misled.It is worth a motor boat ride along this very interesting stretch of the Nile though. Avoid the Feluccas unless its windy. Allow about half an hour upstream from Aswan itself. There is some fantastic bird life.If you are unsteady on your feet or have mobility problems you will find this place, and the boat, impractical.
Be prepared for the crowds no wonder there building a new one this place looks like it’s stuck in a time warp it’s got stuff everywhere very over rated

dirty, boiling hot, unstructered....  items are not well preserved and a lot got stolen during the uprise, resulting in many empty rooms. tuth-ankh-omun's golden mask is great and they do have still some unique and interrtesting objects, but the presentation is 'worthless'! the museom burned during the war and it is still a shambles!!!!!!

Yes this is incredibly beautiful but the price has gone up again, it's now 1400 LE, £70 or nearly 90US$, I checked the price online in advance and didn't see anything indicating the price hike, and I'd drawn 2000 LE in cash for both Nefertiti and Seti 1.  You don't see the notice telling you 'only 10 mins inside the tomb' till after you've paid then you get rushed through by the tomb guardian and pushed out again when you've barely had time to look it all over once.  I understand the reasons for limiting entry but it should be made clear what you get for the price before you pay up so you can decide.  I didn't bother with Seti 1, I'd have had to find an ATM to draw more cash and I felt I'd been ripped off once.

We was so excited to go to this lovely mosque. Not an excellent place but it was ok! Itis not the most important thing to visit! And be careful because they are trying to lying you

I don’t know if it was an “off day” or “off season” or what, but this visit was a complete waste of time and funds.  The only reason I am posting 2 stars is because the boat ride there and back was nice.  Our Nubian boat driver was warm and hospitable and pleasant.  His smile was like sunshine!  The actual Nubian Village was boring.  You walk through the center that had a bunch of China-made items and even the vendors appeared to be missing. Finally, you are brought fresh bread, molasses, local cheese, some halva, and mint tea.   Big deal.  No conversation, no sightseeing, no nothing.  Do yourselves a favor and skip this one!

half the jets didnt work and just wasnt all that good its was just a fountain with lights show was not set to the music

The pyramids at Giza were great but our visit was spoiled by a dishonest person at the gate who said he worked there.We were there Dec 31/09 . We hired a taxi to take us to Giza.  Instead he dropped us off at some business premise about 1/2 mile from back entrance to Giza, which is in front of Pizza Hut and KFC outlet.  This fellow at the business premise came out and invited us in and tried to make it sound like he was doing us a favor by outlining the route that we'd take to see the pyramids.  We promptly left as we didn't want a tour or guide.  We had no idea where the entrance was and stumbled along the back way until we found the entrance in front of KFC.We are a 66 and 55 yr old couple.  As soon as we entered the gate after paying admission, this Egyptian by the name of Abdullah  (probably not real name) attached himself to us.   We said we didn't want a guide or tour.  This fellow said yes, he knows that but it's his job to take us and guide us, not to worry, no charge, it's his job.  We thought this was weird and wondered if there was some rule if you're not in a tour maybe you can't walk around on your own.  Abdullah ingratiated himself on us by offering to take pictures of us and by posing a certain way in front of the pyramids and sphinx.  He led us where he wanted us to go and follow.  He let us know that he knew the tourist police there by greeting them warmly like an old friend who worked with them daily, pats on the back and so on.  There were two underground crypts that he informed us we had to pay a tip of 50-60 LE each to the male attendees stationed at the entrance.  When we went in there were no tour groups there, just us.   It took about an hour and 15 minutes to circle the Khufu - Cheops pyramid and we (husband and I ) talked amongst ourselves that we wanted to get rid of him and be on our own but that we should tip him something.  We both agreed that $20 US would be fair.  So we informed Abdullah that we wanted to be on our own.  My husband at first gave him Egyptian money but he said he wanted US money.  He then gave him 20 US and thanked him.  Abdullah then informed him that that wasn't enough, he wanted more money.  My husband then reluctantly gave him another 20 US and he said that that wasn't enough.  By this time he extracted another 10 US for a grand total of 50 US and Abdullah seemed content.   Now you must understand we didn't want any trouble with the tourist police who wear guns or have anything to do with the Egyptian police.... we just wanted to get rid of this leech.  We were so DISGUSTED and it ruined our visit to this beautiful site.We initially wanted to see the light show that evening but after what happened we felt the place was so sleazy and creepy we left and didn't bother.Since coming back from our trip I've been reading other people's exploits and realize we SHOULD have called the tourist police but I just didn't trust anyone there, especially since they all seemed to be in cahoots together with the patting each other on the backs and so forth.   I realize now it's all orchestrated amongst themselves.I want other people to know what we went through so they don't have to go through the same thing and get stung like we did.If you don't want a guide, no matter what they say, "free" "no worries" don't BELIEVE it, keep them away from you at all costs.Rose

Please note it is extremely annoying and long to reach by foot, the entrance is far from everything. And to leave the area taxis just propose rip-off prices, and Uber drivers have it difficult to stop by...So surprised with the reviews of this place! It feels almost like a tourist trap, extremely expensive for what it is. Thankfully it was included in the Cairo Pass we had, as the only worthwhile thing is the view. I must say that the treasures are not treated as such. The building and artifacts are kept in poor conditions. Undeserving. Staff unprofessional and ready to accept bribes and allow rules to be broken.

We visited the citadel on a holiday - we knew it was a holiday but there were no signs or any other information saying that this would impact the opening hours and a lot of people were still piling in when we arrived (3.40ish). We paid the full price and got in only to discover NOTHING was open and people were starting to be herded out. We were disappointed with this and so asked for our money back. We believe this was a reasonable request and should have been easily sorted. Not to be- everyone so unhelpful saying "welcome to Egypt" but not able to offer any reasonable solution. Absolutely shocking treatment.  Also when we came back on here to read the other terrible reviews we realised we were scammed as well - we thought we had given the ticket office 200 but when were asked for more and it turns out it was a 100. Of course we thought this was our mistake and paid the difference, however we really were convinced that we gave 200 (as this was what at ATM issued). An absolute scam and we would not come back nor recommend it.
Longish line, no stairs to walk (just boring elevator), and just a tall building - better to just see from the outside.

The majestic statue once stood in the heart of Cairo in Rameses square. However, it has been moved to the site of the new museum which is expected to open in a couple of years. The statue is covered with wool and wooden panels for protection and is not clear for tourists.
This museum is so old it is dirty nothing is organized. People compared to one in Paris the Paris museum is an amazing place this is the biggest dump I’ve ever seen

This is by far the most unpleasant and creapy turist attraction we have ever experienced.On you way in you will become the hunted pray.No facilities and 98% locals doing everything to pres you (beyond any  resonable, or UNreasonable limit).Felt hunter even before we bought our tickets.Stayed there for 40 minuts before we had to escape at (almost) any cost.We learned nothong abort this great historycal place,  but a lot about how low humans will go for (un)deserved tips.Been there, done that, and for sure: A place never to go again.
Not as spectacular as it was made out to be, And it was only intermittent. The shops in Soho Square and the atmosphere were good though

They just escorted us through this Nubian village and there were absolutely nothing to see. we went here in the day and it was amazing our guide hady was very knowledgeable however on the last night of our cruise we returned for the sound and light show. this was a complete waste of £25 each. firstly we were marched at speed some distance from the site entrance to the temple and then left for some time b4 the event started. then we just had to follow the lights and sounds with no guides. the voices were very flat and boring so of the light shows were very disco like. we then walked a distance again were sat in rows for more lights and sounds. everyone was saying how bad it was as we walked out. we were held up at 1 point by the next show people entering. this was the only event we found disappointing in our 2 weeks holiday

We went to the pyramids on new years eve the place is like a dream,  too bad it is not well organized,  first when you go to pay if you are arabic and have your arabic ID with you,  you will pay a very small amount but if you are not the ticket is really high,  then qhen you go in people there will do what ever it takes to get money to get money from you no matter what you tell them and they will try to trick you so be careful.
