If you have seen catacombs in Paris, this is very disappointing. It's totally empty and apparently often partially flooded although the day we visited, it had recently been pumped. Ten Philae Temples, once located on Philae Island, were moved to near by Agilkia Island, a higher island in the Nile River that was not affected by rising water levels after the building of the High Dam.  At the quay on Agilikia Island an information board provided details of this move done with the help of  UNESCO.  Diagrams showed how river water around Philae Island had been pumped out after a barrier had been erected around the island. Ten Philae temples had been cut into 40,000 pieces and then transported to Agilkia Island where they were re-pieced together.Philae Island had been an important religious site for the Egyptians and the Nubians as it was one of Osiris’s burial places. Only priests, no commoners were allowed on the island; thus it was surrounded by a lot of mystery. Pagan gods had been worshiped in these temples until 550 when Justinian forbade such worship and allowed Coptic Christians to use the temple as a church.While ten temples had been saved from being submerged, other, older religious structures that were left on the island have disappeared from view.  All that I could see of Philae Island on my visit to the Philae Temples on Agilkia Island was  outcropping of some rocks. Only the most adventurous would be interested to climb on those rocks to try to find traces that Greco-Romans may have left behind. Tourists now focus their attention on Agilkia Island to visit the temples that were removed to that island. To get to Philae or Agilkia Islands take a 10 minute ferry boat ride from the marina close to the British Dam.
Alas... not open yet, scheduled first quarter of 2019. Preview looks sensational. Will be one of the (if not the) biggest musea in the world.

I'm glad most folk enjoyed it. I thought it was rubbish. We paid $110 for a taxi from Cairo, $100 a night for a very average room for two nights at the Morgenland (plus $2 for a bottle of water!!!!!) and then found that the monastery was closed. Pyramids itself: Amazing!Locals who constantly want to sell something: I dont mind, I expected that already.Beating a camel with sticks to make it obey you: Can not let that slide.The Pyramids is an awesome worldwide known tourist site, visited by millions of people a year. Why are there still so many people who support the absolute animal abuse that goes on there? DO NOT RIDE THE ANIMALS! I saw the ribs on some of the horses and again two guys almost beating the life out of a camel. Its a horrifying sight and the sound of that whip with the scream of that poor animal still ring through my head.

I visited Cairo back in April with a trip to the Pyramids. Yes, I enjoyed the Pyramid site very much. The city of Cairo and getting to the Pyramids is a trash land. I imagine there's no room in the landfill or no trash service. Heaps - and I mean mountains - of trash line the roads going to the Pyramids. It's quite disgusting and I don't know why the Egyptian government doesn't take care of the problem since the Pyramids are then #1 attraction of Egypt.I visited Jordan during the same trip. If I would've known what a disappointment Cairo was and that I was only going to spend a few hours at the Pyramids, I would've saved my money and stayed longer in Jordan.

If you're there it is a must see. But I will say IMHO I was disappointed. I thought it would be an amazing experience but I just didn't get the vibes I expected. Walk in, look at this little shelf on the left where his mummy was, look at a large empty room where the treasures were; that's about it. Just standing in wonder in The Valley of the Kings was an amazing experience but if you have to stand in line or go out of your way it wasn't worth it other than saying, "I was there".

I would not recommend the sound and light show. We found it to be poor value for money. Visiting Karnak early in the day and spending the whole day there would be much better than visiting the sound and light show.
Really not worth your money. The only reason I rated it was that I could see for the first time Tut's Mummy.
If you've seen the dancing fountains in Vegas or Dubai, you will be very underwhelmed.As the fountains are in the middle of Soho, you are likely to catch a glimpse anyway.

Of the Kings tombs in the Valley of the King's this is the smallest, and is only famous because of what was found in here 100 years ago.After a period of unrest as a boy Tutankhamun was really not much of a king, the publicity is better than the tombs reality, not worth the extra fee.

I would rate this terrible just because of the hawkers trying to sell you things who are extremely agressive in the whole vicintity. They will walk up right in your face and repeatedly as for services. They will also charge extremely high pries which the local propulation would not pay.The inside of the pyramid are worthless to see - its a closed room of stone with no windows. its also a suffocation and the way they run it is a stampede hazard.The best way to enjoy the pyramids is to drive up and observe them from a distance and let the howkers know you are not interested in their service.

I was very disappointed with my experience at the Pyramids. Nonexistent organization in any sense, most notably in terms of sighns, on-site information and restoration visualisations. You can wonder around for hours not knowing where you are going or how to get there besides crossing directly vast distances under the scorching sun on foot while being chased relentlessly by annoying salesmen. Decline politely (if your nerves hold) ANY "gift" or "free" stuff, ANY friendly interaction, offer or compliment. Nothing is free, nothing is "good price" and noone ever does anything from the goodness of their heart. I'm not saying they are bad people. They are just there to make a living and you are their means to do it. Don't fall for it because they will suck you dry. Don't allow them to get you onto a camel for "a good picture" unless you are willing to pay for it or if you refuse to - pay to get off the camel as they are pretty aggressive. Don't ask for directions or information, you will end up surrounded by at least 3 people working together to get your money. As a last resort to get rid of someone extremely annoying, after repeatedly telling them you are not interested or you don't want the gift they want money for, you can tell them they are ruining your visit and that you will call Tourist Police. They are found all over the place and their job is to protect you in such cases. Anyway, I wouldn't recommend going but if you will, go with a guide and stay phisically near them so you will be less vulnerable.

Dont go out of your way to see these two cracked statues, if the temple was still there behind them, it would be a different matter, we found that the hassle from the locals was excessive and not necessary.
This nondescript monument isn't worth stopping for. You can see many more at other sites in the area so focus your energy elsewhere.

A lot of litter in the sea, which we had to swim through, on one of the 2 beaches we snorkelled on during a half day trip there. Could not believe the excursion companies do not have the litter cleaned up, considering the popularity of the trip. I picked up a load myself

Unfortunately, I cannot provide a review of the museum or its collection. Me (a foreign tourist) and my friend (Egyptian national) arrived at the museum on July 28 2023 hoping to see the art. There were security guards sitting near the entrance - once they saw my friend was a local, they asked to see his id and said they’re gonna hold his documentation until our visit is over. They introduced themselves as police - despite not wearing any formal clothes or badges. Naturally, we wouldn’t leave identification documentation with the random person for hours without any confirmation of this fact so we left. No visit. Shame on the museum treating people that want to marvel at art like that. And I missed on the museum too - and this was my only chance to go. I do not recommend this place if you want to be treated with respect.

While visiting this site I had the continuous feeling of fakery, OK the village existed before the tourist and there are the remains of original life but tourism has overtaken this. Is this a good visit, it will depend on your views as to who is being exploited.
This is simply another neglected treasure in Cairo. The place is a gem in the heart of Islamic Cairo, but is very unorganized and severely humiliated. The bathrooms are terrible and nonhuman.
You have to pay extra for this trip but there its nuffink in it , its all in cairo musuem so its not worth paying extra for .

Having been looking forward to seeing the pyramids I got quite a shock when I got there.As soon as you go through the security check you will be pestered until you leave by people trying to sell cheap tacky souvenirs or simply hassling you for money. Apart from the police, EVERYONE IS GOING TO PESTER, PLAGUE AND ANNOY YOU FOR MONEY. I paid to go inside the great pyramid and had to leave my camera with the security guard at the entrance, on exiting the pyramid the security guard holding onto my camera said "I look after your camera, you give me money".Every time I tried to take a photo or a video someone would try to  walk into the shot and then ask for money because they were in your picture.These people were like mosquitoes in the way that they swarmed around the tourists and every bit as annoying.Considering they would have to have passed through security, the constant pestering completely ruined what should have been a fascinating experience and they should be removed from the area.

The government or someone needs to do something about the management of this place! It is baffling that Egypt has one of the 7 wonders of the world in its own backyard, but have it run by a bunch of imbeciles. The pyramids and sphinx themselves are magical! Truly a masterpiece and they always leave me startled.BUT GOOD LUCK if you decide to ride a horse or camel around there. It is a complete mess. Not only will you get ripped off (most likely), but the animals are also beat down and treated poorly by their captors. It is simply unfair and it was saddening t to see it happen before my eyes.

You need to find a well informed guide to explain all about the catacombs.  It is not well kept and needs signs, brochures, maybe a whole new approach to make it more interesting as the story behind them is fascinating yet it needs lots of attention to make it worth going there.
it's developed the last few years. Today it's more like a tivoli. So if you enjoy a lot of people, traffic and yelling - it's the place for you.

Although the exterior is rather impressive, despite the grime covering the alabaster walls, the interior is a hideous pastiche of the great Mosques of Istanbul and Edirne. The view from the Citadel is the best reason for a visit. For those interested in bad deals: the broken clock that the French traded to Egypt for one of the obelisks at the Luxor temple is displayed in the courtyard - it has never worked!

I would expect a $20 ticket to get you into a well organized modern museum with plenty of information or at least a leaflet with a map and basic info, hopefully ventilation, toilets or water dispensers. Everything was left as it was created when it was created. There was broken glass, typewriter-written signs from 30-40 years ago, missing information all over the place. The only part or the museum that was somewhat well done was the Yuya and Tuya exposition. If you need to go to the toilet you have to get out of the museum and to the restaurant, leave a tip even though it's free, get back in again, argue with the guards why your ticket is already torn 3 times and hope you won't have trouble with the military there if you by any chance get a bit frustrated by the whole thing.
I was disappointed. For the cost of 100 EGP to enter I expected to see more. It was a small room. Save the entry cost it was over rated. The display to see is at the Cairo museum
statues are in a poor state, but I suppose they can be interesting for someone who cares about history of art. otherwise, you can spend five minutes here on your way to the Valley of Kings.

Not on the hotel list of things to do so booked independent from Hurgada, 24 euro taxi return for the three of us, not so bad, camera packed watwer on board and off we go, pulled into the gate and there is not another vehicle or person in the car park, anyway paid the 15 euros EACH to entry, and |I asked where do we get on the pedal boats, no boats in maintenance, also the water in the waterways was very low, and a big mechanical digger was banging away in one corner, digging for .....water.No where to eat as no restaurant was open, also to drink was just the cans 2 euros each, bear in mind here the exchange rate is 200-1 approx, so 400 Egyptian pounds for a tin of drink !!.If you get chance visit senso mall shopping, good supermarket with realistic prices.On this trip id say pass and find something better.Would recommend the quad bikes, Periscope boats, Diving, day boat trips but do yourself a favor and pass this one over.

I was looking forward to the most prized relicts of Egyptian culture to find them treated like a junkyard. The building has seen better times, probably not been maintained for 50+ years. I have been to many museum large and small in countries rich and poor, but this Museum treats its artifacts with so little love, pride and care that it is shocking. I came with great excitement and left sad and with a sense of defeat.There is no context provided, labels are minimal and guides are a joke. I stood next to a large stone with inscription and listened to three different guides give three completely different explanations. The just told their audience what they through would entertain them best.There are ancient clothes pieces hanging in dusty display cases from rusty hooks. The cloth clearly ripping and disintegrating because of the way it is “displayed “.Most artifacts lack any description and are stacked in display cases that probably date back to the original opening day of the museum.Please don’t get me wrong, There are beautiful treasures in this museum, but the way they are treated is truly appalling.

Our trip up Mt Sinai was a bit of a disappointment. We are a family of 2 adults and two kids aged 8 and 11. We are all relatively fit and 18th months ago all of us managed to hike up Mt Teide (Tenerife) which is more than 1km higher. So we were not worried about fitness levels, or the kids being able to make it. However where I would recommend Mt Teide (if you have the strength and stamina), I’m not so sure with Mt Sinai.These are my reasons why:•	Almost a thousand people climb up every night. There is no peace, no quiet, no harmony with nature.•	The locals keep trying to sell you something (the camel guides were by far the worst). Given that there were at least a hundred people that tried to offer us a product or a service, this trip was more of a visit to a bazaar than a hike in Nature.•	I don’t know where everyone else was brought up, but everyone litters profusely. With so many people, you are constant walking on bottle tops. I wish that Egypt would implement the plastic bottle refund system present in the Nordic countries. If returning each bottle earned someone 1LE, the mountain would so much cleaner.•	The toilets are few, dirty and expensive $1 US or 5 LE. The implication of this can be seen once the sun has risen. Step away from the path and you are walking through human excrement everywhere.•	The camel herders are rude and unpleasant. Our kids were keen to try riding on the camel, so eventually we agreed on a price with one of the camel herders and our youngest climbed on. The intention was that midway he would swap with our oldest. The camel herder then promptly sped off. We asked our guide what was happening and he said that he would drop off our son near the top of the mountain where he would have to wait for us. Well we were not going to leave an eight year old on a mountain top by himself at night, so we tried to catch up. Once we caught up, the camel herder said that we will have to pay extra if we want him to walk at our pace. In his haste to extract the extra payment from us by making the camel go as fast as it would he nearly knocked off a couple of elderly walkers with walking sticks off the mountain twice. Both time rudely shouting at the couple to get out of his way.•	After a night of hiking and then a day of visiting the monastery and driving back, it takes another day to recover. With only a week in Egypt, I’m not sure if it was the most constructive use of our time.The few positives were:•	It is nice to have coffee shops distributed along the route.•	Renting out blankets on top is a nice touch. It is very cold waiting for the son rise. Although you can hike up with a thin jumper because you are constantly exercising, once you stop moving it gets very cold.With so many people hiking up the mountain, the whole religious experience is lost. It was very much like what I imagine when I read Matthew 21:12-13 where Jesus chases out the sellers and moneychangers out of the temple.

Family of 3 visiting these incredible buildings. They are truly spectacular.My low rating comes from the experiences around the pyramids, rudeness from the horse carriage guy and his treatment of his horses. And the world’s most aggressive and annoying souvenir sales guys. We booked a horse carriage to the other side of the pyramids. What a mistake! We were 4 people in the carriage. The old horse was really working hard in the deep sand off road. The whip was used several times on the poor horse. After we got back and tipped the carriage driver he just laughed and said it wasn’t enough. His boss even came to put pressure on me and said many tourists paid 10 € per person. That really pushed my buttons and I told him I make the decision about tips, not him. He backed off and I walked away with my family. Very bad experience.If you are not particularly interested in the pyramids my advice is to stay away. Yes they are spectacular but time, wind and sand has put its mark on them.We stayed in Hurghada and went there by bus. It was a 7 hour drive excluding two breaks where all buses where meeting before continuing.A 20 hour tour all in all. I can’t recommend that.There are more interesting things to see like Luxor and Valley of the Kings.If you go, prepare mentally for aggressive behavior from locals around the pyramids.

Only a building and a sea, nothing to see there, not worth to come to it. People said there is an aquarium beside it the is huge but i missed it and didn't know about it. So maybe see the aquarium and the fort together.

This is not a visit but an attraction. Prepared for tourists so not really that interesting.They take you in a longboat (where kids arrive singing in floating boards and they make you dance before you get beaded bracelets and necklaces displayed for sale) and then in a motorboat. After aproximately 30 minutes you arrive to a beach where you can bathe in the Nile.You are then taken to a camel ride before you arrive to the nubian village. Even when the service is paid a tips included they will ask for extra cash...You are then taken into a house, well prepared for tourists, where alive baby crocodiles are shown... Then you go up to a terrace to see the village from above, you are invited to tea and sweets and wait your turn to get a henna design painted on your skin...And then you are taken back in the motorboat...I do recommend getting some spices from the market before you leave. Careful with the final price... 1 egyptian pound (less than 10cents) per gram adds up very quickly to 50€ if you get carried out by low prices... They have a lot of choice with extremely good quality

This museum has so many artefacts but it is very badly organised with pieces stacked up together.  Parts of the museum, especially on the ground floor look more like a store room!  That said, the history in here is truly outstanding.  The mummy room is fascinating as is the Tutankhamun's mask.  I would recommend a visit here if you are in Cairo

I can't understand how a museum holding some of the most important historical discoveries ever can be so terribly set up. No signage, poor labeling, scruffy display cases and poor lighting. You pay 75ep to get in and then you have to pay an extra 100ep to see the mummies, which they don't tell you until you are already inside.All in all, this is a must see in Cairo due to the finds it holds, but be prepared for a really rubbish museum.

When we got to the ticket office the man behind the counter tried to give us the wrong change. Be careful and check your change.The exhibits are amazing, but the museum is badly kept and it's a shame that there is not the money to display everything properly.

I know it is must to visit the main museum in Cairo but apparently this period of year is a transitional period for the museum and everything is moving to the new grand museum in Giza. I’ve never been in the museum before so I have no idea if the situation was the same before. Although there is a vast variety of different eras in Egyptian history. Highlight of the visit is Golden Mask of Tuttankhamoon which luckily is still in the old museum and under strict supervision (no photos). The building is old and has its own charm and character. Nice staircase and columns, great place for showing history. Unfortunately there is a lack of café/ resting place , there is only one gift shop in a badly managed situation and licking roof! Gift shop is at the exit way of the museum and expensive items compared to the ones in bazaar.

Yes, it's grubby, dusty and ill-kempt but then this is Egypt. I think it adds to the flavour. Tutenkamun's mask kept me enthralled as did the process of mummification. To think that Rameses II was staring back at me albeit rather leathery and hollow eyed was an amazing feat. Take Cairo with a pinch of dust and avoid disappointment.
If going there make sure you also go on the sound and light show at night. That where pretty amazing. Where there during the day too and the temple is really something to see.

The men who work at the pyramids are pushy and rude, simply after a tip.  One actually cornered me inside of the tomb into which we had climbed.  His rude behavior left a sour taste in my mouth for what should have been a fantastic site.
The history is interesting and the view of the city from the top is worth the walk. The rest of it is just so-so in my opinion.
If you're in Cairo you must see this statue.  It is breathtaking, although the surrounding grounds and city leave much to be desired....
Is a must for the Giza trip but nothing special.You can visit and learn but with a good tour guide.

Probably a really good museum once but now it's rather a case of faded glory.  Lots of good stuff in there, but a lot of it is very badly displayed.  Full of old wood & glass display cases, and rather poor out-of-date labelling.  Lots of stuff has gone as it is in the process of being moved to the new Grand Egyptian Museum.  However there are some impressive highlights, including statues and mummies and (in its own gallery) most of the contents of Tutankhamun's tomb, including the world-famous death-mask. You can't take photos in that room, but you can in the rest of the galleries, provided you purchase a camera permit with your entry ticket.  It's a vast place, so hard to do it full justice in just one visit. Once all the stuff is in the new GEM this place will become redundant.

This is one of the most great Egyptian attractions that one must see, but I believe that it needs proper facilities for tourists such as : clean and decent toilets,  nice cafes, good restaurants and trusted antique shops.I felt as going to a military castle due to the enormous number of security gates and soldiers .I only recommend a quick visit to the sight and do not visit any of the cafes and resturants there.

I went last month to visit Mohammad Ali mosque in Cairo. That was my first time to go there and i was really shocked. The place is in very poor condition. The place contains 3 places; the mosque, national military museum and the police museum. The mosque is in very poor condition and the the military museum is closed for maintenance and the police museum is small and not bad. I felt sad to see the mosque in that condition and i hope it will improve.
... but too many snorkellers being shepherded for my liking
Stargazing in the Sinai mountains was spoilt by a full moon. Surprised at the amount of rubbish strewn around

Because if you saw gems around - Hatshepsut temple, Kom Ombo temple, Karnak temple and especially Luxor temple - these two huge "uncles" will not take your breath away.We were on a private tour in Luxor and Aswan (the Nile cruise) so just made a stop, took a couple of pictures and drove further on.Sorry, old chaps.

You must go see the Pyramids sometime in your life, but one thing you don't need to do is ride a camel.  Other people said the same thing, but our driver said it's not safe unless we do the guide by horse or camel.  This isn't true, you can walk around yourself, just dont take too much cash with you and watch your wallet.  If you really don't want something say know... or they will keep trying to convince you other wise.  In general, Egypt isn't the safest place right now, but the Pyramids were very safe with lots of guards.

The museum is not very well looked after and it does not really tell a story. It looks more like a large storage warehouse than a museum. If it wasn't for my pocket tour book of Egypt I would not Know what I am looking at. The extra money for the Mummies are worth it!
Expensive for what it is. Like many things in Egypt. It is a mosque like so many others.Be careful, take water with you otherwise they will charge you a high price for water.
Great  to see all the artifacts but poor labeling.  Pay the extra for mummy room..   You can cover the museum in     2 hours..

2.5-3 hr excursion done my local Nubian man. One of the last surviving Nubian Villages here, most were relocated due to building of dam. Bird watching otw to Nubian village. See Aga Khan mausoleum and Old Cataract Hotel along the way. Watch subset on the back (beautiful!). Nubians villagers were watching soccer while we visited. Seems to enjoy slow way of life. We liked tour well enough but may not choose to take the tour again as you may not see enough..
In comparison to the other landmarks I visit, this was underwhelming.  We stopped for a quick visit and photos and that was good enough
Went on sand buggy trip. Make sure you take water and scarves with you as this will cost you £8 on the all inclusive trip!!

The colossi don't need you to get out of your bus but all tourism companies stop at those impressive statues to get some photos , they're big and they stood in front of a huge temple which's destroyed now but work is done to get it again repaired , they are big and amazing!deserves 10 minutes
- very nice place for historical photography- only open up to 4 pm , so it’s terrible to visit during noon and after noon hot climate .

We visited Tut Tomb in Jan 2018 during our trip to Luxor. The tomb requires an extra ticket to be paid - approx. 10€ extra pp and no photos are allowed inside.Tut Mummy is still there. there is only 1 main room with 2 main paintings. In my personal opinion, compared to the other tombs of the valley this is the less impressive.. given you will probably visit Cairo museum it could make sense to complete your visit also with the addition of this tomb but don't expect more that what you find surfing on website.no photos are allowed inside. the guardian will be there with you looking at every movement of your hands..instead go an visit Seti I... which is stunning. an extra ticket is required (50€ pp) but it's the most beautiful tomb you can see in the KV.

We went by boat there I think that was the best thing ,the people there are so kind they welcomed us .it s a different place from busy cities if you want to relax  for 2or 3hours are enough .the Nile was amazing .we saw a big crocodile at a house and took pictures .you can ride a camel and buy some things popular for them .i liked it .
It was ok. Pretty when in action, and I felt that more appropriate music would improve the experience. Classical went well
If you already visited the mosque in Istanbul, you'd be disappointed here. The city skyline from the mosque isn't too attractive either.
If you come after October 2020 his body will be at the new museum. Small tomb area. Most of his stuff is in the museum in Cairo.

When I was offered a tour to the Nubian village by my local tour guide, I was a bit apprehensive as I read mixed reviews about this place. We paid USD45/person for this tour, covering boat ride, visit to a family home and learn how the Nubians live. I found it similar to this kind of tours in different countries, They are paid by the tour guide to serve us local drinks and show their homes, so they don't hassle you with selling stuff. I felt safe with my tour guide around. However, when she offered us to walk around the village ourselves, I did ask her to come with us. For me it is expensive, but safety and comfort come with a price.As to what we see and learn, it is just okay.. each culture has their own unique habits and customs. I like their colourful houses, open areas covered by sand, learn how they adjust their lives in the harsh desert environment. Yes, they have crocodiles as pets and I respect their believe that crocs protects them (I didn't even go near them😉). The best part is the boat ride. As they are a bit away from Aswan, the boat ride was around 30 mins one ways. You will pass different islands, see some ruins, pass the Old Cataract Hotel, a local beach on the Nile river and camels! So travellers, if you are worried about safety and comfort, it's not as bad as you think as long as you have a local tour guide with you.

I been to British museum, the louvre, the hermitage and many more great museums.  I found this museum was very hard to navigate and hard to discover by yourself.  You can get an audio guide, but it’s outside the security, you can not go back out through security and have to leave through a exit in a different corner of the building and I was unsure if I could re-enter.   The king tut exhibit is worth the price of admission. Hopefully the new museum will be better laid out and easier to navigate.  Certainly worth doing but I think if you can get a good English guide it would be worth it.

Abu Simbel is a really cool temple, but if you have to board transport at 3am don’t do it and if you do make sure you take a coach bus. My daughter (20) and I were up at 230am to get on the tour, which was late btw. Also we had to stop on the roadside because we weren’t allowed to cross the dam until 4 - so not sure why we had to leave when we did. While we waited (about 30 min) the driver shut off the car (a/c) and opened the door so the entire time we were feasted in by mosquitoes. Our ride was a poorly air conditioned bumpy minivan so that sleep we’d hoped to get on the way eluded us.We enjoyed the temples, but we were done in an hour. Then we had to wait another hour at a “coffee shop” outside to board the bus back home. It’s 3.5 hour each way.Again, it’s a cool temple, but I’m sure we could have done many other things and had a much more enjoyable day. If you are going to go, make sure you’re going on one of the many more comfortable ac coach buses which we saw waiting in the parking lot.Also yes they do charge 300LE (about $15) to take photos and they enforce it pretty strictly.

If you have time and can stop then worth a look as the Colossi get bigger the further you go.  Shame so much of it was destroyed and the temple is flooded.  Restoration is occurring but this is a big job.  If time poor simply slow down as you go past on the way to/from the Valley of the Kings.  For maximum understanding bring a guide else it won't mean much.

The scenery is spectacular BUT you get a far better (if dustier) experience by taking a PRIVATE quad biking trip in the early evening (5.30pm plus). Try and avoid the group quad experiences as they are slow with multiple stops at overpriced shacks run by locals living in air conditioned villas on the coast. At £3 for a luke warm coke its a rip off. You should only pay £15 each for a private quad session lasting 2 hours - minimum of three people. Fast and furious and you get to see the parts groups don't.

Climb into the pyramid..although it may be a smaller pyramid, I guess my stamina must be really low as by entering it..climbing in and out..just that short distance, I can feel my muscle cramp (lousy....hahaha)...Anyway, it is a beautiful place.

Went to the pyramids yesterday for the second time and to the Citadel, as a much more seasoned inhabitant of Cairo, and feel the need to share a few words of advice about Egypt.First and most obvious, it's a completely difference culture here so do not expect any local to think or reason as you do. They will not every time and that should be expected. So, you must change your behavior and tactics while in their country.Stay close to yourself.  Don't speak your western language loudly, don't answer any of the "Welcome to Egypt", "What's your name?" or "Where are your from?" calls you will receive, don't make lingering eye contact and for Pete's sake keep your wits about you.  Be aware of everyone and every thing.  A stranger approaching you being especially friendly would sound alarms at home...it should especially do so here as well.Egyptians are for some reason unusually fascinated by westerners to the point of being rude and obnoxious.  It can be exhausting.  People will ask to take a picture with you, politely say no unless you are prepared to be firm and set limits.  Don't offer to share any photos.  School children are very aggressive and will overwhelm an unsuspecting soul in a second.  For women it's like Italy but worse, much worse.  Hair should be pulled back or put up and keep yourselves covered.  It's a Muslim country!  If you dress in an inappropriate manner you will pay the price.  Think about your clothing choices before you go out...seriously...In Egyptian tourism, everything has a price.Take your picture?  He's expecting money.Take a picture of my camel?  He's expecting money.Can't find something?  I'll show you.  He's expecting money.Go out with small bills every day.  ATMS here dispense mostly 200's, and NO business prepares for that.  With few exceptions, NO one has change ever, it's partly a way to get bigger bills off tourists, but it's also just one of those cultural differences.  Taxis are required by law to run their meter for each and every trip.  Make sure they do that before you get in the cab and check that it is running once he starts.  If he is not using the meter, tell him to stop and get out.  Seriously---I've done it quite a few times and so has everyone else who lives here.  Don't let a jerk roll you.A trip to Egypt is best done with a guided group.  Spend most of your time In Alex and Upper Egypt.  Come in the late fall through very early spring otherwise it's just too hot.  You really only need 2 days to see the highlights of Cairo.  See the pyramids (early morning), the Khan, the citadel and the Seven Churches; walk along the Nile at some point, then move on.Viva la Masr!
Just a pit stop for some photos.  Since you are in the area might as well.  It is 2 large statues in a sort of open field.  They are quite large.

We arrived in the Nubian village aboard a small boat. We passed on a small and dusty street full on both sides with shops selling traditional things, they said: all kinds of decorations, spices, shawls, veils, shmehr for men, dried fruits, African masks made of wood, leather goods ... I don't know, many other things. The houses in the village have a typical architecture and are very colorful. We were welcomed into the household of a family from the village. We were served tea and hibiscus juice, a cheese paste mixed with something spicy, there was something else on that tray, but I couldn't identify what. The family had a cage in their yard containing three baby crocodiles. With the youngest we took pictures. Of course, something new and unusual for us as tourists, but too bad for those animals, they should have been free. After all, we tourists encourage their captivity. Carried in so many hands, I don't think it was good for the poor animal. Honestly, I'm so sorry now that I took a picture with poor baby crocodile in my hands.Looking at the photos posted here, I am sure that not the same village is visited by groups of tourists. As such, I can't say for sure which Nubian village I was in.

The fact that this tomb was, incredibly, found intact has elevated its fame beyond those of many of the more deserving tombs in the Valley of the Kings. However, the 1700 items discovered in the tomb are no longer there and there are only 2 small rooms to view. There IS the mummy of the young king and his sarcophagus (minus the mask etc) but there are equivalents in the Cairo museum.If you're there, spend the £10 to see the tomb but don't raise your expectations or you risk being disappointed.

Good:A lot to see, this place was big. We spend more than 3 hours. We were overwhelmed by the number of artifacts. We do not know much about ancient Egypt but we found it very interesting especially after visiting Giza and Saqqara. We could see the different style of scribes and statues between different dynasty.The mummy rooms were very interesting. We saw many mummy kings and queens.Entrance fee was not expensive. Although not as cheap as local price but foreigner price is no where as expensive as any other international museums overseas. No way any where we could see so much with this price. People complain about foreigners pay much more but it is only fair the Egypt government provide their own people some national benefits to learn their own history.Bad:Badly organized, there is no enough signs to tell where is what. There were not always information about the artifacts. Many of them had no information.The whole place was like a warehouse. A bit cheaply put together and bad maintenance. A bit dusty and the glass shields for the artifacts were dirty. Some was just repaired by duct tape.Artifacts on display were crowed and many had no barriers or protections. There were ‘don’t touch’ signs around but no body cares. Everyone put their hands on the statutes to take pictures. Staff did not seem to care either.Mummy rooms had a “keep quiet” sign but the cleaner staff were just chatting away loudly and kids were running around like a playground.There were no many chairs around where we could sit an rest a bit to let everything sink in. The place was hot and stuffy, no air condition for most parts.There were no cafe or small deli where we could get a snack or a drink. There was a man selling water and soft drinks in the bucket.Summary:It is worth a trip. It is the best organized tourist place you can get in Cairo. There were only a few tour guides and vendors hassling you. We were only approached by one guide and a couple of street vendors. Unlike Giza and Saqqara, so in comparison, it was the most pleasant visit for us.

There was was more to see at our local beach - coral beach opposite Tiran Island.We liked the day, the first two stops and the lunch was excellent, but the variety under the water was not as good as we had expected.

This is a nice high spot to view Cairo and the Nile River from up high day or night.  It’s 200 Egyptian Pounds for foreigners to enter the tower. As far as the tower there’s not much there except the tower and a few places to eat.

Abu Simbel is a beautiful work of art and the preservation efforts are incredible. Now if you have to decide to visit it or not here are some points to consider:- You have to stay 2 nights in Aswan to see the site. (it is possible to fly directly to Abu Simbel though).- Wake up a 4 am to get into a convoy (the region is unstable and attacks from Sudan are feared)- 2 to 3 hour race through the desert into either a taxi or a bus at the risk of your life. (150 Km/h at less than 3 feet distance from the vehicle ahead) Even in a desert it is extremely dangerous.- 2 hour visit to see a crowded site.- No nice place to get a well deserved lunch.- Guides are not allowed inside the temple (it is crowded enough). So memorize before getting in or you won't know what you are seeing.

We're quite well travelled but keep an open mind and like to see new things. I'd put this on my list of things to see,  as we'd planned to go to soho square.  The crowd had gathered and it was almost on the hour.The music started and the water began to flow. Well it was a bit disappointing. Ok the fountains spray up and down and there are coloured lights and music but we've been to Las Vegas and seen the Bellagio fountains, which are fanastic.Unfortunately this made these fountains seem poor. But if you've never seen anything like this, you'll enjoy it.  It's all about experiences, so I'm glad we watched them.

The temple is amazing but you have to be more careful about the planning and prices.The temple is situated in an island far away from Aswan. In order to go there, you need to take a taxi to the port and a motorboat .The 25 minute taxi ride took me 200 EGP after some hassling, and there could be little hassling about the motorboats because they were all from the same owner and looked exactly like a mafia group. It costs 150 EGP for every two people (go and back after one hour).The sound and light show starts at night but tickets are sold at 6 pm on the other side of the river. That means if you haven't bought a ticket online, you have to take the motorboat back to the entrance, buy a ticket, and take the motorboat back to the temple. Eventually I gave up the show. The temple itself was very good but hassling and inconvenience ruined the mood.

Beautiful church (actuallly two churches) built right into the face of a cliff by the people who live in the area collecting the garbage from around the city. The churches are beautiful but not historical and are only a required site for those interested in a religious tour of Cairo.

This is probably one of the few mountains in Egypt. It's a 2 hours climb (they advise it a difficult but not for someone coming from a country with lots of mountains). The area is quite crowded and there's beduins with camels offering to ride you to the top so you have 2 choices of getting there !
Not the best month the visit, really hot.But the artifacts have the most amazing stories, my 14 year old son loved it.It will be good to see everything again in the new building.

Alhamdulilah, it was great to finally see the pyramids. It really is mind blowing, to see such amazing architecture, I do wonder how they managed to build them without the tools we have today.I’m really glad I went with my husband whose mother tongue is Arabic, as Egyptians will extort you soo badly once they realise you’re a tourist.It’s £10 for Egyptians & £120 (Egyptian pounds) for tourists!My husband has been many times and has always brought an Egyptian ticket, as he was born there (even though he is Palestinian).Anyway, because I was with him, the security forced us to change both tickets, to tourist tickets 🙄 it’s like I had a sign on my forehead saying (British Tourist, please just suck me for my money 😅)..And they also made us pay £20 for the tripod, which is clearly daylight robbery. I didn’t even use it as much as I thought I would! Don’t even bother bringing one..Camel rides are a bump if you don’t know how to hustle... Don’t take the first offer, walk around for a bit & ask about prices. The guys in the front will charge tourists £300 & Egyptians £100, whereas further in you can get a ride for £20-40.A horse & carriage is better but each to their own

Not particularly interesting, as most of the walls are bare. Better skip this site if you have limited time as there are many more interesting sites to see (Karnak, Luxor Temple, Luxor Museum, Medinet Habu, Ramasseum, Valley of the Kings, Valley of the Queens, Tombs of the Nobles, Artisan village,...).

The pyramids in Egypt are very large and worth seeing. However, there are many merchants who force you to buy things and it is very hot in Egypt. So I recommend seeing the pyramid and going directly to a hotel that has a pool.

Well on my last visit every one said did you see king tut's tomb and I had to say no. Well this time I spent the extra 100 pounds to go in. Yes it is not the best in artwork but you do get to see king tut and see some pristine artwork in the burial chamber. Is it worth 100 pounds No, but I can say yes I've been in tut's tomb and said hello.
After visiting the Memphis museum we headed to saqqara.It was an ok experience because of the lack of interest in this deserted area.No big deal as for me....
We went here around 16:30 and would have probably been nicer in the morning for taking photos and tripods were not allowed. Sad.It was the last stop of our luxor day tour.

If these statues were anywhere but Luxor, you would be well advised to go out of your way to see them.  However, you can see equally impressive statues on a visit to nearby temples.  They are along the side of the road on the way to the popular West Bank sights.  They are worth a fifteen minute stop and make for good photos.
The catacombs themselves are amazing as is the story behind them, but getting to this location is a task and the neighborhood is unsightly.

You have to look for historical interest to take this pathWe took it from Karnak to LuxorThe only downside is this toll in the middle of the journey marking the end of one temple towards the otherYou therefore need 2 tickets to do the full visit

Probably should have done my research first but expected more. It is small and not the most decorated. You can see a mummy in Luxor museum and his sarcophagus nothing amazing. Not worth paying 100LE to see, no wonder it was empty!
Nice to see but it really is only a 10 minute stop to take a photo and get hassled by the shop vendors.

While we were in Egypt we had to head to Cairo to see the Pyramids. It was an experience that we'll never forget, however, the hassle you get walking around is just stupid! Our tour guide stayed in the bus so I'm not sure if that's why they approached us more often. We had people pretending to be security guards, people trying to take our tickets off of us and then just people constantly asking you if you want a horse ride or camel ride.. GO AWAY AND LET US SOAK ALL THIS IN!!

It was great to see this place and I would recommend going there; there's a great view of Cairo from the top there, despite the smog, and the Mosque was very pretty, but again, I hated that you had to pay to have a KKK-esque cloak, even when we were both totally covered up, scarves and all. We were quickly ejected from the outer court-yard of the mosque and so didn't get to see the beauty inside. Was quite annoying. Everyone seemed to want to get money from us the whole time, so it put a total downer on the trip.

Not as enjoyable to visit as the Luxor Temple, but it is so close to where you will likely be staying - particularly if staying in Nefertiti Hotel - why wouldn't you visit this temple too!  Medinat Abu better choice if you have to make such a decision between Karnak and it.
Nice water display to accompany misical accompianment. The display is the same whatever the music. The Russians and Egyptians love it

When we visited at the end of August the Mummy Room was already closed as part of the relocation to the new site at Giza.  Much was already packed away or being packed.  Fortunately the King Tut mask was still there.  Great shame even when largely empty of tourists that you can’t take photos.
Otherwise, it's not a big tomb, not as well painted as some, because, the tomb was not originally for a king but used because he died suddenly at a young age.

This is one of the highlights that you can see quite quickly if you are on your own. The amazing thing about this site is the scale of the project and the panoramic view on the approach to the temple. A lot of the detail of the reliefs and statues are missing. Once you have climbed the two flights of stairs and seen inside the temple structure at the top there isn't a lot else to see. Don't be fooled into buying a train ticket either. This isn't like Valley of the Kings, the walk is not even two minutes and you get some great photos as you walk towards the monument.

Such places I advice to visit with local guide in order to enjoy a bit of the history surrounded with this place.There are two old mosques and a nice panoramic view on Cairo.Mohammad Ali mosque (Salaheddine mosque) is nice place, especially its yard, which contains a clock that never worked since the time French gave it to Egypt.

Nubian village one of the stops included in the tours. You get to see Nubian house construction plus a change to cool off from the sun and have either mint tea or hibiscus tea. Dome construction of home keep them cool and allow the air to circulate plus very colorful theme. Bring tennis shoes because small streets are not paved, it can be dusty. Small shops along the Nile for souvenir purchase.

Cairo is a city with very poor air quality and often is very smoggy.  The day we chose to visit, it was very hazy and difficult to see much beyond the Nile.  It would be better on a clearer day.  But, the surrounding area of Zamalek is a great place to walk around even if the air quality is poor.

NO AIR CON inside this place! how! like this place holds so many tourists how could that have been the state of the museum. if this was in America it would be so valued and looked after. Get a guide to explain everything to you otherwise you will walk past the most imporatnt things and miss it. We took about 5 hrs here and we were hot and tired. Hardly anywhere to sit and so we just could not enjoy it as much. do not go in the summer!

The museum was a total disappointment. After viewing the Tut Exhibition in Los Angeles in January of 2019 where every artifact was magnificently displayed - the Grand Egyptian Museum is shabby at best. Its old glass and wood cases echo a different bygone era where you would easily find dusty display cases. The splendid and varied artifacts have no presentation pizzaz and the signage is useless. Shame on the Egyptian government for not putting money into refurbishing display cases, updating lights, and improving signage. This may be a Third World Country, but the heritage and cultural achievements represented by all the artifacts should shine.King Tut’s artifacts were in a small separate room and crowded together. The death mask was in a glass cubical. This spectacular item deserves a great presentation.

There were too many sellers around the pyramids, they followed you around quite a bit, however the landmarks were breathtaking and definitely something to see. If you felt a bit homesick there was a also a pizza hut right opposite the pyramids which was so strange. The area was not busy at all near the pyramids which was unexpected.

The place is not maintained at all. Its like a ware house. Things are lying around and you will be lucky if you find a find a sign board next to it to see what the thing actually is. It looks like authorities got the stuff and they dumped it and didnt know how and where. Watching mummies was great. Like a dream come true i always thought if i will be able. Stuff is good but authorities can do much better in managing and cleaning things. Please keep change with you as the cleaner in toilet will not let you do your stuff untill you pay him tip.

It's a nice cultural and religious place to visit, architecturally it has a great value and historically as well. If you are into churches and religions it definitively a go to, but you should schedule a visit while touring the old Citadel and then you can catch these 2 and also the Moses synagogue.

If you visit the west bank of Luxor you will probably drive past the colossi of memnon. These are two statues in the wilderness of the Pharaoh Amenhoteph III. This is all that remains of a temple that is beleived to have been built by this great king of egypt. These statues pale in comparison with the other attractions in luxor and rest of egypt mostly due to a lack of context. Nevertheless, the do provide an opportunity for a quick photo.
The place is too dirty and too occupied to be holding such important ruins. Do not get guides, they are mostly importers

Went to this site by boat.the site is on an island and the size of the big temple on a small island was peculiar.our guide mohamed badawy was excellent in ecplaining to us the rich history of the temple.

Starting my visit with the magnificent view of the building, as you get closer you start seeing how much maintenance work it actually needs and this fact continues inside as you walk through the various exhibit halls.Entering the building to start your visit you become amazed by all the ancient statues and artifacts that are on display,the total experience was very enjoyable and informative thanks to the well educated guide that was accompanying me on my visit to Cairo, yet I could see that many lone visitors did not get the full benefit of their visit due to them not having a guide to fully explain the "story" behind what they were looking at. Maybe an audio guide would have been helpful (I don't think this was available)All in all, the museum remains a must see attractions when in Cairo

The collections are extensive and this museum is definitely worth visiting, if only to see the more famous things the museum is know for. However, artifacts are littered haphazardly throughout the museum and many items are left unlabeled.

The museum is really big, with lots of artefacts to see. The pieces are marvellous. I went without a guide, which made it a bit difficult to navigate the area, but I prefer to wander so I didn’t mind much. If you like order, I’d suggest you join a tour group. The group leaders know so much about the history and pieces and will guide you through the museum (often chronologically). Alone, the signs aren’t enough to understand what’s going on. Only some pieces will have English translations. I was disappointed with the organization. However, the pieces were still marvellous. I would’ve preferred more of a narrative to go along with each piece (which the guide will no doubt provide). Note if you bring a student card (ISIC card is the best one, the one recognized most widely) you get 50% off. It is 50LE to take photos inside. This isn’t regulated but i didn’t mind paying it since I like to support local tourism etc. DO visit the mummy exhibit. BEST part of the visit. It’s really magnificent and they have several royal mummies on display. You can’t take photos in there, so don’t try. This is enforced. By myself, the museum took me about 2.5 hours. It’s really hot inside so you don’t really want to stay too long (although to really see everything properly it would take you days..)
Marvels place unbelievable beach , fantastic for divers also for snorkelers , nice place to spend a day
Forget any boat trips to Ras Mohamed, get to El finar beach and you can swim across to where the boats drop you off if you were on a boat trip anyway

Great view, but expensive and for a very short time. Max 15 min and you are done. No discounts for students. Restaurant up expensive and not that wow for the value of the money u pay. Better to visit Chillis next to by the Nile. But in a nice sunny day chance to get nice pictures from above.

Not much to see, not accessible by stroller lots of steps and huge hole.Small electric golfette that brings us as close as possible to the site. Small corner point with cold drink and ice cream.Be careful the drivers of the small carts seeks to get tips despite that we pay the trip (very insistent).

The fort is actually empty on the inside and there's nothing interesting to see inside.. but it's a nice place to take lots of photos, especially on the roof! The view is wonderful on the roof and you really get a good view of Alexandria from up there. I also went on a boat ride for 15 minutes or less for 80 EGP
Must visit during your visit to Cairo. An unique collection in the world it is a pity that the building is so old.
It is another face of Cairo. a place you will feel the specialty about it when you are there. be sure you visit the synagogue and Amr Ibn Elaas Mosque there too

This  is a very popular attraction and draws a fairly large crowd even during a period of low tourism.  But just because it's the most popular doesn't mean it's the best.  There are so many temples to be seen on this side of the Nile that I would put others ahead of this one.  While this is reconstructed it seems too freshly built to be authentic.Other temples also have great stories to tell and plenty to be learned.  My favorite by far was Medinat Habu.  If you only have a limited amount of time.  Skip this one.
Tow big status stand alone in the field nothing much about but good to take pictures with you can make your own trip to see
once you get over the disappointment of the location and you see the pyramids from a certain angle avoiding the centre of Cairo you can marvel at this ancient site for hours.

Sight to behold. Every things puts it off.The hawkers or guides spoil the show. So do the taxi drivers or tour agents. All through my trip of 9 days, I found only 1 person who didnt cheat us. Anybody interested in taking private tour around Cairo or Alexandria can contact Hosam E, reach out to him on whatsapp +201116815353.He took us to Alexandria for a day trip and charged less than $70 for three of us. Other agencies were charging $70 per person.There is no MRP printed on items. Shop keepers see foreigners, they inflate the price. You go to a restaurant, they dont share the menu. Google translator helped. However, they too try to cheat.Booked a cab from cairo airport to take us around in cairo, they charged $100. If I had booked via Careem, it would have been lesser than $40.

This museum needs to get to it's new home as soon as possible.  The museum is not air conditioned and many of the items in the museum are getting packed to be moved so they are hard to see.  Very interesting  items to see but they need to be displayed in a new way and labeled so you know what you are looking at.  Obviously you need to go here if you are in Cairo.  They are years behind building the new place to house all the wonderful antiquities.  Wish I could have seen them in their new building.

A complete history of Egypt in one single building.Artifacts in display were amazing!!! I have been to many museums (Metropolitan in NY, Lourve in Paris, National Palace Museum in Taipei Palace Museum in Beijing, etc), this is definitely one of the tops!!There are many drawbacks though:- almost no air conditioning???- construction going around near artifacts, without much protection- loose management… Tutankhamun exhibit area was not supposed to take any photos. I happened to see many Chinese tourists breaking the rules, yet no on stop them- overall cleanliness under par

the pyramids themselves are nice and a very important piece of the world's history but the people there are very annoying, They constantly harass you to buy their services . I had a man harass me to take a photo of me with my camera and when I finally gave in he asked for a tip.

King Tut's tomb is a must see because of the history around it, but inside is only a mummy. There are also hieroglyphics on the walls around the mummy. A must see because of the historical value, but I was disappointed with what I saw.

Before you enter, there are two queues. One for arabs and one for foreigners. Foreigners pay 120EGP, foreign students with valid student card pay 60egp and arabs pay only 5egp. After that, you get a ticket and before you enter, the guard checks your ticket and asks to see your passport which is so unecessary.When you finally make it inside, PLEASE CARRY A HUGE WATER BOTTLE WITH YOU. There are no shops inside. The museum has thousands and thousands of fascinating pieces and you need to dedicate a whole day for this as there is so, so, so much to see. Definitely see the mummies room which is an extra 100EGP for foreigners and of course, significantly less if you're from other arab nations.If you can, try and hire a tour guide.

Here we got a taxi for 250 LE for hole day. Entrance was 80 LE.This place is in the outside of town when you go there you will find people working on the pyramid doing some repairs, you walk through what look like the court yard and one big hole had no idea what was it for  and some ruble which are small used to be pyramids  there is one open pyramid right side on the T juction that you can go down and have a feeling of the inside, writing on the walls as usual but you might need someone to explain for me it was just an experience of being there nothing much so I had no tour guide. Here I was hustled even by the police trying to be my tour guide, I let him be for some time as he was saving me from other hustlers.For me this place is for only historians and hard core pyramid loversFrom a distance I observed a bent and red pyramid.I was tend off by this pyramid I did not carry on to the bent and red pyramid.

Guide is very good idea to have with You if You are a tourist. We had our own Finnish speaking Guide and egyptologist ( White, female person) and local egyptian man started to shout to her with rage and anger outburst when she translated what the local egyptian Guide was saying. It was terrible when in the exit the museum staff smoked inside and inside at the museum shop next to the textils they were selling!  If the museum wants more clients allow different language speaking guides to do their Jobs, if that is too much to ask then, please educate egyptian ladies to be guides in different European languages to be guides

Obviously king tut is the most famous king, as it was the most recently discovered (1922) ontop of an early death. He died around 19 years old and was king from about 10 years old. His body has damage to his chest and legs which indicates either a hunting accident or wounded gravely at war. Nobody really knows. The tomb is small because it had to be built quickly, and the body and sarcophogas are in the tomb. Everything else is in the museum in CairoHonestly you have to see it, but compared to say Ramesses IV's tomb, it's underwhelming.And it's ridiculous how it costs more to access (200egypt pounds/$18AUD/$11USD), plus you can't take photos, when these photos are literally published in books you can purchase at the gift store on the way in, and are all over the internet. (We paid 80 egyptian pounds). In addition to this you pay extra at the main entrance to use your camera but you aren't allowed to use it here. Pretty silly.We were in there with only 2 other people, and the guardian motioned "shh" placing his finger over his lips and said "photo 1 minute" as if we had to wait for the other people to leave to take a photo. Anyway by the time that happened more people came in so we just left without taking them. The book we bought came in handy as it was basically our tour guide and described the walls.If you meet an egyptian around any tourist site, you just cannot be sure of their motives. It's highly likely he would try extort us afterwards anyway.
An exceedingly rich period of human history that ironically is exhibited in a dismal way. The museum authorities need to get professional help in exhibiting such historic pieces.
These statues are in quite a bad state of repair but it is incredible to see the size of them. They are right next to the road and quite nice for a quick photo stop.

They are in the process of moving many items to the new Egyptian Museum so there are a lot of crates in the Museum.  Still has the Tut collection, which makes it worth the visit.  Other exhibits are interesting, but often not labeled or described in any language other than Arabic.
I think it’s almost ready to open for tourists. The photo shows one of the sphinxes on the side. I shoot this photo when I pass by with the horse ride.

The museum is quite large and houses a large number of artifacts and antiquities. Unfortunately, since a new museum is being built in Giza, most of the artifacts are unlabeled and stored in a haphazard manner. While there were some which could be viewed and one could get a feeling of wonder and awe, most left me feeling dissatisfied. The area which houses mummies were separated by an entrance which costs an additional $16 and photography cost is approx.. $15 and that was on top of the museum entrance fee of $6, so be aware that it can cost about $30 or so. All in all, I was sad that things of such value was not given their due importance and I left feeling I visited a large warehouse of priceless artifacts than a musuem.

This would be a very unusual sight almost anywhere but here, at the gateway to the Valley of the Kings. Impressive and an indication of the splendid building that once stood here and which is still being excavated, these figures are badly damaged but contain enough detail to be interesting.
the fountain is very small compared to the 'norm' of dancing fountains, water moves were not rhyming with the music...however Soho square is a very nice place...lovely light decorations.
The Cairo tower is the tallest structure in Cairo. If you have been to or are from any large city you have surely seen/been to much nicer and much larger buildings.
Many objects have already been moved to the yet unopened Grand Egyptian Museum in Giza.  Go to the brand new Museum of Egyptian Civilization instead and see the mummies.

Visited it twice - by yacht and by bus. There has been not much to see. A salty lake, a bit of mangrove bushes (I have seen rich mangrove forests in Cuba, so in Ras Mohamed I was disappointed) and the sea with fish. The next day I saw much more fish in the sea by my hotel. So nothing extraordinary. But the yacht trip itself was good.

It was interesting to see the Egyptian history but the facility of the museum is not modern standers at all. Not many bench or places to sit down, no cafes or restaurants, the museum shops had almost nothing.It is not the must place to go in Cairo unless you have a plenty of spare time.

We booked with an agent at the Old Market (Peace Tours) who promised us a trip there with a big bus, free drinks, movies and an on-board toilet but was picked up by a mini-bus with none of the above.  Once you are on the trip all public toilets cost 2 Egyptian Pounds and were not that clean so would have appreciated the original offer as we went without a snacks or drinks thinking it would be supplied.The hike is a bit tough, the view alright, the tour of St Catherine's crowded but was glad I went.  Would not go a second time though.
dont bother, go to Hurghada instead and go diving. Also way more hassle getting on a boat in Sharm,
Soho district is worth to be seen. Especially in the evening. It's full of lights , cafes , shops, etc. But the fountains do not work in February.

I visited Sakkara 16 years ago and it was much more vibrant than now.  Sakkara is almost completely covered in scaffolding, and virtually abandoned.  Current restoration efforts have been halted due to an issue with the company doing the work.  The Sarapeum located nearby was far more interesting
the king tut too dark, too many people could not see the exibits except for king tuts mask, the exibitsfrom the elements except glass so are deterioating

This museum needs to be cleaned and needs to have better light... it hosts amazing masterpieces and it is a pity to be in this condition. Also it needs better order so that the guest will be able to follow

Beautiful place, but unfortunately not what you would expect.If you are an Egyptian you will know how to handle it, but make sure not to look too much like a tourist (like I did) and carry only what you need.The balloon and sweets sellers will give your kids stuff to put you on the spot with them, but if you this in advance you know your kids and can easily pass this.Don't expect a tour or any explanations on site, but if you want to see a beautiful  historic place and let your kids know more about it, pay it a visit for sure.From the sellers on the corniche, we bought some nice large seashells and bottles of colored sand with the kids name written in them, and the kids loved it.Don't forget to get ice cream from Azza Ice-cream on ur way out. Just a few flavors, but amazing taste and good price.

Two prices £25E for Egyptian visitors and £75E for foreigners. At entrance.Then join the lift queue - we went around 2pm but queues were long. When we got to the front the reason why was the single lift. It's old and slow and only carries around 8 people at any one time. A man operates it from within. Recommend to go early in morning or around sunset but expect longer queues.Views pretty good, but very smoggy air so couldn't see much into the distance. This was disappointing as we heard on a clear day it could be possible to see the pyramids.The inside of the tower could do with modernising.Overall a decent experience.

I visited the museum before 2011 so my review does not account for what happened since the 2011 revolution.  The amount of antiquities is amazing.  The layout can quality of display needs a lot of improvements.  Also you were forced to pay an extra fee for photography and videotaping.

We visited this fountain again. It was not working properly. The songs were less amusing and less enchanting. Nevertheless we had Movenpick ice cream, close by the fountain. Shops around are most elegant. The Christmas light decorations are fabulousand most spectacular. Taking pictures is a must.

We had a trip to the museum planned as part of our tour of Cairo. Our tour guide took us round to some of the most fascinating aspects of the museum and then gave us some time to look around.The museum was hot, noisy and pretty busy and we didn't get as much time as we wanted but overall it was enjoyable.No photos were allowed and they took your camera off you when you walked in but they didn't take any mobile phones which was strange as they are all used as cameras these days. So we saw countless number of people taking photos on their phones - all the signs didn't mean a thing.

Came here via boat and camel ride.I am not sure if there is multiple camel companies but the one we took was horrible.The camels were not treated well.The guy handling mine hit it way to much.The Nubian school we went to was nice! They even did a small class for us, which was interesting.The people selling stuff, constantly were yelling at us. Trying to get our attention. It became very annoying.We ate at one of the Nubian homes. Food was good. Their was a woman doing Henna tattoos. Claimed they were authentic. It was black, which to my knowledge is chemicals. A few girls had reactions and were trying everything to get the henna off.I personally wouldn't go out of my way to come here.

The Pyramids of the Giza plateau are a must-see if you are visiting Egypt. These millennia old burial sites are a miracle of ancient engineering and construction. It is also a miracle if you get away with the shirt still on your back. Unless you go with an organized tour, you will be shaken down, hassled and generally bushwhacked by every person you see on the site.Tourists are few and far between in Cairo at the moment. But rather than less pushy and fleecing to attract more tourists, the guides, camel and cart drivers, and sellers of paraphernalia seem to think they should now extract their previous income levels from the few tourists that are venturing into the site.Don't get me wrong: GO; for all the reasons listed in other reviews.  But take one of the organized/ packaged tours from Carlson or a legit tour operator.  Then you can actually enjoy the site, ride on a camel, see the panoramas; do what ever you really want to do, and not what you do not want to do, relatively free of harassment.

We only had about half an hour here. But still worth a look. Market close by nice enough to walk through but nothing we wanted to buy. Has historical interest but not really a peak moment of the days activities.
new  ( 10 years or so) and beautiful mosqueNot to be compared with the old mosques like in Instanbul but nevertheless wortwhile to visit if you are in Hurgada

We visited the temple as part of an organised tour to Luxor. I have to admit that I had never heard of the temple before and on reflection this was the only part of our itinerary that I gladly would have sacrificed to shorten the day a little.The entrance fee is EGP 50 and again you have the option to buy tourist tat at the stores near the car park.Parts of the ruins have been rebuilt, for which we apparently have the Poles to thank.The ruins include a few interesting statues and a quantity of hieroglyphics, however nothing that eclipses what you can see at Karnak by the bucket load.The site does have a welcome open air cafe offering shade from the sun.
You can't enjoy it in peace, people won't leave you alone. Spoilt my experience totally! Great pyramids, they are. But what an absolute dive, I wouldn't go there 100%.

Very nice tower, I enjoyed a lot with restaurant and the view from top.The people there are very kind .The bad thing that I face in this visit , when I want to exit some people that I thought working in the tower , but I think working in tourism company . They were very annoying. Otherwise everything was good.
We had nice time and took pictures a historical castle by the sea with wells and forts of soldiers used long ago during war

I recently visited and was disappointed overall as a number of artifacts are packed up ready for shipping and displays look tired overall with minimal information available. The main attraction being Tutankhamen treasures which I have viewed before was the biggest disappointment with very limited items on display. I hope the displays will return to their former glory when moved to the new museum. If you are in Cairo it’s still worth a visit but not a must visit anymore

I find the temple similar to all the other temples my friends and I went to.We went here by private car, and we didn’t realize how challenging it was to get to the temple because of the motorboat ride. The ride was okay, it’s negotiating with the men on the boats that make it wayyy challenging.We were lucky to share the boat ride with some other tourists who went by car, and it wasn’t peak season yet.
It was very busy, but worth the visit! Best done with a guide to get the best out of this superb artefact.

You will probably drive past coming back from one of the other highlights (Valley of Kings, Deir el Bahari or Medinat Habu), so may as well stop for a quick photo. These giant depictions of Pharaoh Amenhotep III have been standing guard here for nearly 3,500 years. Now they sit in the middle of nowhere, badly broken and damaged, and the parking lot is a bit of a tourist circus, with hawkers and touters flocking around the busloads.So stop, get out, take your pictures and get going.
This was our first stop on our  tour and it was nice to learn about the first step pyramid in Egypt. Going inside the tombs were also incredible.

The Avenue of Sphinxes is under restoration at the moment since they have done the last part of excavation recently, They already found it all but still restoring them.After they make full restoration of the 1600 sphinxes they will open it for tourist to walk through and wander.It links Karnak with Luxor Temple for the distance of 3 km, it was built during the reign of Amenhotep III (1400 b.c.e) to celebrate the annual festival of Opet (you can see the details of this festival on the wall of Tut Ankh Amoun in Luxor temple)Enjoy your holiday in egypt
Not a single temple here A huge site containing much of Egypt's ancient history from the past There is a very interesting atmosphere. The weather is so hot, it's a bit depressing.

Went to the Giza pyramids with a driver from the company i was working for.Paid 80 Egyptian pounds for entry, then was pestered for money by so called guilds.Was led to a camel told it was free, it wasent spoilt the trip.I would recommend yo dont go,as couples as women are pestered for cash.
camel ride was a bit short. Food was taditional Egyptian so we didnt eat much.Worse thing about the trip was the children were begging when we got off the camel & trying to sell us beads
I can understand why they are doing this and it'll probably be spectacular when finished. At the moment, it still looks like a building site.

The temple itself is fairly impressive, but what really makes the show is the setting.  The temple is built into, and backs onto, a huge rock outcrop.  Both building and rocks catch the sun.  It must have been a fantastic place in ancient times, and it's still impressive today.  There are carvings, columns and statues, and there is an avenue of sphinxes, but there is not a huge amount to see.  Approach is via a rather silly little land train, although I suppose useful for those who can't walk very far or are suffering from the heat.  Usual avenue of tat-hawkers when you come out.

This is not the biggest or the best preserved ruined temple you'll see in Egypt, but it's still a very rewarding place to visit. It's Ptolemaic in era, but unusual in that it is dedicated to two gods rather than one.  The whole place is totally symmetrical.  It also has a "secret" corridor where the priests could both hear what the penitents were asking of the gods, as well as (possibly) supplying the voice of the god's "answer".

Pyramids of Giza is magnificent. Taking a horse ride around the pyramids in Giza is one in a lifetime experience and this activity is a must to truly experience Giza! It costs $30 to go inside the Pyramid of Giza, but not mush is there when you go inside. If you visit Dahshur, Red Pyramid there is free to enter.Be ware of local touts there (and in Egypt in general). Many people can just smile while you take picture or give you something. If you smile back or take it, they will demand for tips. I know how ridiculous it sounds, but that is the truth and Egyptian authority has done nothing about it!!! If you hire a tour guide, you will less likely to get such "harassment". So, get a tour guide the moment you reach the pyramid site, if you intends to get one.You can combine Giza with Sakkara and Memphis in a day trip. If you book a tour with hotel, remember to do some research on price and haggle the price. Due to limited time in Cairo, I did a day trip to Giza, Sakkara, Memphis and Dashur in one day. It is definitely doable, but I wouldn't recommend that.

... but has to be seen. It is the smallest and least decorative of the tombs - he was only 19 when he died. My visit was spoiled a little by the three archeologists working around the sarcophagus at the time - it is tiny in there!

During my visit, most of the area were assess denied like the police museum, part of the citadel.
