And the only building which you allowed to go inside is the mosque.So basically nothing much to see in citadel that time except for the view of Cairo which you can't appreciate pyramids from here.It's quite far from town.Entrance ticket is 60 le for foreigners and half price for students
the entrance is very expensive and in the tomb there is not much to see.You better go visit other more beautiful tombs in the valley.

The good news is on almost every tour to the valley of the kings you will make s hort picture stop here, the bad news, it is not worth anything more. The statues are not that intact and even though the last decades the area has overgone some good archeological work there is nothing much to see.The good news is, only a few slaespeople here so you can still enjoy the short moment of antiguity with those two statues.

We made a quick stop here as part of tour.  Very little here is original but still interesting as the story of queen who demanded she be worshipped as a king.  Good on her!  Our guide explained the history and again how the dynasty and family followed.Once again the mandatory market people in our face.
The citadel lays at the top of the mount, where you can enjoy the view of Amman lights all around. I attended Yanni concert there,it was well organized and very joyful.
A bit expensive for a small tomb. For the price we thought we were going bigger. Ok there is the mummy but the other tombs are much more beautiful and larger.Disappointed for the price

This is not a historic must go place. When you're walking close to the pyramids, it's impressive for sure but the locals try to grab you to get some money by offering a camel walk or even a picture. This is disgusting. They're poor and want to earn from tourists but they should offer better service, they should keep their animals healthy.
Unique collection. However although the building is very nice, built 1901, it is dirty and in bad shape.

The Pyramids was on my "Bucket List" of must do things, The experience was ruined when our driver took us to his "friend" that owned a stables to hire a carrage and tried to extort a fee of 55 UK pounds (550 Egyptian) EACH for a 1 hour trip arround the site, we walked down to the ticket office and after buying out UKP6 entery ticket found a driver waiting who was charging just UKP8 for the both of us for the 1 hour trip, mind you after we finished he tried to extort 150 Egyptian saying the 80 did not cover the horses food plus he has 6 kids at home! We gave him 100 and jumped off and rant to the exit with him hot on our heels! There is no such thing as an agreed deal so beware!
Great to see this historical place, but not the ost exciting place for a visit. You see it and then, get back in your car. Maybe better to see it on Nat Geo. :)

The Egyptian Gov't is in the process of relocating all structures from this right now. The Karnak side shows a little of what it will look like in a few years. The Luxor side is to be desired. I would love to see this in it's glory in a few years when everything is done!

There is nothing special about this place, its a new building in the traditional style.  Unfortunately we were not allowed in, though our guide did enter but refused to take us in we did see other tour groups enter.  Our Guide did though enter into a rather long and I think miss guided diatribe about Islam which became very political. Not exactly what I wanted to hear on a holiday.

A good place to spend some time, else nothing much and interesting to see, just some underground catacomb. The catacombs were supposedly built by the Greek to bury or rather place the bodies in the burial chambers of people after their death

In the day, these sphinxes were lined up for miles along the road to link Luxor temple to Karnak.  Today they are only a few hundred defaced ones left.  It only takes you a minute to take a look when you visit Luxor Temple.
Along the road while driving to the Valley of the Kings, there is almost none of the temple remaining other than these two old statues populated by pigeons. Time and earthquakes have aged them.

My wife loved it but I was disappointed after doing the 17 day grand tour of Egyptand all that I had seen and witnessed, for the money to fly there I was moreimpressed with all the other temples that we saw along the Nile.

We visited the Nubian villages on Elephantine island.  You can take a small motorboat or felluca for a few dollars to any island.  Boats are plentiful and they will give you their number to call, or arrange a pickup time, or there will be boats waiting.  Bargain with one boat owner, we spent about ten dollars for two of us to go to several different islands for the day.At first we couldn't work out whether we were in the villages, there were small non descript mud brick houses down narrow dusty lanes with plots of green vegetables or walled back yards filled with trash.  There's no trash pick up here so they burn the stuff or toss it over a wall- which does have the advantage of not seeing the trash unless you peek over the walls.We found Animalia by accident and really enjoyed the talk by the owner's daughter on Nubian culture.Like all of Egypt, say hello to anyone and they will smile and welcome you, and then ask if you want to give them a dollar.  If you laugh and say, No, why?  they will laugh back and happily practice their English with you.We did not find many of the picturesque painted houses you see in photos- perhaps we didn't look hard enough?  This is a poor, lived in village.  We did visit the drinking water treatment plant, again by accident, it was a somewhat different level of operation from US or UK treatment plants  We opted out of drinking the lime green water, or even having the tea, but enjoyed the view from the top of the tower.
Because of all the hype, this is rather underwhelming and doesn’t hold a candle to some of the other tombs. The mummy is here, though, so it kind of finishes off what you see in the Cairo museum.

Great place to visit. Make sure to get a guide if possible as there is so much to see and not the most info. Having a guide will help you connect things with your trips to the pyramids and other locations in Egypt.
Hatshepsut's Temple is only five percent original since it has been reconstructed by Polish experts. That way you get a good impression of the impressive building.

The tomb is small and pretty much empty, King Tut lays in state in a glass box, not looking as handsome as his golden masks suggests, but then again he is a couple of thousand years old. All the treasure is in the Cairo Museum, so don't go expecting to see any of that. Not sure its worth the extra 10 english pounds Ram 6th is much more impressive and worth paying the extra for.

This was part of the Muhamed ali mosque area that we saw and contained a military museum.This is a non pyramids type tour we took.It was very popular among locals it seemed.This is not our thing so to us it was a cool old building with a very nice mosque.
A shame it was half empty because of the upcoming move to the new Grand Egyptian MuseumKing Tut area was still quite complete. I will have to return to Cairo once the new Museum is open.

We visited SoHo quite a few times during our recent stay in Sharm.  Our Hotel had a water fountain,  and we thought the musical finain was on par with that.  It's a nice experience and if yi have the chance to visit,  am sure you'll like it too!

The guide and several other locals told us that a visit to this tomb is avoidable. However, I had been reading since childhood about Howard carter's discovery & persistence of a decade and also tales of the curse and had pretty much made up my mind that I would visit this tomb even before we arrived in Egypt.Most people visit this tomb because its the most popular in the valley and got the most media coverage. However, after visiting the other open tombs in the Valley of the Kings (especially Ramses III which was spectacular), I couldn't help feel disappointed while visit Tutankhamen's.The Tutankhamen tomb entrance is an additional cost of 100 LE and was also the smallest of the tombs that we visited in the valley and with the least to see. Some of the other open tombs with no additional entrance cost were far larger and with more intricate sculptures & drawings.If you've visited the mummy's room in the Egyptian museum in Cairo then the mummy displayed inside the tomb is no different.The tomb treasure is anyway stowed there at Cairo museum.If you're on a tight budget or schedule, then I would recommend pocketing the 100 LE and spending more time in some of the other tombs which I found to be more worthy visiting.
The fountains are ok, but, after a few minutes the show does get a tad repetitive. Once you've seen them once, you've seen them. I guess they are a nice touch, but I would rate them as an attraction.
This is another stop in the full day Tour of west bank. Its only a couple of big rock statues. Nothing special. Worth about 10 minutes.

I didn't go to the bent pyramid but saw it from far away. The story about how it was built was very interesting. Basically, this was a product of miscalculation. According to the story though, there was never a Pharoah buried inside (the one who ordered its construction didn't appreciate the bent shape). So I would recommend: hear the story, have a look at the bent shape, but no need to go there unless you are particularly interested.
Visit this museum to find the treasures of Egypt. Truly fascinating and a privilege to be within such close proximity to the rich hosting of Egypt.

Our visit at the pyramids had been a dream coming true. The monument is heavily visited so please keep an eye on your belongings. Our carriage driver forced us into giving him a tip so make sure that you make some sort of prepaid tour arrangement beforehand.
A temple on an island in the middle of the Nile. Its view was outstanding and the wall paintings were greatly detailed.

It's a good temple to see the history of the only queen in Egyptian history. The story if hatshepshut is amazing, you should know if you go. Take water and food and cap and cover you body. It would be preferred to visit in winter.

The pyramids themselves are fantastic. Absolutely worth to see, but the whole experience is ruined by all the people trying to scam you for money.I had a driver from my hotel that was very helpful in explaining what to be aware off.Even so the official "tour guide" at the Pyramids was trying to make me pay 90$ for a little round trip in a horse carriage I refused and suddenly the price was 40$ ( still way overpriced ) on top of that when we were done he tried to explain how much people normally tip ( 40-50$ ) needless to say I didn't give that, but they are good at putting pressure on you for money. So make up your mind beforehand how much you wanna pay.In fairness the guide did take some nice pics of me with the pyramids in the background, and was as such nice enough, but I just don't like the feeling of being pressured to pay more than I should.After that 45 mins tour my driver took me down to the Sphinx, also there you have to be aware of scammers. The entrance ticket gives you access to the area, to go inside a small pyramid next to the two big ones and also the Sphinx. Outside the Sphinx some people will approach you trying to get you to buy an extra ticket, ignore them unless they have some kind of id-card hanging around their neck. Same goes for people offering to take great pics of you - don't let them it's gonna cost.I didn't pay the extra to go inside the big pyramid, was told it was not worth is. Guess it depends on how much time you have, and how interested you are in the history.It's also possible to walk around the whole area, just takes some more time and you'll have to deal with all the "nice" people trying to sell you stuff.It's a disgrace that the authorities allow this to happen at one of the worlds seven wonders. I have been lucky enough to see quite a few things around the world, and this is by far the baddest experience I have had in terms of scammers ruin a beautiful place.

I really liked this tomb, and the Mummy of Tut is incredible, but it is 100 pounds extra to see and is very very small compared to others.  Most people pay the money just to say they saw it.  I would still go, but don't be too excited

It's nice to visit the citadel or Al-Kalaa, its name in Arabic. The historic feeling is still alive when looking from above and see streets and alleys of crowded Cairo. The great dusty walls are still visible and make one travel in time to where Saladin ruled the Middle-East from its tower. When we were there, only few Egyptians and individual Westerners roamed around. Many closed rooms and few were turned into mosques. One was full of worshipers, and the screaming Sheik was heard lecturing verses and Hadiths.. Outside we had to walk about half mile to get to the castle, and our cab had to wait inside the car because traffic officers won't let anyone park near the entrance.Flowers andtrees at the entrance gives a sense of relief and security. Paved roads are clean, and employees were very nice when asked about directions. A small fare has to be paid for entring tickets.

It is a tourist trap. Not bad but the point of going is to get a small sense of Nubian culture and this is possible.But the agenda is to sell hena tattoos, different sovereigns etc. For me the sail trip to the place was better than the visit

With only the outer sarcophagus still left at the Tomb, there is not much left for the Tourists.The paintings on the walls are also not elaborate as that of Ramses II.A separate entrance fee is also levied for this tomb.If time permits, one can visit the tomb.

I really liked the museum it is really intersting and a must do if you are in Cairo. Unfortunately it is way over packed and you can not really enjoy it. The guides are screaming at each other because they are on the way which was really weird. If you really want to see everything you need lots of time. We were glad we were in but never going there again.
Hopefully the new museum will be amazing. This is not. Some fabulous exhibits surrounded by so many antiquites displayed very poorly.

The status of Ramses II is impressive in terms of size. You could clearly see the waterline with pitting on the status. The "Museum" is built around where the statue was found and there are several other artifacts displayed outside.Please do not expect too much besides that status as seen in the picture. A 30 minute stop if you are doing the full tour but not worht the visit if it is not on the way,
The mosque here was wonderful but dust prevailed on everything and other than the main mosque there was not much to see. Good views of Cairo.

. It is a tower, with no historical meaning (compared to Galata tower/Istanbul or Pisa Tower/Italy, etc...)... The entrance fee: 180 EP is over exaggerated compared to other more interesting sites in Cairo (example: entrance to pyramids area = 120EP) (although it is still a low price, as compared to other countries). We were lucky & there was not too much people there, or we could have waited for hours before reaching the last floor. Since there is only 1 elevator which have space for around 7-8 people... For the view it is nice, & you can see the whole city 360.. But again there are other places which can offer you a similar view (like mokattam mountain & the citadel). The bad thing is that the fence up there is too high & with a very poor design, so all your photos will have a fence in the back ground as high as your height.. For pictures of the city, you can manage to take the photos without showing the fence... There is an interesting revolving restaurant just a floor below, but we didn't try it, so i can't give my opinion about the food & services.. But i saw it, & It has a great view... Overall: if you have enough time to see the more recommended sites, then you can give this place a try, perhaps dining there at the revolving restaurant..

One of our  least favourite archeological visits during our two week visit to Egypt. Road trip out to the site was over rough roads & for a good portion of the way followed a canal choked with with garbage. If tourism is key to the Egyptian economy cleaning up the canal may be a worthwhile project.

The walk up Mount Sinai is the reason people go to St. Catarina. People climb the mountain either at sunset or sunrise (most popular). We went at sunset. You have to take a guide (he was good) although there is absolutely no need for this and you climb the mountain with hundreds of other people and folk offering you a camel ride. At the summit you can see Egypt's highest mountain and I climbed this the following day. This was a great walk up to over 2,600m. I met only three people tending their goats and had a real feeling of achievement when I completed it. I would recommend Mount Catarina over Mount Sinai.
Most of the sphinxes are out of view and we were unable to walk along them. They looked like they were being excavated but cound not see any order to the dig.

You had to pay 75LE for the entrance and 100LE more to see the mummies. The main reason for visiting this museum are the mummies and that doesn't included in the ticket and nobody tell you that from the beginning. No cameras allowed in the museum.

The mosque architecture and details are not superb, but it represents a very important place in making the egyptian and islamic history, so you need some one to guide you through its history. The most fantastic in visiting the mosque, is actually the walk in the area surrounding it, where you will feel the old history  of cairo, during walking in the narrow streets of the area, setting in a historic cafe "El Feshawy cafe", eating in a crowded retaurant serving traditional food "El Ahd El Gedeed", and discovering bazar contents of perfumes, water pipes, hand crafts and other things. You can enjoy the area for half a day at least. You have to do some burgeoning when you buy and take care of pickpockets.

We love museums. Not like, love. This museum is a mess. Very little is labeled, so while the artifacts are incredible, you lose a lot to the lack of ambience. Plus, they have an odd camera policy. Cell phone cameras are allowed in, but other cameras are not, without purchasing a pass - and I'm honestly not sure what you'd take photos of. We have hopes that the new museum being built near the pyramids will be an improvement, because these pieces deserve to be seen.

ExtenuanteExcellent to see God ( maybe you you reach Him, personally)...Obvious it is a religious adventure but be careful if you have advanced age or some fisical problems.Be aware about be  a excruciate trip to the summit . very difficult

It was good to come & learn about it. Only thing that disappoint me much was the cleanliness & arrangement at the entrance. I came on Friday so it was an off day for Egypt. Too crowded & people were sleeping, sitting & running/walking around. It was uncomfortable for first timer like me. The interior was very good it’s just that the authorities need to Improve in many areas ie: cleanliness, entrance, inside the Mosque etc. If nothing couldn’t be taken care of, it wouldn’t attract tourists to visit. It is sad to see Our Mosque is so dirty & no systematic. Can be improved!
If you have time pop in and see this mosque but its not as lovely as some in Abu DharbiIt's got a lovely outlook over the city due to its high position

I was very disappointed to see the state of one most important mosque in the world,dirty inside and especially on the outside mosque, really sad to see that, I hope they will give the true value.beautiful architecture and significant history

Went diving here after dive school recommendation but didnt find anything to get excited about. You can only get there by boat and there are no buildings allowed, so it is unspoilt, but as a dive site I expected more.

The museum itslef is very old and the curation could be a lot better. Possibly worth getting a guide as hardly anything is labelled. Its worth paying extra to see the Mummy exhibition and the museum is worth the visit to see Tutankhamun's mask.
Not much to do here except take a photo and marvel
this cairo tower is the best place that can see cairo in the higher point at the city. however the lift is too small, when arrived to top level, has nothing else apart from to nice view.

I visited this place the same day I visited Pompey's Pillar, An excellent visit.tourist police will search your bag and if you have a camera they will insist you leave it at the ticket office. no photosEntrance Free EGP 60
Not too much to say - supposedly the oldest of the pyramids.  Could not go in it of course.  I wouldn't go out of my way to see it.

Obviously, the King Tut exhibit is really amazing - and you can spend the day just in this section.  The rest of the museum could be fantastic - but the infrastructure is really inadequate.  The lighting is very poor - and at times is so dimly lit that you cannot read the information cards on the exhibits.  No maps given - many items not labeled - sad really.
Just two statues and nothing else. It's free of charge though.  You won't regret skipping this place.

The Alabaster Mosque is exactly as the name depicts. I beautiful structure with an exterior made entirely of Alabaster. You can tell that the structure has not been well taken care of because the once white and shiny exterior is now brownish in appearance. But that doesn't take a way from the history and importance of this mosque. Mohamed Ali is buried in the mosque!! And I think that in itself is amazing. The views of Cairo from the courtyard are very beautiful. On a clear day you can almost see all the way to the pyramids. I would suggest taking a tour with someone who is very knowledgeable and can provide you with the history of Mohamed Ali, the mosque, and the fort that it is located in.

There is not so much to see inside so - in my opinion - there is no need to spend the money for the ticket because seeing this temple from outside is enough. In fact this is the most beautiful and impressive seen from the distance. Good tip is to see this from the top of one of the hills around. As I am Polish the nice feeling was to see the information inside that this temple was discovered and conserved by the Polish - Egyptian Archeological Mission :-)

The pyramids were beautiful a great place for people who love history. Beautiful but there was trash everywhere on the sand. Attention Tourists if you are going to visit the Pyramids of Giza be prepared for sellers. If the sellers find out you are an american then they are going to be all over you begging you to pay something from them. Overall the Pyramids and Egypt was Beautiful.

We visited during March 2014, and there seem to have been some maintenance done, we had no clue where are the Ram road and we walked a lot to reach the main temple area. Some of the areas were closed, and there was no places to take a rest. I believe the whole area needs to be improved in order to make a good sight of it!best to visit during Nov, Dec, Jan.

If you climb the pyramid independently next to the path, you get a little extra at your visit.Guards (soldiers) will show up their Ak47.For the rest total chaos.Too bad the people of Egypt don't understand tourism at all.If you are in the area definitely visit but good luck wished

We were told that this mosque is similar or a replica of the Blue Mosque in Istanbul, but that one is much nicer.  This one wasn't lit well at all, so you couldn't see any of the ceiling detail.  I've been to a lot of mosques and this one didn't do anything for me.  Glad I saw it, but we liked the Sultan Hassan mosque much better.
Sadly the Al Azhar university is no longer here so you can't take a peek at what is one of the largest and most respected places of Islamic scholarship

We wwe really excited to go here and see all of the things inside the museum with explanations of what everything was.  We were somewhat disappointed to find that not everything we saw in the museum was described with what it was.  It's a very large place with lots to see and after touring the ground floor, we found ourselved getting a little bored with not being able to know what everything was or where it had been found etc.We decided to go straight to the only thing that we were most excited about seeing and photographing - King Tut's mask.  We found the small side room where his mask was located alongside his beautiful coffin and before we even entered there was a sign stating 'No Photographs'.  This was the only thing we wanted to get a photograph of.  We knew we weren't going to get a picture when we draw a security guard standing right next to the mask in a glass case.  After looking we decided to leave as this really disheartened us.  You can photograph everything in the museum except the items in King Tut's room as long as the flash is off so why not allow this rule here too?

the Pyramids as nice to look at the say yes iv done it but be warned you will get pestered to death by the locals to buy this or ride their camels. they do not take no for an answer. if you a women and very pale skinned it will be twice as bad.

We saw it from far away from the Saqqara pyramids. It has the bent pyramid and red pyramid among others. However, it is quite far away and will take some serious energy just to go all the way there.plus point is that there are hardly any tourists as it is out of the way and not recommended by many.

The museum is extremely crowded with people and artifacts.   There are so many things to see, but few labels to help know what one is viewing.  Also, no A/C.  We visited on a very hot day in March.  The new museum at Giza should be a wonderful thing when it is completed.
If you like to shop it is a great place to go, but for me it was just a little underwhelming as I am not a shopper.

if you are looking for an experience with competence and expectation of knowledge of a different cultural reality, forget it.The operation of the program is efficient in terms of times and places in the defined time periods. However the program is constantly adjusted with general conversations and general historical knowledge and photo tourist.Do not think that Egyptologists are really experts. I would even say that they are a group of natives with some information that, poured in a concentrated way, even makes us believe that we are acquiring knowledge.It was a pity that a journey so far to such disparate realities was not more gratifying.if you are looking for an experience with competence and expectation of knowledge of a different cultural reality, forget it.The operation of the program is efficient in terms of times and places in the defined time periods. However the program is constantly adjusted with general conversations and general historical knowledge and photo tourist.Do not think that Egyptologists are really experts. I would even say that they are a group of natives with some information that, poured in a concentrated way, even makes us believe that we are acquiring knowledge.It was a pity that a journey so far to such disparate realities was not more gratifying.Never forget that the objective of the guides is gratification, always present in every moment.

This is a landmark with which we were able to get a sense of our location.  Central Cairo, this tower is easy to spot.  From the observation deck there are great views of the city and you can even see the pyramids of Giza in the distance.  There is often a heavy haze over the city so viewing great distances may be a challenge.   For Egypt, the entrance fees were a little high, but you'll probably only be there once in you life.  They security guys at the top are helpful in pointing out different landmarks.  A few pounds helps this along.
Not as impressive as the Giza pyramids so probably better to see them before

This church is very close to the Coptic Museum and I thought it was the much more interesting of the two. Not huge but it takes a while to look at all the artifacts, especially the relics of dozens of saints. These are all wrapped up and sealed in cases, so I’m not sure exactly what they consist of but it’s worth a good look round nevertheless.

For me to see it from outside will be enough as the façade is good but inside is nothing really special because those were not figuratively developed interiors & ornament rich architectural era without having 3rd dimension of perspective.Try not to go during the summer. If you are there in summer than try not going at mid day.Need to see it of course but if you are in big hurry than can be passed.

I've been to the tower on many occasions.The views are very nice ... especially during day time.You may enjoy a lovely lunch or a snack while enjoying the views.The food comes in a limited menu though.It's something to do while in cairo

I hired a tour guide to take me to the Museum. I heard that a lot of the materials had been moved to the new museum. My impression was the museum was old and dusty. The first floor materials appear exactly the same as the pictures on the Internet. I found the building to be hot and no A/C on that I could detect.One good thing is that there is a handicap elevator that can take you to the 2nd floor.The reason to go is the King Tut items on the 2nd floor. Stunning. Worth the 200LE to get in.The guide Titto was great and I was there for about 2 hours. However, just as a museum as a whole, it is not in great shape and hope the the new Giza museum will be a lot better. The British Museum in London is in much better shape and has some similar items. Been there 3 times.So I rate the experience as average.
It is a great place for photos, but unfortunately garbage and  beggars are all around the place. Be careful, Egypt is a place where tourists harassment is very common.

I have waited 30 years to come here and unfortunately the experience was less than pleasant.We were hassled relentlessly from time of arrival, much worse than anywhere else in Egypt. I was even called "stupid tourist" by one of the locals trying to get us on his horses.Also, the whipping of the camels and horses really put me off. The poor beasts are struggling and then they get belted to move faster!There are people flashing badges at you saying they work for the government insisting that you not walk and you must take a camel or horse because to get to the last pyramid you are going "outside". You're not. We walked all the way, and it really isn't that far , and didn't go outside the grounds at all.Anyway, climbed into the King's chamber of the great pyramid and took a seat on the floor to rest for a moment only to be yelled at with "No sitting on floor".Inside the small pyramid I was hassled by one of the guards for money. Pretty confronting in a small space, but I just asked "What for?" and walked off.One of the best pieces of advice I received from a local and didn't follow because I didn't want to be rude was -just ignore. Do not say anything, keep walking, don't make eye contact. Once you speak, they know you understand and it's the end of your peace and quiet. Remember, there is a shortage of tourists and they will latch onto you.Would I recommend missing these - no. But don't expect to stand there and take in the moment for too long. It won't happen.I was going to give Saqqara a miss, but I'm glad I didn't. That ended up being a huge highlight.

This was interesting to see but they didn't allow any photos so everyone on our tour bought the postcards (which I guess was the whole idea of not allowing photos)  Also there was a guide down there that tried to make you give him money to show you an empty side room.

Hi guys!So we set off first (at about 08:30hrs) to Sakkara and Memphis which I will elaborate on in another review.At about...14:00hrs we arrived at the pyramids (my guide and I) and I think I`m one of the few to say I was... less than ecstatic. Unlike other visitors, I wasn`t  harassed by peddlers and the KFC doesn`t mar the facade at all but it just wasn`t WOW!!!I guess, somehow, I expected more. Nevertheless, I did feel something akin to awe when I was there `cause let`s face it; it`s a pretty impressive monument especially considering it was built roughly 4,300 years ago. The Sphinx, in my opinion, was impressive mainly `cause of its size. Please note the wonder of the ancient world is just the Great pyramid (not plural) of Cheops/ Khufu which of course, is the largestHelpful tips (I hope);a) If you are just visiting the pyramids and the sphinx you don`t necessarily have to wear sensible shoes cause there are paved roads you can walk on (except of course you walking to all the main pyramids)b) There are about six (6) pyramids at Giza and are all worth seeing. The smaller ones belonged to women; Cheops mother, wife and I think sister and you can go in free of charge.c) To enter the great pyramid (Cheops) you need to pay an extra 200 Egyptian pounds.d) Dress as you usually do since it`s not a religious site but I went in chilly January so dealt with far fewer people and of course people are still afraid of Egypt.e) I don`t think they sell refreshments on the grounds, but you can get something one you step out the gate.f) I watched the Sound and light evening show from my guest house free of charge which  I will talk about in another review.You guessed it, pictures!!!!

We went by sea and it was disappointing, as we never went ashore or got close to the shore, nice snorkelling but we didn't see anything of the park... Neverland is a shoal in the middle of the sea , with a terrible wind and an exaggerated cold, still beautiful but it's dry😂

Somehow the anticipated magic of being close to our Lord at this stage of his very early life, was  missing! we had just completed a pilgrimage in Palestine and this was supposed to add a puzzle piece. ther was no real FEELING of that though .Clearly a precious church in the COPTIC Christian world but not greatly inspiring for us......perhaps we were a little weary.

Not my favorite - it’s grand and impressive but so much work is new . I realize that because it’s the queens temple it has a special vibe - it just never has been one of my favesI do like the Hathor temple on the left sideBut more than likely you will visit and gone to your own conclusions

We thought we'd visit the famous pyramids. It cost us £20 each which is 400j each local currency. It's amazing seeing the pyramids up close but the locals who work there offering horse rides and camel rides just ruin the experience with their persistent huggeling they just don't understand no, regardless of how nice you say it or how many times you say it - in Arabic or English.Over I don't think I'd visit again or recommend to friends or family.
We executed a quick photo stop at the Colossi of Memnon. Too bad that these two giants are in such a sad state of repair.

After visiting the Luxor Temple, I saw the signage about this avenue. It was about 12:30 p.m when I was here and the heat was so intense. I quickly took some pictures of the sphinxes and hurriedly looked for shaded areas where I  could cool off.

Evening view is nice. Where the lighting is an added beauty. The combination of architectures of 1200 BC and 350 BC is an excellence. Few of the coloured wall paintings are mostly intact and show the perfection of  the art and non chemical colour sustainability.
Wow! For the amount of statues and artifacts, but it's a cluttered, unorganized jumble with little explanation

Lots of small and large exhibits from antiquity. Unfortunately I have to admit that they are not shown in an interesting way. What's a pity coz they're so worthy and show ancient times. Anyway you should visit the museum, the entrance fee is adequate and you can see many interesting things.

If all you want to do is see quite a few Niles crocodile, this is the activity for you.The boat ride to the Nubian Village is fun and takes about an hour.Nothing's learned at the village. Just the same tourist stuff sold here.
The pyramids are amazing, but the super aggressive hawkers they allow make it hard to enjoy them.  They force you to be rude.
Not worth so much of travel from Aswan where you need to leave in the convoy at 4 am. Can be given a miss if you have already seen Luxor.

They are building a new Mega-Museum in Giza so this historic building and it's collection are low on maintenance but still high in content especially the Tut artifacts. You'll need a good guide because the labeling is poor to non-existent. Give yourself half a day and get there early ... the crowds are huge.

Worth a visit to see the death mask of King Tut and his fabulous Dagger made out of Meteorite together with other burial treasures. Having said that, the museum is a tired old lady. Holes in the roof through which rained gushed and crates full of goods packed for transition to the modern new museum which is in final throes of construction. The time is right to hand over the baton to a new venue and a more modern educational experience.

To visit Egypt and not to visti pyramids in Giza is a sin! But, like the most, what I really did not want to see are "sellers"! I understand that revolution back in 2011. hit them hard in a sense of tourism backing down, but their approach is not good. I was there with my better half and as we were walking someone grab her arm from behind! I am cool, relaxed guy but I almost lost it! I told him to back of, and he did! As you assume, he was a "seller" wanted to sell her a scarf...This one is important... !!!!!!Around pyramids there are numerous camels, donkyes and horses you can ride. Ofc they have their owners. What I want you to notice is this... We were around pyramids and one of owners came and asked me to take a picture of me and my gf(with our camera). Right then I knew it will be "superb" situation. I said no, but he was annoying so I say ok. He took some pics and asked us to ride his camel, i said NO once again. And the came the "stupid tourist moment" my gf made a puppy face (you know pyramids, camel, love and stuff:)) so we took a ride... Everything  was ok, but suddenly he directed his camel to the other side of the pyramid( no one was there, just us) and then the funny part started. I climbed down from the camel to pay him. He demanded 100$ that's right 100 american dollars for 10minutes on the camel, I said him to piss off , then he wanted 100 egyptian pounds, I told him NO. And then, one more came, team mate of his and started asking me money.. Oh I almost forgotten, my gf was still on the camel and they did not wanted to climb her down. At that moment, I showed him that I do not accept his terms and that I will fight if I need to. I gave him 30 egyptian pounds and 2$ usd, got my gf and left.Do not ride camels around pyramids, if you "have to" do not get apart from your group. They know their "job" and they will try to ripp you off... See pyramids, take a picture,and leave!

It’s really a historic place that reflects one of the barely recent Egyptian eras, but it lacks being well presented because it’s operated by non professionals.no proper guidance is available, really nothing at all from operators side vs a very important place.

Had to go and have a look. Beautiful architecture and very serene place. You are only allowed in one corner but still it gives you an idea of what goes on. Best time to go is early afternoon but check prayer times
Must seen templeHugeHistoricalNot recommended for old people & childrenNeed a lot of maintenanceNeed more guidanceNeed much development

The Citidel has a LE50 entry fee that gives you access to the 19th century Mosque of Muhammad Ali, the 14th century Nassir Muhammad, a police museum and a military museum. Most tourists I'd imagine wouldn't bother with either of the museums but they did seem quite popular with the young locals.Visitors of Turkey will know what to expect to see within the classic Ottoman style Muhammad Ali mosque. While the Nassir Muhammad was of more historical interest it alone isn't worth the Citidel's entry fee.Just a word of warning to bring enough money before you arrive. There are 2 old ATM's before the ticket office but they were both turned off and I assumed indefinitely out of order. There are no banks or shops nearby while food and drinks within are priced at tourist rates such as LE10 for a can of soft drink.While the Citidel with its elevated views over Cairo does make a great escape from the hectic, polluted streets down below and does offer some great photo opportunities. You maybe better off chilling out at the nearby Al Azhar Park instead. Where there is more shade, lush gardens and a much cheaper entry fee.

We booked a trip with a local travel agency because they promised us to see wonderful coral reefs. Unfortunately, we didn't see anything nice. We paid £30 per person for a day boat trip but we were very disappointed. I think that along the beach you can find beautiful reefs and you don't need to pay any money.
We were not allowed to go inside because they were having prayer. Why don't they make a separate room for prayer?

The museum is old school more like a warehouse of fabulous artifacts.  We had a guide so we could move through and find the gems. The new museum will be open soon and, hopefully, be up to modern standards.  That said, until it is, this is a must in Cairo
Beautiful temple, shame about the poor horses pulling carriages outside being whipped to an inch if their lives.

The good news is the pyramids are still standing, and the security fears about Egypt peddled by the international media seem vastly vastly overblown.  The bad news is there is just a handful of foreign tourists visiting the pyramids these days where previously there were busloads (according to our guide)  but the population of souvenir peddlers and camel ride peddlers is just as big, so you have 20 people selling souvenirs attacking  each potential buyer and they won't take no for an answer. There are no security around btw.  So in between dodging souvenir peddlers I managed to sneak a peak at the pyramids.  Frankly I expected to be more impressed than I actually was. Maybe coming from the desert with the pyramids looming in the distance it would be an imposing sight but coming from the city as we were with its tall buildings they looked less majestic. Still, the pyramids should be on everyone's bucket list so go.

One thing you will find in every nook and corner of Cairo is Ramses. This one is the largest lying on its back. The statues feet are missing on on one of its thighs his wife's picture is carved. The body has multiple names in hieroglyphics carved. The forehead has a snake. It just takes about 10 minutes to go up and see around..

When I first went Luxor 20 years ago a lot less Sphinx had been discovered.  They are brilliant view at night. Unfortunately some of the more recently found Sphinx have been used as a rubbish dump. Such a crime.

The lightshow and commentary may be cheesy, but to see the various parts of the temple lit at night was beautiful.  It may be worthwhile to brush up on your history before visiting the temple for the light and sound show because you don't get much in the way of actual history from the commentary.... rather a lyrical interpretation with cheesy voices.Sadly the temple is becoming a bit run down, and not really well looked after considering its historical importance.Also, prepare to run the gauntlet when exiting the temple with very pushy vendors selling trinkets and souvenirs.

The old museum is fast becoming a falling down ruin. The plaster is falling of the walls, the glass ceiling is falling in, there are constantly workers in the building making repairs but just seem to be making a bigger mess. Tradesmen also working over your head making ceiling repairs without any protection. Despite the amount of artefacts to be seen, a lot of the important items have and are being removed to the new Grand Egyptian Museum for restoration which is leaving many random empty sections in the museum. No effort is made to provide temporary alternative displays or even images or an explanation of the missing items.

This was one of our yearly visit to Citadel of Saladin. Actual name is Salah Eldin Alayobi as pronounced in Arabic. The citadel is one of the landmarks in Cairo. It is nice to visit. Only problem is timing of the visit. In winter time is joy to visit as weather is great. During summer, the visit can be a little pain due to the heat and the burining sun. Be prepared to give some tips as you go. It is more interesting to visit the place with a tour guide. You will learn a lot about the history of the place and the background of the role this place played in Egypt’s history. The Military Muslim is another attraction you can visit during your visit to the citadel. Few hours should be sufficient to cover both places.

This museum needs too much care of eygption people..It was too hot there were no air condition and the temperature was 37+ c.The need to clean it cause it´s theirs ancient.Any manager  of this museum can visit the eygption department in De louvre in Paris and see who the foreign take care about it.

Not a very interesting site. A large mosque, similar to the Blue Mosque in Istanbul. Cairo's traffic means that to my mind it is not worth the effort, unless one has not seen a mosque before.Obviously very well cared for, with beautiful lights

Surprising that this world renowned and richest museum in the world appears to be in a state of disrepair inside but especially outside as well. Dirty glass cases protecting priceless antiquities. Hodgepodge of staff in street clothes so you never know who is an employee. Unsurpassed collections throughout such as King Tut's golden beds, gold face mask, and spectacular Alabaster works of art.

We had a guide who was amazing so really benefited from his knowledge about the pieces which were truly spectacular.What lets the museum down, is that the pieces are poorly presented, dusty. The place really needs a good clean. The Citadel of Qaitbay (or the Fort of Qaitbay) is a must see when travelling in Alexandria. This 15th-century defensive fortress is located on the Mediterranean Sea and was established in 1477 AD by Sultan Al-Ashraf Sayf al-Din Qa'it Bay. It became one of the most important defensive strongholds along the Sea coast.The Citadel is easy to access and I can see why it attracts many tourists as well as locals. The Citadel was erected on the exact same site of the famous Lighthouse which was one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World. Parts of the Lighthouse were used in the construction of the Citadel.You can venture inside the Citadel but there isn’t much to see. It’s empty of treasure but next to the entrance there is a museum which contains amazing items such as the fossilised creatures including the remarkable skeleton of a while. There is also an aquarium nearby but not much to see there.The Egypt Yacht Club is also only a few hundred metres from the Citadel but there were few yachts around.As I visited in January, the weather is overcast, which you can see in my photos.TRANSPORT: Travel by train can take anywhere between 4 hours and 40 minutes on an ordinary train to 3 hours and 10 minutes on a special express. For more information on train timetables, go to the seat61 dot com which details departure times, costs and so on. Once you arrive in Alexandria you can travel by taxi to the harbor which every taxi driver knows.  You can also catch at Yellow Tram #25. Here you will see the original site of The Lighthouse where the Citadel is located.PARKING: If travelling by car, parking is available, Egyptian style. In other words, park wherever you can. There are plenty of taxis you can use to get to and from the site.DISABLED ACCESS: Good disabled access from along the harbour. Just watch the horse too from the carriages.ATM: There are ATM’s nearby and within a few minutes walking distance.OPENING HOURS: Open daily from 9 am to 6 pm.ENTRANCE FEE: Adult 25 L.E. ($5 AUST) or 15 L.E. Student ($3 AUST)TOILET FACILITIES: I hadn’t kept my eye out for toilets but there are many restaurants nearby that you could utilise.FOOD: There are a few vendors selling food however, I would recommend Atheneos Restaurant on the Promenade. For more information you can find more information here.This is a double whammy place to visit – the site of the original Lighthouse and the Citadel – two for the price of one (although there is a fee to go inside the Citadel). Don't forget to look back to the city of Alexandria - it's beautiful.

However this is not a 5 star hotel its more of a 3 star, and from what we have gathered its down as a 3 star rating for the next season.The hotel is very clean and the grounds are well looked after, the rooms are clean and have plenty of space the air con is good but they could do with a refurbishment in the bathroom area but they are clean. The food at the hotel was of an acceptable standard but it is geared more towards the Italian guests, however we did have chips (French Fries) twice in one week. Yes a few people did have the upset tummy but if you eat wisely then you won't have any problems.The all inclusive works ok the local beer is good and the spirts are ok too. The biggest down fall is the lack of entertainment in the evenings mainly for the children, as its all in Italian and run by the the Italian reps.The beach is ok but nothing to go on about, however the diving at the end of the jetty is a must the fish are brilliant its like swimming with Nemo and friends.The Nubian Island next door is very much newer and has better facilities you can use them if you ask, like their gym is more equipt and better layed out.Over all we had a good time the bottom line is you get what you pay for one week in Eygpt with brilliant weather for £800 all inclusive. I had already been to The Louvre in Paris and the British Museum in London, and so naturally I found my way to the Egyptian Museum in Cairo (also known as the Museum of Egyptian Antiquities or the Museum of Cairo).It is of course a place that history buffs and first-time visitors to Cairo must make time for. It would be wise to hire an official museum guide. Alternatively, do extensive research before you visit, and allow yourself more time to find your way around. In fact, do your research even if you get a guide, as it would only enrich your museum experience.There are many wonderful statues and artifacts of different sizes and shapes around the museum. The challenge is navigating around the maze of haphazardly organised displays. There is also a dearth of signage and texts on displays are not always easy to read.For a worthwhile and unhurried experience, set aside at least three hours or half a day for this. But do note that the museum closes at 5pm on most days (it is open till 9pm on Thursdays and Sundays).As there is no cafe within the museum, you will also have to work around that with either a meal before or after your visit.  It is also a pity that we found the cafes and restaurants just outside the museum all vacant - either due to poor business or in anticipation of the move to the new museum in Giza, the Grand Egyptian Museum (GEM), otherwise known as the Giza Museum.At this stage of its life, the Egyptian Antiquities Museum is old with neglect, sadly resembling a cluttered storehouse despite its fascinating artifacts.  However, in contrast, the write-ups on the GEM seem promising, with the inclusion of a children’s museum and workshops as well as the employment of technology such as virtual reality.  I look forward to visiting the GEM in Giza as it aims to set itself apart from its impressive counterparts, the British Museum and The Louvre!

The tomb of king Tutankhamun is one of the smaller tombs in the Valley of the Kings. There really is not that much to see, but for many visitors to the valley, the tomb is a must-see. It was here, after all, that the young king was buried around 1323 BC and not found again until 1922 after more than 3000 years.Tutankhamun was not famous in his own time, so it is kind of ironical that he has become the most famous Pharaoh in our time. He was one of the last kings in the 18.th dynasty (circa 1539-1292 BC), to which he belong. Tutankhamun got the throne in Egypt after a turbulent period, where all the gods had been abandoned in favour of the sun-disc, the Aten. He was a boy of 8-10 years when he was crowned, and he died after not more than 10 years on the throne. Throughout most of his reign, the real power was in the hands of the courtier Ay and the generalissimo Horemheb, both of whom was to become Pharaohs after Tutankhamun's death.(Ay's tomb is to be found in the West Valley and Horemheb's tomb, which is breathtakingly beautiful in blue colors, has been closed for years)Tutankhamun's tomb consists of four chambers, none of which are very large. The young king's sarcophagus and his mummy is still in the tomb in the burial-chamber. The burial-chamber is the only chamber in this tomb which is decorated. On a fine yellow background you can see several scenes. On the back wall to the right is Ay, clad in leopard-skin, performing the "opening of the mouth ritual" on the mummy of Tutankhamun. This ritual was done to symbolical give the deceased their senses back, so that they would be able to function in the Netherworld. Also, the scene is important, because as per Ancient Egyptian tradition, the one performing the opening of the mouth ritual was the one who would inherit the throne. Usually it was the deceased king's son who did it, so in this scene Ay is actually acting like he was the king's son, although Ay was maybe 45-50 years Tutankhamun's senior!Left side on the back wall shows Osiris, god of the Netherworld, welcoming Tutankhamun and his ka ("soul") to the Netherworld.On the sidewall to the left side, there is a row of baboons, 12 in total. They symbolize the 12 hours of the night, which the deceased king must travel through in order to be reborn in the Netherworld. On the opposite wall, you can see the funeral procession of the king. His coffins, with the mummy in the innermost one, is being pulled on a sled by a row of courtiers.It is hard to believe that the incredible treasures and gravegoods on display at the Egyptian Museum in Cairo were actually crammed into the chambers of this tomb. So if you want to see where all those things were placed, you need to go to Tutankhmun's tomb. Otherwise, it can be a little disappointing if the visitor expects a grand tomb, just as grand as his gravegoods. This tomb is not that grand at all.You buy your ticket to the tomb of Tutankhamun before you enter the Valley. At the time of writing (2008) the price is around 100 Egyptian Pounds, rumors have it that the tomb is going to be closed completely later this year, but until now it is only closed between noon and 2PM.

Sakara is in the South of Egypt. A guide showed the way and really was worth the money. Besides that nothing very spectacular and you definitely could do without seeing these pyramids. Nevertheless it is lee touristic and a trip inside the tombs is interesting but not thrinning. I was a little bit socked how the Egyptisans deal with their world heritage.

Though the monuments are kept well and guides take you along to explain the mummification processes and the history and myth behind it all , the authorities do need to pay heed to providing clean washrooms especially as they charge for the use .  This is an issue across Egypt unfortunately!

The tomb of Ramesses VI lies in KV9 at Valley of the Kings at Luxor, once meant for his predecessor Ramesses V.The latter's administration was corrupt and he never paid his people well, hence his workers did not complete his tomb and instead it was completed for his uncle Ramesses VI. The decorations are theological, narrating the story of the origins of the heavens, earth, the creation of the sun, light and life.It has three chambers and a 4th pillared chamber was added by him depicting scenes of the sky goddess, Nut. There are numerous graffiti made by Romans during their invasion, and at that time, it was known as the tomb of Memnon.

It is free to go inside the mosque, as when entering the Citadel the admission of 100 LE gives you access to the mosque.  The mosque is interesting, with the high dome ceiling, however the floor is quite dirty, I am surprised why it cannot be kept cleaner.  The mosque often referred to the Alabaster Mosque due to its extensive use of that stone on the exterior walls and other surfaces.  An interesting note, I was approached several times by children trying to take a selfie with me or photo / video of me, I had attained instant celebrity status.

This temple is large but there is not a great deal to look at really and the restoration from its original state was not done as it should have been.  It's worth a visit if you are already in the area of the valley of the kings as its quite close by but I would not make a special trip
